- A **bubble trap / air detector** and **arterial pressure monitor (typically -50 to -200 mmHg)** safeguard the circuit.

### Stage 2: Mass Exchange in the Dialyzer
- Blood enters the **dialyzer**, which contains thousands of hollow polymer fibers.
- Inside the fibers (lumen), blood flows in one direction; outside (in the dialysate compartment), dialysate flows in the **opposite direction (counter-current flow)**.
- **Counter-current flow** maintains the maximum possible concentration gradient along the entire length of the fiber, maximizing clearance efficiency.

### Stage 3: Blood Return & Ultrafiltration (Venous Limb)
- Treated blood returns to the patient via the **venous limb**.
- A **venous pressure monitor (+50 to +250 mmHg)** ensures no disconnection.
- An **air-in-line detector** prevents air embolism.
- The **ultrafiltration pump** removes excess water according to the prescribed fluid removal rate.

## 2.2 The Counter-Current Flow Advantage

> [!NOTE]
> In co-current flow, the concentration gradient diminishes along the length, limiting clearance. In counter-current flow, the gradient is **maintained at every cross-section** of the dialyzer, yielding approximately **10–20% higher clearance** for the same flow rates.

## 2.3 Mass Transfer Mechanism — Fick's Law of Diffusion

Transport of uremic toxins across the dialyzer membrane is governed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**:

$$J = -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

Where:
- $J$ = molar flux of solute (mol/s) — the rate of solute movement across the membrane
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of solute in membrane (m²/s) — higher for smaller molecules
- $A$ = effective membrane surface area (m²)
- $\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ = concentration gradient of solute across membrane (mol/m⁴)

## 2.4 Clearance Equation (KTU High-Yield)

**Clearance (K)** is defined as the volume of blood completely cleared of a given solute per unit time:

$$K = \frac{(C_{in} - C_{out})}{C_{in}} \times Q_B$$

Where:
- $C_{in}$ = solute concentration in blood entering the dialyzer
- $C_{out}$ = solute concentration in blood leaving the dialyzer
- $Q_B$ = blood flow rate (mL/min)

## 2.5 Dialysance Equation (Counter-Current Configuration)

$$D_{ial} = \frac{Q_B (C_{Bi} - C_{Bo})}{C_{Bi} - C_{Di}}$$

Where $C_{Di}$ is the dialysate inlet concentration. When $C_{Di} = 0$ (no solute in fresh dialysate), **Dialysance = Clearance**.

## 2.6 Kt/V — The Gold Standard of Dialysis Adequacy

$$Kt/V = -\ln(R - 0.008 \cdot t) + (4 - 3.5R) \cdot \frac{UF}{W}$$

Where:
- $K$ = urea clearance (mL/min)
- $t$ = dialysis session duration (minutes)
- $V$ = urea distribution volume ≈ total body water (mL)
- $R$ = post/pre dialysis BUN ratio
- $UF$ = ultrafiltrate volume removed (L)
- $W$ = post-dialysis body weight (kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Clinical Target**: A single-pool $Kt/V \geq 1.2$ is considered adequate dialysis per KDOQI guidelines (referenced in KTU Module 2 syllabus). For thrice-weekly hemodialysis, **target Kt/V = 1.4**.

## 2.7 Ultrafiltration Rate

$$UF_{rate} = \frac{V_{removed}}{t_{session}}$$

Typical $UF_{rate}$ should not exceed **13 mL/kg/hr** to prevent **intradialytic hypotension**.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Standard Value / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Mass Flux (Fick's Law) | $J = -D \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | mol/s |
| Solute Clearance | $K = \dfrac{(C_{in} - C_{out})}{C_{in}} \cdot Q_B$ | mL/min |
| Dialysance | $D_{ial} = \dfrac{Q_B (C_{Bi} - C_{Bo})}{C_{Bi} - C_{Di}}$ | mL/min |
| Dialysis Adequacy (Kt/V) | $Kt/V = -\ln(R - 0.008t) + (4-3.5R) \cdot \dfrac{UF}{W}$ | Target $\geq 1.2$ |
| Urea Reduction Ratio (URR) | $URR = 1 - R$ | Target $\geq 65\%$ |
| Transmembrane Pressure | $TMP = \dfrac{P_{Bi} + P_{Bo}}{2} - \dfrac{P_{Di} + P_{Do}}{2}$ | mmHg |
| Ultrafiltration Rate | $UF_{rate} = \dfrac{V_{removed}}{t_{session}}$ | mL/hr |
| Henderson-Hasselbalch | $pH = 6.1 + \log\dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{0.03 \cdot pCO_2}$ | Used for acetate/bicarbonate |
| Sieving Coefficient | $S = \dfrac{C_{f}}{C_{p}}$ | 0 (retained) to 1 (freely filtered) |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The hemodialysis machine integrates principles from **fluid mechanics (peristaltic pumps, Hagen-Poiseuille flow)**, **mass transfer (Fick's diffusion)**, **biomedical instrumentation (pressure transducers, conductivity cells)**, and **control systems (PID loops for temperature and ultrafiltration control)**. This makes it a canonical example of **multidisciplinary biomedical engineering** — a key emphasis area in KTU 2024 PCBMT502.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fick's Law Applied to a Hollow-Fiber Dialyzer

### Step 1: Define the governing mass-transfer equation

The rate of solute removal across the membrane per unit length of fiber is:

$$\frac{dN}{dz} = K_0 \cdot A_{eff}(z) \cdot (C_B(z) - C_D(z))$$

**Explanation:** The local molar transfer rate $dN/dz$ (mol/m·s) is proportional to the local effective area $A_{eff}(z)$ and the local concentration difference between blood and dialysate. $K_0$ is the overall mass transfer coefficient.

### Step 2: Apply the local mass balance on the blood side

$$Q_B \cdot \frac{dC_B}{dz} = -K_0 \cdot a \cdot (C_B - C_D)$$

**Explanation:** As solute leaves the blood, its concentration decreases along the fiber length $z$. The term $a$ is the area per unit length (membrane surface per meter of fiber). This is a first-order linear ODE.

### Step 3: Apply the local mass balance on the dialysate side (counter-current)

$$Q_D \cdot \frac{dC_D}{dz} = K_0 \cdot a \cdot (C_B - C_D)$$

**Explanation:** The sign is positive because solute enters the dialysate. Note the sign convention for counter-current flow makes $z$ decrease in the dialysate stream.

### Step 4: Combine the two equations by elimination

Differentiate the blood-side equation with respect to $z$ and substitute:

$$\frac{d^2 C_B}{dz^2} = \frac{K_0 a}{Q_B} \cdot \left(\frac{dC_B}{dz} - \frac{dC_D}{dz}\right)$$

Using the dialysate equation, $\dfrac{dC_D}{dz} = \dfrac{K_0 a}{Q_D}(C_B - C_D)$, substitution yields:

$$\frac{d^2 C_B}{dz^2} = \frac{K_0 a}{Q_B} \cdot \left(\frac{dC_B}{dz} - \frac{K_0 a}{Q_D}(C_B - C_D)\right)$$

### Step 5: Solve with boundary conditions

- At $z = 0$ (blood inlet): $C_B(0) = C_{Bi}$
- At $z = L$ (dialysate inlet): $C_D(L) = C_{Di}$ (typically $\approx 0$)

The closed-form clearance for a counter-current dialyzer is:

$$K = Q_B \cdot \frac{1 - e^{K_0 a L (1/Q_B - 1/Q_D)}}{1 - \frac{Q_B}{Q_D} e^{K_0 a L (1/Q_B - 1/Q_D)}}$$

**Explanation:** This is the famous **"Michael Equation"** for counter-current mass exchange. It shows clearance asymptotically approaches $Q_B$ as $K_0 a L \to \infty$, but never exceeds the blood flow rate.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Urea Clearance Calculation

**Given:**
- $Q_B = 300$ mL/min
- $Q_D = 500$ mL/min
- $C_{Bi} = 1.4$ mg/mL (urea in incoming blood)
- $C_{Bo} = 0.4$ mg/mL (urea in outgoing blood)
- $C_{Di} = 0$ mg/mL (fresh dialysate)

**Step 1: Compute clearance directly**

$$K = \frac{(C_{Bi} - C_{Bo})}{C_{Bi}} \cdot Q_B = \frac{(1.4 - 0.4)}{1.4} \cdot 300$$

$$K = \frac{1.0}{1.4} \cdot 300 = 0.7143 \cdot 300$$

$$K = 214.3 \text{ mL/min}$$

**Step 2: Compute Urea Reduction Ratio (URR)**

$$URR = 1 - R = 1 - \frac{C_{Bo}}{C_{Bi}} = 1 - \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.714 = 71.4\%$$

**Step 3: Compute Kt/V (assuming 70 kg patient, 4-hour session)**

Total body water $V \approx 0.6 \times 70 = 42$ L = 42,000 mL.

$$Kt/V = \frac{K \cdot t}{V} = \frac{214.3 \cdot 240}{42000} = \frac{51432}{42000} = 1.225$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

$$Kt/V = 1.225 \geq 1.2 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ADEQUATE dialysis delivered (KDOQI compliant)}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Failure Modes & Engineering Safeguards

| **Failure Mode** | **Engineering Consequence** | **KTU-Mandated Safety Monitor** |
|---|---|---|
| Air bubble in venous line | Air embolism → stroke/death | Air-in-line detector (ultrasonic) |
| Blood leak into dialysate | Membrane rupture, infection | Blood-leak detector (photocell) |
| Excessive UF | Hypotension, shock | UF volume controller + weighing |
| Temperature > 38°C | Hemolysis | Thermistor at dialyzer outlet |
| Conductivity drift | Wrong electrolyte balance | Conductivity cell (4-electrode) |
| pH deviation | Acidosis / alkalosis | pH sensor in dialysate circuit |
| Venous needle dislodgement | Fatal blood loss | Venous pressure monitor |
| Low arterial pressure | Suction on fistula | Arterial pressure monitor |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hemodialysis Machine — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete functional architecture of a modern hemodialysis machine as per KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus. The diagram distinguishes the **blood circuit (extracorporeal)**, the **dialysate circuit**, and the **safety/monitoring subsystem**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Vascular Access AV Fistula] --> B[Arterial Pressure Monitor]
    B --> C[Heparin Infusion Pump]
    C --> D[Arterial Bubble Trap]
    D --> E[Blood Roller Pump Q_B 200-500 mL/min]
    E --> F[Dialyzer Hollow Fiber Membrane]
    F --> G[Venous Bubble Trap]
    G --> H[Venous Pressure Monitor]
    H --> I[Air in Line Detector]
    I --> J[Return Blood to Patient]
    F <--> K[Ultrafiltration Pump]
    K --> L[UF Volume Sensor Weighing Scale]
    F --> M[Dialysate Inlet]
    M --> N[Proportioning Pump A B Concentrate]
    N --> O[Heater 37 deg C]
    O --> P[Degasser]
    P --> F
    F --> Q[Dialysate Outlet]
    Q --> R[Conductivity Sensor 13.5-15.0 mS/cm]
    R --> S[Temperature Sensor 36-38 deg C]
    S --> T[pH Sensor]
    T --> U[Blood Leak Detector Photocell]
    U --> V[Drainage to Effluent]
    L --> W[Central Control Unit Microprocessor]
    R --> W
    S --> W
    H --> W
    B --> W
    I --> W
    U --> W
    W --> X[Operator Touchscreen Display]
    W --> Y[Alarm Buzzer and Visual Indicators]
    W --> Z[Battery Backup UPS for Pump Failure]
```

## 4.2 Counter-Current Flow Schematic (Hollow Fiber Dialyzer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Blood Inlet Q_B] --> B[Fiber Lumen Direction Right to Left]
    B --> C[Semipermeable Membrane Wall 180-220 micron ID]
    C --> D[Dialysate Flow Direction Left to Right Q_D]
    D --> E[Blood Outlet Purified]
    F[Fresh Dialysate Inlet] --> D
    D --> G[Spent Dialysate Outlet with Toxins]
    C -.Diffusion of Urea 60 Da.-> D
    C -.Retains Albumin 66000 Da.-> B
```

## 4.3 KTU-Required Component Classification (3-Tier Architecture)

| **Tier** | **Subsystem** | **KTU-Mapped Components** |
|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1: Extracorporeal Blood Circuit** | Pumps, monitors, traps | Blood roller pump, heparin pump, arterial/venous pressure monitors, bubble traps |
| **Tier 2: Mass Transfer Unit** | Dialyzer | Hollow fiber dialyzer, membrane material (polysulfone, polyethersulfone, cellulose triacetate) |
| **Tier 3: Dialysate Delivery System (DDS)** | Preparation & delivery | Proportioning pumps, heater, degasser, conductivity cell, UF controller, BLD |
| **Tier 4: Safety & Monitoring** | Sensors & alarms | Air-in-line detector, blood-leak detector, TMP monitor, temperature sensor, venous clamp |

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-Patient vs Single-Pass System**: Modern machines use the **single-pass** system where fresh dialysate is continuously produced and spent dialysate is discarded. The older **recirculating single-tank** system (Redy sorbent) is rarely used today but is sometimes asked in KTU exams for historical context.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Q1. Define hemodialysis. Mention any two uremic toxins removed during the procedure. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Hemodialysis is an extracorporeal renal replacement therapy in which blood is circulated through a semipermeable membrane against a counter-current flow of dialysate to remove metabolic waste products and excess fluid. Two uremic toxins removed are:
1. **Urea** (molecular weight 60 Da) — the principal end-product of protein catabolism.
2. **Creatinine** (molecular weight 113 Da) — produced from muscle creatine phosphate breakdown.

> Both toxins accumulate in renal failure and are removed by diffusion across the dialyzer membrane.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Q2. What is the principle of counter-current flow in a hemodialyzer? Why is it preferred over co-current flow? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
In counter-current flow, the blood and dialysate streams flow in **opposite directions** within the dialyzer. This maintains a **constant concentration gradient** of uremic solutes along the entire length of the membrane, thereby maximizing diffusive clearance. It is preferred over co-current flow because, in co-current flow, the concentration gradient diminishes along the length as both streams approach equilibrium, reducing the net diffusive transfer. Counter-current configuration typically yields **10–20% higher clearance** for the same flow rates and membrane area.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a hemodialysis machine** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List the **components of the dialysate delivery system (DDS)** and explain the role of the **proportioning pump, conductivity sensor, and temperature controller** in maintaining dialysate quality. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Derive the **Fick's Law of Diffusion** as applied to a hemodialyzer and explain how **clearance (K)** and **dialysance** are calculated for a counter-current dialyzer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A patient undergoes hemodialysis with the following data: $Q_B = 250$ mL/min, $Q_D = 500$ mL/min, $C_{Bi} = 1.5$ mg/mL, $C_{Bo} = 0.45$ mg/mL, $C_{Di} = 0$, body weight = 68 kg, session duration = 4 hours. Calculate (i) Urea clearance, (ii) URR, and (iii) Kt/V. State whether the dialysis is adequate. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions for Part B

### Solution to Question A:

#### (a) Working Principle of Hemodialysis Machine (7 Marks)

The hemodialysis machine consists of two independent fluid circuits coupled at the dialyzer:

**1. Blood Circuit (Extracorporeal):**
- Blood is withdrawn from the patient's vascular access (AV fistula) through an **arterial needle**.
- An **arterial pressure monitor** (-50 to -200 mmHg) measures pre-pump pressure to detect fistula dysfunction.
- A **heparin syringe pump** continuously infuses anticoagulant.
- A **blood roller pump** generates pulsatile flow at $Q_B = 200$–$500$ mL/min.
- Blood passes through the **dialyzer** where toxins diffuse across the membrane.
- A **venous bubble trap** removes air and clots.
- A **venous pressure monitor** (+50 to +250 mmHg) and **air-in-line detector** ensure safe return.
- Treated blood returns to the patient via the venous needle.

**2. Dialysate Circuit:**
- Concentrates A (acidic) and B (bicarbonate) are mixed with purified water in a **proportioning pump** (typical 1:34 dilution).
- A **heater** warms dialysate to 36–38°C.
- A **degasser** removes dissolved air (prevents air embolism via membrane pores).
- Dialysate flows counter-current to blood in the dialyzer.
- **Conductivity sensor** (~14 mS/cm) verifies electrolyte concentration.
- **Blood-leak detector** (photocell) detects membrane rupture.
- Spent dialysate is drained; a **UF pump** controls net fluid removal.

**3. Safety & Control Subsystem:**
- A microprocessor-based central unit monitors all sensors and triggers alarms/venous line clamping on out-of-range values.

#### (b) Dialysate Delivery System (DDS) — Component Roles (7 Marks)

| **Component** | **Function** | **KTU Specification** |
|---|---|---|
| Proportioning pump | Mixes concentrates A and B with RO water | Dilution ratio 1:34; flow ~500 mL/min |
| Conductivity sensor | Measures dialysate ionic strength | Target: 13.5–15.0 mS/cm; alarm if deviation > ±5% |
| Temperature controller | Maintains dialysate at body temperature | 36–38°C; alarm at >39°C (hemolysis risk) |
| Degasser | Removes dissolved gases | Negative pressure ~ -500 mmHg |
| Blood-leak detector | Optical detection of RBCs in dialysate | Photocell at 560 nm wavelength |
| UF controller | Regulates fluid removal rate | Volume accuracy ±1% |
| pH sensor | Monitors dialysate pH | Target 7.0–7.4 |

The **proportioning pump** ensures the correct ratio of acid and bicarbonate concentrates to avoid metabolic acidosis/alkalosis. The **conductivity sensor** provides continuous feedback on total ionic concentration, as conductivity is directly proportional to electrolyte content. The **temperature controller** uses a PID loop with a thermistor to prevent hemolysis and patient discomfort.

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### (a) Fick's Law and Clearance/Dialysance Derivation (7 Marks)

Fick's First Law states that the molar flux of a solute across a membrane is proportional to the concentration gradient:

$$J = -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

For a hollow-fiber dialyzer with $N$ fibers of length $L$ and inner radius $r$, the total surface area is $A = 2\pi r L N$. The mass transfer rate is:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Clearance (K)** is the volumetric rate at which blood is completely cleared of solute:

$$K = \frac{\text{mass removed per unit time}}{\text{concentration of solute in inflowing blood}} = \frac{Q_B(C_{Bi} - C_{Bo})}{C_{Bi}} = Q_B \left(1 - \frac{C_{Bo}}{C_{Bi}}\right)$$

**Dialysance** is the more general concept valid even when fresh dialysate contains some solute:

$$D_{ial} = \frac{Q_B (C_{Bi} - C_{Bo})}{C_{Bi} - C_{Di}}$$

When $C_{Di} = 0$ (fresh dialysate), dialysance reduces to clearance. The counter-current configuration maximizes $K$ by maintaining a near-constant gradient along the entire membrane length, expressed by the Michael equation derived in Section 3.1.

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $Q_B = 250$ mL/min, $Q_D = 500$ mL/min, $C_{Bi} = 1.5$ mg/mL, $C_{Bo} = 0.45$ mg/mL, $C_{Di} = 0$, weight = 68 kg, $t = 4$ hr = 240 min.

**Step 1: Urea Clearance**

$$K = \frac{(C_{Bi} - C_{Bo})}{C_{Bi}} \cdot Q_B = \frac{1.5 - 0.45}{1.5} \cdot 250$$

$$K = \frac{1.05}{1.5} \cdot 250 = 0.7 \cdot 250$$

$$\boxed{K = 175 \text{ mL/min}}$$

**Step 2: Urea Reduction Ratio (URR)**

$$R = \frac{C_{Bo}}{C_{Bi}} = \frac{0.45}{1.5} = 0.30$$

$$URR = 1 - R = 1 - 0.30$$

$$\boxed{URR = 0.70 = 70\%}$$

**Step 3: Kt/V Calculation**

Total body water $V = 0.6 \times 68 = 40.8$ L = 40,800 mL.

$$Kt/V = \frac{K \cdot t}{V} = \frac{175 \cdot 240}{40800} = \frac{42000}{40800}$$

$$\boxed{Kt/V = 1.029}$$

**Step 4: Adequacy Assessment**

> [!WARNING]
> **Result: Kt/V = 1.029 < 1.2 ⇒ INADEQUATE dialysis per KDOQI/KTU standards.**
>
> Although URR = 70% (which is borderline acceptable), the Kt/V falls below the **mandated threshold of 1.2**. The clinician should either:
> 1. **Increase $Q_B$** to >300 mL/min, or
> 2. **Extend session duration** to 4.5–5 hours, or
> 3. **Increase dialysate flow** to 800 mL/min, or
> 4. **Use a larger surface area dialyzer** (>2.0 m²).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Clearance with Dialysance.** Many students use them interchangeably. Remember: **Dialysance uses the dialysate inlet concentration**; **Clearance assumes $C_{Di} = 0$**.
> 2. **Body Water Volume**: Total body water = **0.6 × body weight** for adults (not 1.0×). Using the wrong factor leads to wrong Kt/V.
> 3. **Time Unit Consistency**: Convert hours to minutes (or vice versa) before multiplying $K \times t$. This is the most common arithmetic error.
> 4. **Counter-current vs Co-current**: Always mention counter-current explicitly when asked about dialyzer flow configuration — the examiner awards a dedicated mark.
> 5. **Kt/V adequacy threshold**: KTU expects the student to know the threshold value **1.2** and to explicitly state whether the calculated value meets the standard. Forgetting this clinical interpretation costs **1 mark minimum**.
> 6. **Membrane material**: If asked about the dialyzer membrane, state the modern standard (**polysulfone or polyethersulfone**) — older cellulose-based membranes are obsolete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Hemodialysis):**

- ☐ Hemodialysis = extracorporeal blood purification via semipermeable membrane; 3 sessions/week, 3–5 hr each.
- ☐ Three transport mechanisms: **Diffusion, Osmosis, Ultrafiltration**.
- ☐ **Counter-current flow** is the engineering standard — gives 10–20% higher clearance.
- ☐ **Fick's Law**: $J = -D \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ — the foundational mass-transfer equation.
- ☐ **Clearance**: $K = \dfrac{(C_{in} - C_{out})}{C_{in}} \cdot Q_B$ (mL/min).
- ☐ **Dialysance** is the more general form including $C_{Di}$.
- ☐ **Kt/V ≥ 1.2** = adequate dialysis (single-pool, thrice-weekly).
- ☐ **URR ≥ 65%** is the secondary adequacy marker.
- ☐ **Blood flow**: 200–500 mL/min; **Dialysate flow**: 300–800 mL/min; **TMP**: 50–300 mmHg.
- ☐ **Dialyzer membrane surface area**: 1.0–2.5 m²; **Hollow fiber ID**: 180–220 µm.
- ☐ **Modern membrane material**: Polysulfone, polyethersulfone, or cellulose triacetate.
- ☐ **Dialysate conductivity target**: 13.5–15.0 mS/cm; **Temperature**: 36–38°C.
- ☐ **Uremic toxins removed**: Urea (60 Da), Creatinine (113 Da), Uric Acid (168 Da), K⁺, PO₄³⁻.
- ☐ **Molecules RETAINED**: Albumin (66,000 Da), blood cells, platelets, large proteins.
- ☐ **Critical Safety Monitors**: Air-in-line detector, blood-leak detector, venous pressure monitor, UF controller, temperature sensor, conductivity cell.
- ☐ **Heparin** is used as the standard anticoagulant in the extracorporeal circuit.
- ☐ **Single-pass** system is the modern standard (replaces older recirculating single-tank).
- ☐ **Bicarbonate dialysate** is preferred over acetate (better cardiovascular stability).
- ☐ **Ultrafiltration rate limit**: < 13 mL/kg/hr to prevent intradialytic hypotension.
- ☐ **Henderson-Hasselbalch** governs the bicarbonate buffer chemistry in dialysate.
- ☐ **Fistula types**: AV fistula > AV graft > central venous catheter (in order of preference for vascular access).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Peritoneal Dialysis: Techniques and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT502 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Peritoneal Dialysis: Techniques and Equipment ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Peritoneal Dialysis (PD)** is a **renal replacement therapy (RRT)** in which a sterile, electrolyte-balanced solution called **dialysate** is introduced into the **peritoneal cavity**, allowed to dwell for a prescribed time, and then drained. The **peritoneum** (a thin, living, semipermeable serous membrane lining the abdominal cavity and covering the abdominal viscera) acts as the **natural filter** that separates the blood in the **peritoneal capillaries** from the dialysate in the cavity. Solutes and water exchange across this biological membrane, accomplishing the **purification** and **fluid balance** functions normally performed by the kidneys. ### 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition Peritoneal Dialysis is a **diffusion- and osmosis-based extracorporeal-free (entirely internal) blood purification technique** that exploits the **peritoneal membrane** as a natural semipermeable barrier. The driving forces are: - **Concentration gradient** ($\Delta C$) for solute removal (diffusion of urea, creatinine, potassium, phosphate, etc., from blood $\rightarrow$ dialysate). - **Osmotic gradient** ($\Delta \pi$) created by an osmotic agent (most commonly **glucose**) for water removal (ultrafiltration). The peritoneum functions as a **living hemodialysis membrane**, offering a **total surface area of approximately 1.0–2.0 m²** in an adult, roughly equivalent to the glomerular filtration surface of the kidneys. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is PD important clinically?** > - **Continuous** therapy (24/7 or 8–10 hours overnight) → smoother biochemical control. > - **No extracorporeal blood circuit** → lower hemodynamic stress (ideal for infants, elderly, diabetics, cardiac-compromised patients). > - **Home-based** → improved quality of life, **better preservation of residual renal function** compared to hemodialysis (HD). > - **No anticoagulation** (heparin) generally required → useful in patients with bleeding risks. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tea-Bag in a Bowl" Imagine placing a **tea-bag** (representing the **peritoneum with blood inside the capillaries**) into a bowl of plain water (the **dialysate**). After a few minutes, the tea (urea, creatinine, toxins) **diffuses out** into the water, and the water becomes colored. If you stir in some sugar (osmotic agent) into the water, water will flow **into the tea-bag** (ultrafiltration) because the sugar-rich solution outside is "thirstier." In PD: - The **tea-bag** = the peritoneal membrane with capillary blood. - The **tea in the bag** = nitrogenous waste products. - The **bowl of water** = the dialysate. - The **sugar stirred in** = glucose (or icodextrin) creating an osmotic gradient. Just as the colored water is thrown away and fresh clear water is used, **spent dialysate is drained and fresh dialysate is infused** repeatedly, in cycles called **exchanges**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook (KTU Favorite):** *PD = Put Dialysate in belly → Dwell → Drain.* This three-step pattern is the **fundamental cycle** of every PD technique, regardless of automation. ### 1.3 Key Numerical Facts (Bolded for KTU Exam Recall) - **Peritoneal surface area:** $\approx$ **1.0–2.0 m²** (in adult). - **Peritoneal membrane thickness:** $\approx$ **20–40 µm** effective transport distance. - **Normal peritoneal cavity capacity:** $\approx$ **2 L** (up to 3 L tolerated in adults). - **Glucose in standard dialysate:** **1.5%, 2.5%, or 4.25%** w/v. - **Standard exchange volume:** **1.5–2.5 L** per dwell (pediatric ~ 10–40 mL/kg). - **Number of CAPD exchanges/day:** **4–5**. - **Number of APD cycles/night:** **5–8** (over 8–10 hours). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomical Basis — Why the Peritoneum Works The peritoneum has two layers: 1. **Parietal peritoneum** — lines the inner abdominal wall. 2. **Visceral peritoneum** — covers the abdominal organs (intestines, liver, spleen, etc.). Between them lies the **peritoneal cavity** (a potential space filled with $\sim$ 50–100 mL of serous fluid in health). The membrane is **richly vascularized** by capillaries from the splanchnic circulation — these capillaries are the **blood side** of the "natural filter." The functional barrier separating blood and dialysate is not the peritoneum alone but the **interstitial tissue + mesothelial cell layer + capillary endothelium**, often modeled as the **three-pore model** of Rippe (1987): | Pore Type | Radius (Å) | Function | % Hydraulic Conductance | |-----------|------------|----------|-------------------------| | **Ultra-small pores (aquaporin-1)** | 2–4 | Water only (transcellular) | ~ 40 % | | **Small pores** | 4–6 | Water + small solutes (Na⁺, K⁺, urea, glucose) | ~ 60 % | | **Large pores** | 20–40 | Macromolecules (proteins) | < 1 % | > [!NOTE] > **Significance for KTU:** During hypertonic (e.g., 4.25 % glucose) dwells, water transport occurs **preferentially through aquaporin-1** — this is called **sodium sieving**, because Na⁺ does NOT follow the water, leading to *hypernatremic* ultrafiltration. This is a common KTU viva question. ### 2.2 Mass-Transfer Mechanisms in PD | Mechanism | Driving Force | What Moves | Engineering Analogy | |-----------|---------------|------------|---------------------| | **Diffusion** | Concentration gradient $\Delta C$ | Solutes (urea, Cr, K⁺, PO₄³⁻) | Smell spreading across a room | | **Osmosis (UF)** | Osmotic gradient $\Delta \pi$ (glucose-based) | Water from blood $\rightarrow$ dialysate | Sugar pulling water out of a fruit | | **Convective transport (solute drag)** | Water flow carries solutes | Solutes coupled to UF | Debris carried by a flowing river | | **Absorption (lymphatic)** | Intraperitoneal pressure | Fluid + solutes back to lymphatics | Slow leak back into the system | ### 2.3 Dialysate Composition — Engineering Specification | Component | Typical Concentration | Function | |-----------|----------------------|----------| | **Sodium (Na⁺)** | **132–134 mmol/L** | Sodium balance (low-Na dialysate improves UF) | | **Chloride (Cl⁻)** | **95–102 mmol/L** | Anion balance | | **Calcium (Ca²⁺)** | **1.25–1.75 mmol/L** | Bone/mineral; avoids hypercalcemia | | **Magnesium (Mg²⁺)** | **0.25–0.75 mmol/L** | Cofactor balance | | **Lactate or Bicarbonate** | **35–40 mmol/L** | Buffer to correct metabolic acidosis | | **Glucose** | **1.5 / 2.5 / 4.25 % w/v** | Osmotic agent (creates UF gradient) | | **Osmolality** | **346–485 mOsm/L** | Hyperosmolar to blood (~ 285 mOsm/L) | | **pH** | **5.0–5.5 (in bag), 7.0–7.4 (in vivo)** | Sterility (acidic in bag) + biocompatibility | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **bicarbonate vs. lactate** as buffers. *Lactate* is metabolized in the liver to bicarbonate, but in shock/liver failure this conversion is impaired → newer **bicarbonate-based dialysates** (physiological pH, two-chamber bag) are now standard in modern systems. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) — High-Yield Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All table entries use `\vert` or `\mid` (not bare `\|`) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. | # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables / Units | Engineering Use | |---|---------------|---------|-------------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Fick's Law of Diffusion (1-D)** | $J_s = -D \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $J_s$ = solute flux (mol/s), $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s), $A$ = area (m²) | Governing equation for solute transport across peritoneum | | 2 | **Peritoneal MTAC (Mass Transfer Area Coefficient)** | $\text{MTAC} = \dfrac{V_d}{t} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{d0} \cdot C_{d0} - V_b \cdot C_b}{V_{dt} \cdot C_{dt} - V_b \cdot C_b}\right)$ | $V_d$ = dialysate volume, $V_b$ = body water, $C$ = concentration (mM) | Quantifies membrane transport capacity (used in PET) | | 3 | **Urea Clearance (PD)** | $K_{urea} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot C_d}{C_b \cdot t}$ | $V_d$ in L, $t$ in min, $C$ in mM, $K$ in mL/min | Weekly Kt/V calculation | | 4 | **Weekly Kt/V (PD adequacy)** | $\text{Kt/V}_{week} = \dfrac{7 \cdot D_{urea} \cdot 1000}{V_{urea}}$ | $D_{urea}$ = daily dialysate urea, $V_{urea}$ = urea distribution volume | Target ≥ **1.7** per KDOQI | | 5 | **Ultrafiltration (Starling-Kedem modified)** | $J_v = L_p \cdot S \cdot (\sigma \cdot \Delta\pi - \Delta P)$ | $L_p$ = hydraulic conductivity, $S$ = surface area, $\sigma$ = reflection coefficient ($\approx$ 0.03 for glucose), $\Delta\pi$ = osmotic gradient, $\Delta P$ = hydrostatic gradient | Net water removal per unit time | | 6 | **Net UF volume per exchange** | $\text{UF} = V_{\text{drain}} - V_{\text{fill}}$ | mL or L | Clinical measure of fluid removal | | 7 | **Osmolality of glucose solution (approx.)** | $\text{Osm} \approx 1.85 \cdot C_{glucose}$ | $C$ in g/L, Osm in mOsm/L | Quick estimate of UF driving force | | 8 | **Henderson-Hasselbalch (dialysate buffer)** | $pH = pK_a + \log\!\left(\dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{[H_2CO_3]}\right)$ | $pK_a \approx 6.1$ | Predicts plasma HCO₃⁻ correction | | 9 | **Solute Diffusion Time (characteristic)** | $\tau = \dfrac{x^2}{2D}$ | $x$ = membrane thickness, $D$ = diffusivity | Optimal dwell-time selection | | 10 | **Peritoneal Creatinine Clearance (weekly)** | $CrCl = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{D_{Cr} \cdot 7 \cdot 1000}{V_{Cr}}$ | mL/min | Target ≥ 45 L/week/1.73 m² | ### 2.5 Why Each Formula Matters in Engineering Practice - **MTAC (Eq. 2):** Engineers designing **personalized cycler algorithms** use the patient's MTAC to compute dwell time for target solute clearance. - **Kt/V (Eq. 4):** The **gold-standard** for dialysis adequacy set by KDOQI/EBPG; informs the prescription of dwell volume and number of exchanges. - **Eq. 5 (Starling):** Validates the **3-pore model** in CFD simulations of peritoneal transport — used by researchers like Rippe to design icodextrin and glucose-polymer-based dialysates. - **Eq. 7 (Osmolality):** Allows an engineer to back-calculate the **glucose concentration** needed for a target UF volume during prescription. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Solute Diffusion Across the Peritoneum (Fick's First Law) > [!NOTE] > **Starting Principle:** Fick's First Law of Diffusion is the **fundamental governing equation** for solute transport in PD. The derivation below shows how we obtain the **peritoneal clearance** from a simple mass balance. **Step 1 — Define the flux $J_s$ (mol/s) of a solute across a membrane of area $A$:** $$J_s = -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$ Here $D$ is the **diffusivity** (m²/s), $A$ the **effective membrane area** (m²), and $\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ the **concentration gradient** (mol/m⁴). **Step 2 — Integrate across the membrane thickness $\Delta x$ (assuming steady state, $D$ and $A$ constant):** $$J_s = \frac{D \cdot A \cdot (C_b - C_d)}{\Delta x}$$ where $C_b$ = blood-side concentration and $C_d$ = dialysate-side concentration. The minus sign of Fick's law disappears because the gradient $(C_b - C_d)$ is positive (blood is more concentrated in toxins than fresh dialysate). **Step 3 — Recognize that $D \cdot A / \Delta x$ is the **Mass Transfer Area Coefficient (MTAC)** for that solute:** $$\text{MTAC} = \frac{D \cdot A}{\Delta x} \quad [\text{mL/min}]$$ Therefore: $$J_s = \text{MTAC} \cdot (C_b - C_d)$$ This is the **engineer's working equation** for peritoneal solute transfer. **Step 4 — Compute the amount removed in a single exchange (dwell time $t$):** $$\Delta M = J_s \cdot t = \text{MTAC} \cdot (C_b - C_d) \cdot t$$ **Step 5 — Recognize that for **peritoneal clearance $K$**, we equate $\Delta M$ to $K \cdot C_b \cdot t$:** $$K = \frac{\Delta M}{C_b \cdot t} = \text{MTAC} \cdot \frac{(C_b - C_d)}{C_b} = \text{MTAC} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{C_d}{C_b}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Substituting a typical value** (e.g., MTAC for urea $\approx$ 20 mL/min, $C_d/C_b \approx 0.6$ at end of a 4-h dwell): $$K_{urea} = 20 \times (1 - 0.6) = 8 \; \text{mL/min} \;\; \text{per exchange}$$ **Step 7 — Multiply by exchanges per day/week for total weekly clearance**, the input to the **Kt/V adequacy equation**. ### 3.2 Derivation: Net Ultrafiltration Using the Modified Starling Equation > [!NOTE] > **Starting Principle:** Water removal in PD is driven by the **osmotic gradient** (sugar-rich dialysate is "thirstier" than blood). Starling's equation, modified for peritoneal transport, gives the volumetric UF rate. **Step 1 — Starling's classical equation (filtration across a capillary):** $$J_v = L_p \cdot S \cdot \left[\left(P_c - P_i\right) - \sigma \cdot \left(\pi_c - \pi_i\right)\right]$$ with $P$ = hydrostatic, $\pi$ = oncotic, $L_p$ = hydraulic conductivity, $S$ = surface area, $\sigma$ = Staverman's reflection coefficient. **Step 2 — Apply to PD by recognizing that the *capillary* blood is on one side and *dialysate* is on the other side. There is **no significant hydrostatic gradient** in PD (intraperitoneal pressure is low, $\Delta P \approx 0$):** $$J_v \approx L_p \cdot S \cdot \sigma \cdot (\pi_d - \pi_b)$$ **Step 3 — Compute the osmotic pressure of dialysate from glucose (a non-ideal solute), using van't Hoff's law with an osmotic coefficient $\phi \approx 0.95$:** $$\pi_d = \phi \cdot n_{glucose} \cdot R \cdot T = \phi \cdot C_{glucose} \cdot R \cdot T$$ For a 2.5 % glucose solution, $C_{glucose} \approx 2.5/180 = 0.0139$ mol/L (since MW of glucose = 180 g/mol): $$\pi_d = 0.95 \times 0.0139 \times 0.0821 \times 310 \approx 0.336 \; \text{atm} \approx 340 \; \text{mOsm/L}$$ **Step 4 — Blood oncotic pressure $\pi_b \approx 25$ mmHg $\approx 1$ mOsm/L × 25 (in equivalent osmotic units). For glucose, $\pi_b$ is negligible because glucose is freely filtered and equilibrates rapidly.** **Step 5 — Substitute numerical values** ($L_p S \cdot \sigma \approx 0.04$ mL/min/mmHg for the peritoneum): $$J_v \approx 0.04 \times (340 - 5) \approx 13.4 \; \text{mL/min} \;\; \text{at start of 2.5\% dwell}$$ **Step 6 — Integrate over a typical 4-hour dwell to find total UF volume:** $$\text{UF} \approx \int_0^{240} J_v(t) \, dt \approx 800 - 1200 \; \text{mL}$$ (UF declines as glucose is absorbed and $\Delta \pi$ drops — this is the **decay of UF over time**.) ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Technique Comparison > [!TIP] > **KTU examiners love comparison tables.** Memorize the trade-offs between CAPD, APD, and IPD for both short and long-answer questions. | Parameter | **CAPD** | **APD / CCPD** | **IPD** | **Tidal PD** | |-----------|----------|----------------|---------|--------------| | **Full name** | Continuous Ambulatory PD | Automated PD / Continuous Cycling PD | Intermittent PD | Tidal PD | | **Operator** | Patient (manual) | Cycler machine (automated) | Nurse / cycler | Cycler machine | | **When done** | Continuous, 24×7 | Overnight (8–10 h) + daytime dwell | Hospital-based, intermittent | Overnight | | **# exchanges/day** | 4–5 manual | 5–8 at night | Multiple short dwells | Continuous residual + tidal refills | | **Dwell time** | 4–6 h daytime, 8–10 h overnight | 1–2 h per cycle | 30 min – 1 h | Variable | | **Patient mobility** | High (between exchanges) | Excellent (free daytime "wet" abdomen) | Low (bed-bound) | Good | | **Cycler needed?** | **No** | **Yes** | Sometimes | **Yes** | | **Peritonitis risk** | Moderate (manual touch) | Lower (closed system) | High (frequent connects) | Low | | **Solute clearance** | Good (continuous) | Better for small solutes | Low | Moderate-High | | **UF control** | Good | Excellent (programmed) | Variable | Excellent | | **Cost** | Low (no machine) | Higher (cycler cost) | Low | High | | **Best for** | Self-motivated adults | Working adults, children | Acute renal failure, inpatients | Patients needing high UF | ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Weekly Kt/V Calculation (KTU Exam Favorite) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example:** A 70 kg patient on CAPD produces the following data in 24 hours: > - Total drained dialysate volume = 10 L > - Average dialysate urea = 12 mmol/L > - Plasma urea = 25 mmol/L > - Estimate total body water (V) = 0.6 × 70 = 42 L **Step 1 — Compute daily dialysate urea removed:** $$D_{urea} = 10 \times 12 = 120 \; \text{mmol/day} = 0.12 \; \text{mol/day}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to grams (urea MW = 60):** $$D_{urea} = 0.12 \times 60 = 7.2 \; \text{g/day}$$ **Step 3 — Compute weekly Kt/V (K = clearance, t = time, V = distribution volume):** $$\text{Kt/V} = \frac{7 \times D_{urea}}{V \times C_b} = \frac{7 \times 120}{42 \times 25}$$ $$\text{Kt/V} = \frac{840}{1050} = 0.8 \;\; \text{per day (BUT this is wrong — correct formula below)}$$ **Step 4 — Correct formula uses concentrations and volumes directly:** $$\text{Kt/V}_{week} = \frac{7 \times V_d \times C_d}{V \times C_b} = \frac{7 \times 10 \times 12}{42 \times 25} = \frac{840}{1050} = 0.8$$ **Step 5 — Compare with KDOQI target = 1.7 → patient is UNDER-DIALYZED. Prescription should be increased (larger volume or more exchanges).** > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Error:** Students often compute daily Kt/V and forget to multiply by 7. Always write "**per week**" in your answer. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The diagrams below are **engineered block-level schematics** (per protocol) since peritoneal dialysis is a clinical/biological process that benefits from functional and process-flow representation. ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Peritoneal Dialysis System Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Blood
in Capillaries] -->|Diffusion of toxins
Driven by ΔC| B[Peritoneal Membrane
Semi-permeable barrier
Area ~ 1.5 m²] B -->|Osmotic UF
Driven by Δπ glucose| C[Peritoneal Cavity
Holds 1.5-2.5 L dialysate] C -->|Drain via gravity| D[Tenckhoff Catheter
Silicon rubber tube
Two Dacron cuffs] D -->|Closed tubing| E[Transfer Set
Y-set or double bag] E -->|Manual exchange
CAPD| F[Fresh Dialysate Bag
1.5 / 2.5 / 4.25 percent] E -->|Automated exchange
APD| G[PD Cycler Machine
Heats, measures, dispenses] F -->|Spent dialysate| H[Weigh and Dispose
Drain bag] G -->|Spent dialysate| H H -->|Record UF| I[Patient Logbook
Volume and weight] I -->|Adjust prescription| F I -->|Adjust prescription| G ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2 — CAPD Exchange Cycle (Process Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD P0([Patient has dialysate
dwelling in abdomen]) --> P1[Step 1: FILL
Infuse 1.5-2 L fresh dialysate
Time: 10 minutes] P1 --> P2[Step 2: DWELL
Wait 4-6 hours
Diffusion and UF occur
No active equipment] P2 --> P3[Step 3: DRAIN
Drain spent dialysate
Time: 20 minutes
Volume = Fill + UF] P3 --> P4{Volume drained
approximately equals
Fill + UF?} P4 -->|Yes, proceed| P5[Inspect effluent for clarity
and fibrin] P4 -->|No, investigate| P6[Check for catheter
kink or constipation] P5 --> P7[Repeat cycle
4-5 times per day] P6 --> P7 P7 --> P0 ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3 — APD Cycler Operational Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Warmer Tray
Heats dialysate to 37 deg C] --> B[Pump 1
Heater Bag Pump] C[Heater Bag
5 L reservoir] --> B B -->|Dispense 1.5-2.5 L| D[Patient Peritoneum
via Tenckhoff catheter] D -->|Dwell 1-2 h| E[Pump 2
Drain Pump] E -->|Measure volume| F[Weighing Scale
UF sensor] F --> G[Spent Bag
Disposal] H[Control Unit
Microprocessor
Touch screen UI] -->|Commands| A H -->|Commands| B H -->|Commands| E H -->|Reads UF data| F H -->|Logs to| I[Memory Card
Patient record] J[Alarm System
Over fill, low flow, temp] -.->|Triggers| H ``` ### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Tenckhoff Catheter Anatomy (Engineering Cross-Section) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[External segment
Exits skin at exit site] --> B[External Dacron Cuff
Prevents bacterial migration
Promotes tissue in-growth] B --> C[Tunnel segment
Subcutaneous

Anchors to peritoneum

Length 15-20 cm
Coiled or straight tip] E --> F[Side holes

For inflow and outflow] E --> G[Cuff tip design

Used for low UF patients] note1[Material: Medical grade silicone rubber
Outer diameter: 5 mm
Inner diameter: 2.6 mm
Length: 35-42 cm] note1 -.-> A ``` ### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Peritoneal Equilibration Test (PET) Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient on PD] --> B[PET Test
2.5 percent glucose
4 hour dwell] B --> C[Measure D/P
Dialysate to Plasma
ratio at 4 h] C --> D{D/P creatinine} D -->|D/P greater than 0.81| E[High Transporter
Short dwells best
APD preferred] D -->|D/P 0.65 to 0.81| F[High Average
Most flexible
Both CAPD and APD] D -->|D/P 0.5 to 0.65| G[Low Average
Long dwells preferred
CAPD or icodextrin] D -->|D/P less than 0.5| H[Low Transporter
