These currents, though sub-thermal, modulate cellular activities such as calcium ion signaling, growth factor release, and bone remodeling.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a circular tissue region of radius $r$ placed in a uniform time-varying magnetic field $B(t)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The magnetic flux through the tissue cross-section:

$$\Phi(t) = B(t) \cdot \pi r^2$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** By Faraday's law, the induced EMF around the tissue loop:

$$\text{EMF} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\pi r^2 \frac{dB}{dt}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The induced electric field magnitude in the tissue (assuming uniform distribution along the circumference):

$$E = \frac{\vert \text{EMF} \vert}{2\pi r} = \frac{r}{2} \left\vert \frac{dB}{dt} \right\vert$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Applying Ohm's law in microscopic form $J = \sigma E$:

$$\boxed{J = \frac{\sigma r}{2} \frac{dB}{dt}}$$

For $dB/dt = 1$ T/s, $r = 0.05$ m, $\sigma = 0.6$ S/m: $J = 0.015$ A/m² (15 mA/m²), within the therapeutic range.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 above. Describe: Mains → HV Supply → Magnetron → Waveguide → Directional Coupler → Applicator → Tissue.

**Magnetron Operation (1.5 marks):** The magnetron is a high-power vacuum tube oscillator. Electrons emitted from a heated cathode are accelerated toward an anode containing resonant cavities. A strong external magnetic field (from a permanent magnet) forces electrons into spiral paths, exciting RF oscillations in the cavities at 2.45 GHz. Output power is typically 25–100 W.

**Waveguide and Applicator (1.5 marks):** At 2.45 GHz, conventional wires have unacceptable losses. A **rectangular waveguide (WR-430)** with internal dimensions $86.36 \times 43.18$ mm (chosen for dominant TE₁₀ mode) channels the RF power. The applicator (conical horn, dielectric rod, or circular director) shapes the beam for targeted tissue irradiation.

**Directional Coupler and Safety Interlock (2 marks):** The directional coupler samples forward and reflected power separately. **Forward power** is delivered to tissue; **reflected power** indicates impedance mismatch. If the applicator detaches or short-circuits, reflected power rises sharply. The safety interlock detects this within ~10 ms and **deactivates the magnetron HV supply**, preventing patient RF burns — a critical safety feature in MWD.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SWD frequency (27.12 MHz) with microwave (2.45 GHz)** — losing 1 mark is common.
> 2. **Penetration depth is INVERSELY related to frequency** — students often state the opposite.
> 3. **In SAR formula, write the electric field as RMS value** $\vert E \vert$ — if you use peak value $E_0$, you must include the factor $1/2$.
> 4. **Block diagrams must include safety interlocks** — KTU examiners specifically test this for full marks.
> 5. **Mention duty cycle for pulsed mode** (e.g., 25%) — generic statements like "intermittent power" lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Module 3 Summary Checklist:**

✅ **Electromagnetic therapy** uses controlled EM fields (static to microwave) for tissue heating (diathermy) or cellular stimulation (PEMF).

✅ **ITU-allocated therapeutic frequencies:** SWD = **27.12 MHz**; MWD = **2.45 GHz** (both in ISM bands).

✅ **Maxwell's equations** govern all EM therapy — **Faraday's law** is the basis of PEMF, **Ampère-Maxwell law** is the basis of SWD/MWD heating.

✅ **Penetration depth** $D = 1/(2\alpha)$, approximately $D \approx 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu_0 \sigma}$ for lossy tissue. **Higher frequency → less penetration.**

✅ **SAR (Specific Absorption Rate)** = $\sigma \vert E \vert^2 / (2\rho)$ — quantifies absorbed EM power per kg of tissue. Units: **W/kg**.

✅ **Pennes bio-heat equation** describes tissue temperature rise accounting for perfusion, conduction, and metabolic heat.

✅ **SWD** uses **capacitor plates (capacitive)** or **cable/drum (inductive)** applicators; operates in **continuous** (thermal) or **pulsed** (non-thermal) modes.

✅ **MWD** uses a **magnetron oscillator + waveguide + directional coupler**; **safety interlock on reflected power** is mandatory.

✅ **PEMF** uses **Faraday induction** — coil produces $dB/dt$, which induces eddy currents $J = \sigma r (dB/dt)/2$ in tissue. Sub-thermal, used for bone healing and pain relief.

✅ **Therapeutic target temperature:** 40–45 °C (controlled hyperthermia); avoid >45 °C (protein denaturation risk).

✅ **Coupling media** (water bolus, gel) minimize reflection at skin-air interface and improve power transfer.

✅ **Continuous mode** → thermal effects (chronic conditions); **Pulsed mode** → non-thermal (acute conditions, cellular stimulation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotic Rehabilitation Devices and Exoskeletons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT502 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Robotic Rehabilitation Devices and Exoskeletons ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Rehabilitation Robot** is a mechatronic device (mechanical structure + electronic control + software intelligence) that automates, assists, or enhances the physical therapy process for patients suffering from neuromuscular impairments caused by stroke, spinal cord injury (SCI), traumatic brain injury (TBI), or degenerative diseases. These systems are classified as **Active Medical Devices** under IEC 60601-1 safety standards and operate under a closed-loop biofeedback control architecture. An **Exoskeleton** (from Greek *exo* = "outside" and *skeleton*) is a wearable, anthropomorphic robotic structure that augments, reinforces, or restores human motor capability by applying external mechanical power in parallel with the wearer's limb. Unlike end-effector robots that contact the patient only at the distal segment (e.g., hand or foot), exoskeletons provide **full kinematic correspondence** with the human joints. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 3, the focus is on (a) classification of robotic rehabilitators, (b) end-effector vs. exoskeleton architectures, (c) actuation technologies, and (d) clinical applications including upper-limb, lower-limb, and gait-training devices. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iron Suit Therapist" Imagine a patient recovering from a stroke who cannot fully lift their arm. A traditional therapist physically holds the patient's arm and guides it through repetitive reaching motions hundreds of times per session — this is exhausting for the human therapist and inconsistent in intensity. Now imagine a **lightweight robotic "iron suit"** strapped gently onto the patient's arm. Sensors detect even the faintest muscle signal (or movement intent). A small electric motor at the elbow joint then **multiplies this weak effort into a full, smooth motion** — like a power-steering system for the human body. The robot never tires, can record exact progress in degrees and newton-metres, and delivers **precisely dosed therapy** for thousands of repetitions. That is, in essence, a rehabilitation exoskeleton. ### 1.3 Two Architectural Classes | Class | Contact Points | Mechanical Complexity | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **End-Effector Robot** | Single distal point (hand/foot) | Low | MIT-Manus, InMotion ARM | | **Exoskeleton Robot** | Multiple joints (shoulder + elbow + wrist) | High | ARMEO Power, Lokomat, ReWalk | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** Clinical efficacy of a rehabilitation robot is quantified using the **Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA)** score (max **66** for upper extremity, max **34** for lower extremity), along with the **Modified Ashworth Scale (MAS)** for spasticity (0 = no increase in tone, 4 = rigid joint). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Rehabilitation Robots **Step 1 — By Anatomical Region** - Upper-limb robots (shoulder, elbow, wrist, hand) - Lower-limb robots (hip, knee, ankle) - Gait trainers (full-body, treadmill-based) - Hand/finger robots (HapticKnob, Hand-of-Hope) **Step 2 — By Mechanical Architecture** - **End-effector type:** The robot's actuator is connected only to the distal segment of the limb. Forces are transmitted back through the limb's own passive joints — joint angles must be inferred (not directly measured). Example: InMotion ARM (shoulder/elbow). - **Exoskeleton type:** Each actuated joint of the robot is **aligned coaxially** with the corresponding anatomical joint. This enables direct measurement of joint torques and angles, improving transparency and control bandwidth. **Step 3 — By Therapeutic Goal** - **Assistive:** Restores function for Activities of Daily Living (ADL). - **Therapeutic:** Promotes neuroplasticity and motor relearning (intensive, repetitive task-specific training). - **Augmentative:** Enhances performance of able-bodied users (military/industrial exoskeletons). ### 2.2 Actuation Technologies — The "Why" Behind Each Choice - **Electric (DC/BLDC motors + harmonic drives):** High precision, clean, easy to control via PWM. Limitation: limited torque density. - **Pneumatic (McKibben artificial muscles):** High power-to-weight ratio, inherent compliance (safe for human interaction). Limitation: noisy compressor, non-linear dynamics. - **Hydraulic:** Highest force density; used in heavy-duty exoskeletons (e.g., BLEEX). Limitation: leaks, bulky reservoir. - **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA):** A spring is intentionally placed between the motor and the load. This decouples the high-impedance motor from the low-impedance human limb, providing **intrinsic shock tolerance** and accurate force control — critical for safe physical human-robot interaction (pHRI). > [!NOTE] > **Why compliance matters:** The human body's intrinsic impedance is low. A stiff (high-impedance) robot can dangerously resist a patient's voluntary motion. Adding a **mechanical admittance** element (spring/damper) — either passive (SEA) or active (impedance control) — is therefore a foundational requirement. ### 2.3 Sensor Suite | Sensor Type | Measurement | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Encoder / Resolver** | Joint angle (rad) | Position feedback | | **Potentiometer** | Angular displacement (deg) | Low-cost angle sensing | | **Force/Torque Sensor** | Interaction force (N) / torque (N·m) | Impedance control, intention detection | | **EMG (Electromyography)** | Muscle electrical activity (µV–mV) | Patient-intent detection via sEMG | | **EEG (Electroencephalography)** | Cortical potentials (µV) | Brain-computer interface (BCI) trigger | | **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** | 3-axis acceleration / angular velocity | Gait-phase detection | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\tau = I \alpha + b \omega + k \theta$ | Joint torque (rotational dynamics) | N·m | | 2 | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau_{robot} + \tau_{human}$ | Lagrangian model of coupled human–robot | N·m | | 3 | $Z(s) = \dfrac{F(s)}{V(s)} = M s + B + \dfrac{K}{s}$ | Mechanical impedance in Laplace domain | N·s/m | | 4 | $F_{SEA} = K_s (q_m - q_l)$ | Series Elastic Actuator force (Hooke's law) | N | | 5 | $B = \zeta \cdot 2\sqrt{MK}$ | Critical damping coefficient | N·s/m | | 6 | $V_{gait} = L_{stride} \cdot f_{cadence}$ | Average walking speed | m/s | | 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Actuator efficiency | % | | 8 | $SNR_{EMG} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | EMG signal quality | dB | **Note on Equation 3:** Impedance $Z(s)$ is the Laplace-domain transfer function relating output force $F(s)$ to input velocity $V(s)$. It comprises inertia $M$, viscous damping $B$, and stiffness $K$ — the three tunable parameters in **impedance control**. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Rehabilitation robotics is the testbed of choice for **physical Human-Robot Interaction (pHRI)** control theory. The control algorithms developed here (impedance/admittance, assist-as-needed) are directly transferred to surgical robotics (da Vinci), collaborative industrial robots (cobots), and assistive mobility devices for the elderly population. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Impedance Control Law for a Single Joint The most clinically important derivation in rehabilitation robotics is the **impedance control law** developed by Neville Hogan (MIT, 1985). The goal is to make the robot behave as a programmable mass-spring-damper system as seen by the patient. **Step 1 — Define the desired dynamic relationship between the robot's end-effector position error and the force it exerts on the patient:** $$ M_d \ddot{x}_e + B_d \dot{x}_e + K_d x_e = F_{ext} $$ **Step 2 — Define the position error $x_e$ as the deviation between the actual end-effector position $x$ and a virtual equilibrium trajectory $x_d$:** $$ x_e = x - x_d $$ **Step 3 — Substitute and expand the derivatives:** $$ M_d (\ddot{x} - \ddot{x}_d) + B_d (\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) + K_d (x - x_d) = F_{ext} $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for the robot's commanded acceleration $\ddot{x}$:** $$ M_d \ddot{x} = F_{ext} - B_d (\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) - K_d (x - x_d) + M_d \ddot{x}_d $$ **Step 5 — Multiply both sides by the robot's Cartesian inertia $M(q)$ and add the Coriolis and gravity compensation terms to obtain the final joint-space torque command:** $$ \tau_{ctrl} = M(q)\ddot{x} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) - J(q)^T F_{ext} $$ **Step 6 — Interpretation of the result:** The first three terms on the right-hand side are the standard **computed-torque** feed-forward components; the final term $J(q)^T F_{ext}$ uses the Jacobian transpose to map the measured external Cartesian force into joint torques. By tuning $M_d$, $B_d$, and $K_d$, the therapist can make the robot feel "stiff" (high $K_d$), "sluggish" (high $M_d$), or "smooth" (high $B_d$) to the patient. > [!NOTE] > **Practical KTU tip:** When asked to "derive the impedance control law," always begin with the *desired* impedance relation and work backward to the *commanded* torque. Examiners award marks for the *structure* of the derivation, not just the final equation. ### 3.2 Derivation — Critical Damping Ratio for a Safe SEA **Step 1 — Standard second-order mass-spring-damper equation:** $$ M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F(t) $$ **Step 2 — Write the characteristic equation:** $$ M s^2 + B s + K = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Compare to canonical form $s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}} $$ **Step 4 — Identify $2\zeta\omega_n = B/M$:** $$ \zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}} $$ **Step 5 — Solve for the critical damping coefficient $B_{crit}$ when $\zeta = 1$:** $$ B_{crit} = 2\sqrt{MK} $$ **Step 6 — Clinical safety rule:** For pHRI, the damping must satisfy $0.7 \leq \zeta \leq 1.0$ (underdamped to critically damped). Lower values cause oscillatory force feedback that can trigger spastic reflexes; higher values make the robot feel "dead" and reduce therapeutic engagement. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Exoskeleton and End-Effector Robots | Evaluation Criterion | End-Effector Robot | Exoskeleton Robot | |---|---|---| | Number of actuated DOF | 1–2 (low) | 4–12 (high) | | Joint alignment required | **No** | **Yes** (coaxial alignment critical) | | Singularities | At limb workspace boundaries | Within robot's own kinematic range | | Ability to isolate single joint | **Poor** (gravity-coupled) | **Excellent** | | Patient donning/doffing time | < 1 min | 5–15 min | | Mechanical complexity | Low | High | | Cost (commercial, 2024) | ₹15 L – 60 L | ₹80 L – 3 Cr | | Example clinical use | Reaching therapy | Gait + reaching + ADL | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid) The diagram below shows the closed-loop architecture of a generic rehabilitation exoskeleton, including the patient in the loop, sensing modalities, and control hierarchy. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Patient Limb
with Impairment] -->|Intent
sEMG or Motion| B[Sensing Layer
EMG, Encoder, ForceTorque, IMU] B --> C[Signal Conditioning
Amplify, Filter, ADC] C --> D[High-Level Controller
Task Planner and Trajectory Generator] D --> E[Mid-Level Controller
Impedance or Admittance Law] E --> F[Low-Level Controller
Current or Velocity Loop] F --> G[Actuator Bank
BLDC Motor or SEA or Pneumatic Muscle] G -->|Mechanical Power| H[Exoskeleton Frame
Aligned with Human Joints] H -->|Force and Motion| A D --> I[Therapist GUI
Progress Tracking FMA and MAS] I -->|Set Parameters: Md Bd Kd| D ``` ### 4.2 Control Hierarchy — From Intent to Actuation ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Patient Intent
Cortical or Peripheral] --> P2[Sensor Fusion
sEMG plus IMU] P2 --> P3[Task Layer
Reach or Grasp or Walk] P3 --> P4[Trajectory Layer
Minimum Jerk Path] P4 --> P5[Impedance Layer
Md, Bd, Kd Selection] P5 --> P6[Actuator Layer
Torque Command] P6 --> P7[Plant
Human plus Robot Coupled] P7 -->|Feedback| P2 ``` ### 4.3 Functional Subsystems of a Lower-Limb Gait Exoskeleton ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Mechanical M1[Hip Module] --> M2[Knee Module] --> M3[Ankle Module] end subgraph Actuation A1[Battery Pack] --> A2[BLDC Motor] --> A3[Harmonic Drive Reducer] --> A4[Series Elastic Element] end subgraph Sensing S1[Joint Encoders] --> S2[Load Cells at Cuff] --> S3[Foot Pressure Insole] --> S4[IMU on Trunk] end subgraph Control C1[State Machine
Stance plus Swing] --> C2[Finite State Machine
Gait Phase Detection] --> C3[Torque Modulator
Assist as Needed] end A4 --> M1 M1 --> S1 S1 --> C1 C1 --> C3 C3 --> A1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why a Finite State Machine (FSM)?** Human gait is inherently **discrete-phase** (heel strike, stance, toe-off, swing). An FSM with hysteresis between states provides robust, computationally cheap, and clinically interpretable control — the dominant approach in commercial exoskeletons like ReWalk and Ekso GT. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Distinguish between end-effector and exoskeleton-type rehabilitation robots. Give one clinical example of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | End-Effector Robot | Exoskeleton Robot | |---|---|---| | Contact | Single distal point (hand/foot) | Multiple joints, each aligned anatomically | | Mechanical alignment | Not required with anatomical joints | Must be coaxial with each joint axis | | Singularities | Within patient's passive workspace | Within robot's own kinematics | | Example | MIT-Manus (InMotion ARM) | ARMEO Power, Lokomat | End-effectors are simpler and easier to don but cannot isolate motion to a single joint. Exoskeletons enable joint-specific therapy and record isolated joint torques. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is a Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)? Why is it preferred in rehabilitation exoskeletons over a rigid actuator?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Series Elastic Actuator places a passive spring **in series** between the motor and the load. The transmitted force is computed from the spring deflection as $F = K_s (q_m - q_l)$ (1 mark). It is preferred because (i) it provides **intrinsic compliance**, protecting the patient from impact forces (1 mark), and (ii) it enables accurate **force control** since spring deflection is a low-noise proxy for output force, unlike estimating force from motor current in rigid actuators (1 mark). --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 3 Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently (a) confuse impedance control with admittance control, (b) omit the Jacobian transpose when mapping Cartesian forces to joint torques, and (c) forget to mention **safety compliance** in clinical systems — losing 2 to 3 marks per question. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the closed-loop architecture of a robotic gait-training exoskeleton. Explain the role of the high-level task planner, mid-level impedance controller, and low-level current controller. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the impedance control law $M_d \ddot{x}_e + B_d \dot{x}_e + K_d x_e = F_{ext}$ and show how the commanded joint torque $\tau_{ctrl}$ is computed using the Jacobian transpose. Discuss how the therapist adjusts $M_d$, $B_d$, and $K_d$ to suit patients at different stages of recovery. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** The closed-loop architecture consists of four interacting layers: 1. **Patient Sensing Layer:** Surface EMG electrodes detect residual muscle activity; IMUs (trunk + thigh + shank) detect segmental orientation; foot pressure insoles detect ground contact; encoders at each actuated joint measure joint angle. **(1 mark)** 2. **High-Level Task Planner:** Generates the desired reference trajectory $x_d(t)$ for the gait cycle. The cycle is divided into **stance phase (60%)** and **swing phase (40%)** of the gait period, and a finite state machine with hysteresis selects the appropriate sub-trajectory. The therapist can adjust step length, cadence, and body-weight support via the GUI. **(2 marks)** 3. **Mid-Level Impedance Controller:** Implements the desired mechanical impedance. It receives the position error $x_e = x - x_d$ and the measured interaction force $F_{ext}$ and computes the desired Cartesian acceleration: $$ \ddot{x}_{cmd} = \ddot{x}_d + \dfrac{1}{M_d}\left[F_{ext} - B_d \dot{x}_e - K_d x_e\right] $$ **(2 marks)** 4. **Low-Level Current Controller:** A PI/PID loop regulates motor current at a bandwidth of 1–5 kHz, providing the inner torque-control loop. Harmonic drive reducers step down the motor speed while stepping up the torque. **(1 mark)** Safety layer: An independent **watchdog circuit** monitors current limits, joint limits, and EMG-based reflex signals (e.g., sudden spastic burst), and can cut off power within 5 ms. **(1 mark)** **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Derivation (5 marks):** Step 1 — Define the desired Cartesian impedance behavior: $$ M_d \ddot{x}_e + B_d \dot{x}_e + K_d x_e = F_{ext} $$ Step 2 — Substitute $x_e = x - x_d$ and expand each derivative: $$ M_d(\ddot{x} - \ddot{x}_d) + B_d(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) + K_d(x - x_d) = F_{ext} $$ Step 3 — Solve for the commanded acceleration: $$ \ddot{x} = \ddot{x}_d + \dfrac{F_{ext} - B_d \dot{x}_e - K_d x_e}{M_d} $$ Step 4 — Map to joint space using the Jacobian $J(q)$ (relating $\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$ and $\ddot{x} = J(q)\ddot{q} + \dot{J}(q)\dot{q}$): $$ \ddot{q}_{cmd} = J^{-1}(q)\left[\ddot{x} - \dot{J}(q)\dot{q}\right] $$ Step 5 — Apply computed-torque control law with gravity and Coriolis compensation, and the Jacobian transpose for force mapping: $$ \tau_{ctrl} = M(q)\ddot{q}_{cmd} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) - J(q)^T F_{ext} $$ **Clinical Tuning Strategy (2 marks):** | Recovery Stage | $M_d$ | $B_d$ | $K_d$ | Patient Experience | |---|---|---|---|---| | Acute (Flaccid) | Low | High | **Zero** | "Floating" — robot carries limb weight | | Sub-acute (Spastic) | Medium | High | Low | "Guided" — high damping suppresses reflexes | | Chronic (Recovering) | High | Medium | Medium-High | "Resistive" — robot challenges the patient | The therapist begins with **zero stiffness and high damping** (transparent, "zero-impedance" mode) so the patient is not intimidated, then progressively increases $K_d$ to provide error-correcting assistance as motor recovery occurs. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the four major actuation technologies used in rehabilitation robots. Compare their force density, bandwidth, and suitability for upper-limb versus lower-limb applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Series Elastic Actuator has motor position $q_m = 1.05$ rad, load position $q_l = 1.00$ rad, and spring stiffness $K_s = 500$ N·m/rad. Compute (i) the transmitted torque, (ii) the energy stored in the spring, and (iii) the maximum safe patient interaction force if the maximum allowable spring deflection is 0.2 rad. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** | Actuation Type | Force Density | Bandwidth | Best Suited For | Limitation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Electric (BLDC + Harmonic Drive)** | Low–Medium (~50 N·m/kg) | High (>100 Hz) | Upper-limb precise tasks | Limited peak torque | | **Pneumatic (McKibben)** | High (~200 N/kg) | Medium (5–20 Hz) | Ankle/soft-assist devices | Compressor noise, non-linear | | **Hydraulic** | Very High (~500 N/kg) | High (50–100 Hz) | Lower-limb heavy-duty (BLEEX) | Leaks, bulky, maintenance | | **SMA (Shape Memory Alloy)** | Very Low (cycle limited) | Very Low (<2 Hz) | Research/lightweight orthotics | Slow cooling, fatigue | **Upper-limb preference (4 marks):** Electric BLDC motors with harmonic drives are dominant because upper-limb joints require low torque (typically < 20 N·m) but high bandwidth and precision. Examples: InMotion ARM, ARMEO Power. **Lower-limb preference (3 marks):** Hip and knee joints during gait can require peak torques of 60–100 N·m. Hydraulic systems dominate here (BLEEX, Raytheon XOS), though modern electric SEAs (Lokomat, Ekso) have closed the gap for clinical settings. **Model Solution — Part (b):** **(i) Transmitted torque (2 marks):** $$ \tau = K_s (q_m - q_l) = 500 \times (1.05 - 1.00) = 500 \times 0.05 = 25 \text{ N·m} $$ **(ii) Energy stored in the spring (2 marks):** $$ U = \dfrac{1}{2} K_s (\Delta q)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 500 \times (0.05)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 500 \times 0.0025 = 0.625 \text{ J} $$ **(iii) Maximum safe patient interaction force (3 marks):** $$ F_{max} = K_s \times \Delta q_{max} = 500 \times 0.2 = 100 \text{ N} $$ **Safety interpretation (1 mark):** This 100 N limit must be compared with the **Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC)** of the targeted muscle group. For the elbow flexors, MVC is typically 200–300 N, so the 100 N SEA limit provides a safety margin of ~2.5–3× against involuntary muscle forces, satisfying the **ISO 13482** standard for personal care robots. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Rehabilitation Robot vs. Exoskeleton:** A rehabilitation robot is the broader category (therapeutic + assistive); an exoskeleton specifically refers to a *wearable* structure with joint-by-joint alignment. - **Two architectures:** End-effector (simple, distal contact) vs. Exoskeleton (complex, anatomically aligned). - **SEA formula:** $\tau = K_s (q_m - q_l)$ — must be memorized for any actuator-related numerical. - **Impedance control law:** $M_d \ddot{x}_e + B_d \dot{x}_e + K_d x_e = F_{ext}$ — the cornerstone equation of pHRI. - **Computed-torque mapping:** $\tau_{ctrl} = M(q)\ddot{q}_{cmd} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) - J(q)^T F_{ext}$. - **Gait phase split:** Stance 60%, Swing 40%; FSM with hysteresis is the industry-standard control approach. - **Clinical scales:** Fugl-Meyer (FMA, max 66/34) for motor recovery; Modified Ashworth Scale (MAS, 0–4) for spasticity. - **Safety standard:** IEC 60601-1 for active medical devices; ISO 13482 for personal care robots. - **Sensors to recall:** Encoder, force/torque load cell, sEMG, IMU, foot pressure insole. - **Critical damping rule:** $0.7 \leq \zeta \leq 1.0$ for safe physical human-robot interaction. - **Common commercial devices (know 1 from each category):** Upper-limb — InMotion ARM; Lower-limb gait — Lokomat; Wearable — ReWalk.

topics.title: Radiotherapy Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT502 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Radiotherapy Equipment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Radiotherapy** is the medical discipline of treating malignant neoplasms (cancers) and certain benign conditions through the controlled application of **ionizing radiation** to localized tissue volumes, delivering a precisely calculated dose sufficient to cause irreparable DNA damage (primarily double-strand breaks) in tumor cells while sparing adjacent healthy structures within the limits defined by the **ALARA principle** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) and **ICRU Report 50/62** target volume definitions. The **Radiotherapy Equipment** is the umbrella term for all electromechanical, radiological, and computational hardware used to generate, shape, monitor, and deliver therapeutic radiation beams, classified broadly into: 1. **Teletherapy (External Beam) Equipment** – Source at a distance from the patient. 2. **Brachytherapy (Internal/Sealed-Source) Equipment** – Source placed inside or adjacent to the target. 3. **Treatment Planning Systems (TPS)** – The computational backbone (3D-CRT, IMRT, VMAT, SBRT). 4. **Dosimetry & Quality Assurance Hardware** – Phantoms, ionization chambers, film, EPIDs. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Sniper Rifle vs. Shotgun" Analogy** > Imagine a tumor is a single rotten apple inside a closed wooden crate. A **shotgun** (older, low-precision radiation like orthovoltage) sprays pellets everywhere — the apple is destroyed, but so is the crate. A **sniper rifle** (modern linear accelerator with MLC, IGRT, VMAT) takes carefully aimed, sculpted shots, damaging only the apple. Radiotherapy has evolved from the shotgun to the sniper rifle, and the "radiotherapy equipment" is the rifle, scope, ammunition, and sighting system combined into one machine. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Radiotherapy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)** > - **Dose unit:** Gray (Gy) = 1 J/kg of absorbed energy (**SI unit, replaced the older rad where 1 Gy = 100 rad**). > - **Activity unit:** Becquerel (Bq) = 1 disintegration/second (**replaced the Curie, 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq**). > - **Equivalent dose:** Sievert (Sv) = Gy × W_R (radiation weighting factor). > - **Exposure unit:** Coulomb/kilogram (C/kg) (**replaced the Roentgen, R: 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg**). > - **Hounsfield Unit (HU):** CT number scale used for treatment planning electron density mapping. > - **Mean photon energy of Co-60:** **1.25 MeV** (average of 1.17 and 1.33 MeV gamma lines). > - **Mean photon energy of LINAC 6 MV:** ~2 MeV average; **6 MV** = nominal accelerating potential. ## 1.4 Classification of Radiotherapy Modalities | Modality | Source Type | Energy Range | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Superficial X-ray (Contact Therapy)** | X-ray tube | 50–100 kVp | Skin lesions, depth < 5 mm | | **Orthovoltage (Deep Therapy)** | X-ray tube | 200–500 kVp | Superficial & shallow tumors | | **Cobalt-60 Teletherapy** | Radioisotope | 1.25 MeV γ | Mid-range (legacy workhorse) | | **LINAC – Low Energy** | Electron linac | 4–6 MV photons | Head, neck, breast | | **LINAC – High Energy** | Electron linac | ≥ 10 MV (15, 18, 23 MV) | Pelvic, abdominal, deep-seated | | **Gamma Knife** | 201 Co-60 sources | 1.25 MeV γ | Intracranial SRS | | **CyberKnife** | 6 MV LINAC on robot | 6 MV | SRS/SBRT extracranial | | **Brachytherapy – LDR** | Ir-192, I-125 seeds | < 2 Gy/h | Prostate, eye plaques | | **Brachytherapy – HDR** | Ir-192 afterloading | > 12 Gy/h | Gyn, breast, lung, esophagus | | **Proton Therapy** | Cyclotron/Synchrotron | 70–250 MeV | Pediatric, chordoma, ocular | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Physical Basis of Radiotherapy The goal is to deposit a **lethal dose** in the PTV (Planning Target Volume) while keeping the dose to OARs (Organs at Risk) below tolerance. Radiation damages cells through: - **Direct DNA damage** (high-LET radiation: α particles, neutrons, carbon ions) - **Indirect damage via free radicals** (low-LET: photons, electrons — dominant in standard therapy) > [!NOTE] > Photons and electrons are **low-LET (Linear Energy Transfer ~ 0.2–2 keV/μm)** radiation, while protons and α particles are **high-LET (50–200 keV/μm)**. Higher LET = more lethal per unit dose but harder to control. ## 2.2 The Photon Beam Interaction Chain (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Photon enters tissue** → interacts via Photoelectric effect, Compton scattering, or Pair production. 2. **Secondary electrons (δ-rays) are set in motion** along the photon track. 3. **These electrons deposit dose** as they traverse nanometer-to-micrometer distances. 4. **Dose builds up** until electronic equilibrium is reached (depth ≈ d_max). 5. **Beyond d_max, dose attenuates** roughly exponentially with depth. 6. **Off-axis dose** comes from scattered photons (collimator scatter + phantom scatter). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Build-up region**: Photons create high-energy secondary electrons that travel *forward*. Therefore, dose is **low at the surface** and peaks at **d_max** (1.5 cm for 6 MV; 3–4 cm for 18 MV). This is a *therapeutic advantage*: spares skin for high-energy beams. ## 2.3 Cobalt-60 Teletherapy Unit — Block Architecture > [!NOTE] > The Cobalt-60 unit was the workhorse of radiotherapy from the 1950s–1990s and is still used in many developing nations due to its **mechanical reliability** and **low cost** compared to a LINAC. **Key Physics Properties of Co-60:** - Half-life: **5.27 years** (must replace source every 4–5 years). - Decays to Ni-60, emitting **β⁻ (0.31 MeV, absorbed in source capsule)** + **two γ photons: 1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV**. - Average γ energy: **1.25 MeV**. - **Specific activity**: ~300 Ci/g (for HDR source pellets). - Source-to-Skin Distance (SSD): typically **80 cm**. - Beam is **always on** (cannot be switched off — relies on source-on/source-off position in the head). ## 2.4 Linear Accelerator (LINAC) — Operating Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Medical Linear Accelerator (LINAC)** accelerates electrons using RF electromagnetic waves in a waveguide (traveling-wave or standing-wave structure) to kinetic energies of **4–25 MeV**, then either (a) strikes a high-Z target to produce a bremsstrahlung photon beam, or (b) uses the electrons directly for electron therapy. **Main Subsystems of a LINAC:** | Subsystem | Function | |---|---| | **Electron Gun** | Produces electrons via thermionic emission (heated cathode, ~6 V, pulsed). | | **Injector / Buncher** | Bunches electrons into micro-pulses and pre-accelerates to ~50 keV. | | **RF Power Source** | **Magnetron** (≤ 6 MV) or **Klystron** (> 6 MV) — produces 3 GHz (S-band) or 10 GHz (X-band) microwaves. | | **Waveguide / Accelerating Tube** | Where electrons gain energy from the traveling RF wave. | | **Bending Magnet** | 90° or 270° achromatic bend; directs the beam horizontally. | | **Target (X-ray mode)** | Tungsten or gold (high Z, high melting point) for bremsstrahlung. | | **Flattening Filter** | Cone-shaped brass/aluminum to even out the central-axis intensity. | | **Primary Collimator** | Defines the maximum circular field (~50 cm diameter at isocenter). | | **Secondary Collimators (Jaws)** | Two pairs of motor-driven lead blocks form rectangular fields. | | **MLC (Multi-Leaf Collimator)** | 40–160 individually driven tungsten leaves for irregular field shaping. | | **Gantry** | Rotates 360° around isocenter for multi-angle delivery. | | **Treatment Couch** | 6 DoF (linear, rotational) with indexable carbon-fiber top. | ## 2.5 Brachytherapy Equipment > [!NOTE] > Brachytherapy places a sealed radioactive source **inside or immediately adjacent to the tumor**. The dose falls off rapidly with distance (∝ 1/r²), giving an excellent therapeutic ratio for accessible tumors. **Modern Brachytherapy = Remote Afterloading:** - Source is stored in a **safe** inside the machine; transferred pneumatically or via cable through applicators to the patient. - Eliminates staff radiation exposure. **Two main types of afterloaders:** - **LDR (Low Dose Rate):** 0.4–2 Gy/h. Classic manual or pulsed devices. Use Ir-192, I-125, Pd-103, Cs-131. - **HDR (High Dose Rate):** > 12 Gy/h. Modern stepping-source Ir-192 afterloaders (e.g., GammaMed, Flexisource, VariSource). A single tiny **Ir-192 source (~0.6 mm × 3.5 mm)** is stepped through dwell positions in catheters. **Ir-192 key data:** Half-life **73.83 days**, average photon energy **0.38 MeV**, maximum ~ 0.6 MeV. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (MUST MEMORIZE) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are listed in the high-yield KTU cheat sheet. They are evaluated for derivations and numerical problems. $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{(1) Inverse Square Law:} \quad I &\propto \frac{1}{d^2} \\[4pt] \textbf{(2) Percentage Depth Dose (PDD):} \quad \text{PDD}(d) &= \frac{D(d)}{D_{d_{max}}} \times 100\% \\[4pt] \textbf{(3) Tissue-Air Ratio (TAR):} \quad \text{TAR}(d) &= \frac{D_d}{D_{d_{air}}} \\[4pt] \textbf{(4) Tissue-Phantom Ratio (TPR):} \quad \text{TPR}(d) &= \frac{D_d}{D_{d_{ref}}} \\[4pt] \textbf{(5) Peak Scatter Factor (PSF):} \quad \text{PSF} &= \frac{D_{d_{max},\,field}}{D_{d_{max},\,10 \times 10}} \\[4pt] \textbf{(6) Output Factor (OF):} \quad \text{OF}(A) &= \frac{\text{Dose at } d_{max}, A}{\text{Dose at } d_{max}, 10 \times 10} \\[4pt] \textbf{(7) Monitor Unit (MU) Calculation (SSD setup):} \quad \text{MU} &= \frac{\text{Prescribed Dose} \times 100}{\text{Calibration Dose} \times \text{PDD}(d) \times \text{OF}(A) \times (\text{SSD} + d_{max})^2 / (\text{SSD} + d)^2} \\[4pt] \textbf{(8) Linear-Quadratic (LQ) Cell Survival:} \quad S &= e^{-\alpha D - \beta D^2} \\[4pt] \textbf{(9) Half-Life:} \quad N(t) &= N_0 e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} \\[4pt] \textbf{(10) BED (Biologically Effective Dose):} \quad \text{BED} &= D\left(1 + \frac{d}{\alpha/\beta}\right) \\[4pt] \textbf{(11) Equivalent Square Field (F):} \quad F &= \frac{2 \cdot a \cdot b}{a + b} \\[4pt] \textbf{(12) Gamma Knife Helmet Geometry:} \quad N_{collimators} &= 4, \text{ beam diameter } 4, 8, 14, 18 \, \text{mm} \\[4pt] \textbf{(13) Exposure Rate Constant of Co-60:} \quad \Gamma &= 0.325 \, \text{R·m}^2/(\text{Ci·h}) \end{aligned} $$ | Symbol | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---| | $D$ | Absorbed dose | Gy | | $d$ | Depth in tissue | cm | | $d_{max}$ | Depth of maximum dose | cm | | $A$ | Field area at isocenter | cm² or cm × cm | | $\alpha, \beta$ | LQ model radiosensitivity constants | Gy⁻¹, Gy⁻² | | $\lambda$ | Decay constant | s⁻¹ or h⁻¹ | | $T_{1/2}$ | Half-life | s, days, years | | $\alpha/\beta$ | LQ ratio (low ≈ 3 Gy for late-responding tissue, high ≈ 10 Gy for early/tumor) | Gy | | $\Gamma$ | Exposure rate constant | R·m²/(Ci·h) | > [!WARNING] > Do not confuse **SSD (Source-Surface Distance)** setup (used for Cobalt and some LINACs) with **SAD (Source-Axis Distance)** setup (used for modern isocentric LINACs). The MU formula differs accordingly. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Radiotherapy physics** drives multi-disciplinary engineering: RF/microwave engineering (LINAC), precision mechatronics (gantry + MLC + couch), software algorithms (TPS inverse optimization for IMRT/VMAT), image processing (CBCT, EPID), and radiation dosimetry. - The **multi-leaf collimator** is a masterpiece of precision mechanics — 0.5–1 mm positional accuracy at 0.5 m travel, with 50–160 leaves each controlled by its own motor. - The **Gamma Knife and CyberKnife** systems are textbook examples of mechatronic + image-guided therapy. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Percentage Depth Dose (PDD) and the Mayneord F-Factor PDD is defined for an **SSD setup** (source fixed, patient surface at fixed distance, depth varies): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PDD}(d, A, f) &= \frac{D_d}{D_{d_{max}}} \times 100\% \\[6pt] &= \frac{D(d,\,A,\,f)}{D(d_{max},\,A,\,f)} \times 100\% \end{aligned} $$ Where $A$ is the field area at the surface and $f$ is the SSD. **Why does PDD increase with field size $A$?** At larger field sizes, more **scattered radiation** from the irradiated volume contributes to the dose at depth $d$, while $d_{max}$ has slightly less proportional gain (saturated scatter). Hence the ratio $\frac{D_d}{D_{d_{max}}}$ rises with $A$. **The Mayneord F-Factor** (used to convert PDD from one SSD to another): $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \left[\frac{f + d_{max}}{f + d}\right]^2 \\[6pt] \text{PDD}_2(d, f_2) &= \text{PDD}_1(d, f_1) \times F \times \frac{1}{\text{PSF correction}} \quad (\text{often approximated for Co-60}) \end{aligned} $$ **Derivation of F:** Mayneord assumed the dose at any point varies as $\frac{1}{(f+d)^2}$ (inverse square). So: $$ \begin{aligned} D_1(d) &= \frac{K}{(f_1 + d)^2} \cdot \text{(scatter term)} \\[4pt] D_2(d) &= \frac{K}{(f_2 + d)^2} \cdot \text{(scatter term)} \\[4pt] \Rightarrow \frac{D_2(d)}{D_1(d)} &= \left(\frac{f_1 + d}{f_2 + d}\right)^2 \end{aligned} $$ For PDD normalization to the same $d_{max}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PDD}_2(d, f_2) &= \text{PDD}_1(d, f_1) \times \left[\frac{f_1 + d}{f_2 + d}\right]^2 \times \left[\frac{f_2 + d_{max}}{f_1 + d_{max}}\right]^2 \\[6pt] \Rightarrow F &= \left[\frac{f_2 + d_{max}}{f_2 + d} \cdot \frac{f_1 + d}{f_1 + d_{max}}\right]^2 \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem:** A Co-60 unit (SSD = 80 cm) gives a dose of 1.40 Gy at $d_{max}$ for 100 MU with a $10 \times 10$ cm² field. Given PDD at 10 cm depth for $10 \times 10$ cm² = 58.5%, calculate the MU required to deliver 2.0 Gy to a tumor at 10 cm depth with a $15 \times 15$ cm² field. (Assume no off-axis change for the same SSD, use Mayneord F for SSD = 100 cm and ignore PSF correction.) **Step 1 — Given Data** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SSD} &= 80 \text{ cm}, \quad \text{PDD}_{10}(10 \times 10) = 58.5\% \\[4pt] \text{Calibration} &= 1.40 \text{ Gy per 100 MU at } d_{max} \text{ for } 10 \times 10 \\[4pt] \text{Prescribed dose} &= 2.0 \text{ Gy at } d = 10 \text{ cm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply MU Formula (SSD setup)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MU} &= \frac{D_{Rx} \times 100}{D_{cal} \times \text{PDD} \times \text{OF} \times F} \end{aligned} $$ For the same SSD (80 cm), $F = 1$ and OF for $10 \times 10$ is 1.000. For the **$15 \times 15$ cm² field at 10 cm depth**, $\text{PDD}_{10}(15 \times 15)$ is higher. From standard Co-60 data: $\text{PDD}_{10}(15 \times 15) \approx 63.0\%$. **Step 3 — Equivalent Square** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{eq} &= \frac{2 \cdot a \cdot b}{a + b} = \frac{2 \cdot 15 \cdot 15}{15 + 15} = 15 \text{ cm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute MU** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MU} &= \frac{2.0 \times 100}{1.40 \times 0.630 \times 1.000 \times 1} \\[4pt] &= \frac{200}{0.882} \\[4pt] &= 226.76 \text{ MU} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $\approx 227$ monitor units. ## 3.3 Derivation: Tissue-Air Ratio (TAR) → Tissue-Phantom Ratio (TPR) The TAR was introduced by Johns (1953) for isocentric (SAD) calculations: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TAR}(d, A_d) &= \frac{D_d(\text{in phantom})}{D_{d_{air}}(\text{same field at } d_{air})} \end{aligned} $$ Where $D_{d_{air}}$ is the dose measured in air at the same isocenter with the same field $A_d$ (the field at depth, not at surface). **Disadvantage of TAR:** Measuring $D_{d_{air}}$ for large field sizes is impractical. Hence **TPR** was introduced: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TPR}(d, A_d) &= \frac{D_d(\text{at depth } d)}{D_{d_{ref}}(\text{at depth } d_{ref})} \end{aligned} $$ Usually $d_{ref} = 10$ cm, with $A_{ref} = 10 \times 10$ cm². Both measurements in phantom. **Relationship:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TPR}(d, A_d) &= \frac{\text{TAR}(d, A_d)}{\text{TAR}(d_{ref}, A_{ref})} \\[6pt] \text{PSF}(A) &= \frac{\text{TAR}(d_{max}, A)}{\text{TAR}(d_{max}, 10 \times 10)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Linear-Quadratic (LQ) Model of Cell Survival The LQ model describes the surviving fraction $S$ of cells after a single dose $D$: $$ \begin{aligned} S(D) &= e^{-\alpha D - \beta D^2} \\[6pt] \ln S &= -\alpha D - \beta D^2 \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $\alpha$ (Gy⁻¹) = probability of a single lethal hit (linear component, dominant at low doses). - $\beta$ (Gy⁻²) = probability of two separate sub-lethal hits combining (quadratic, dominant at high doses). **The $\alpha/\beta$ ratio** determines the **shape of the survival curve**: - High $\alpha/\beta$ (~ 10 Gy) → early-responding tissue and most tumors → nearly linear curve. - Low $\alpha/\beta$ (~ 3 Gy) → late-responding tissue (spinal cord, kidney, liver) → very curvy (more sparing with fractionation). **Biologically Effective Dose (BED):** $$ \begin{aligned} E &= n \cdot d \cdot \left(1 + \frac{d}{\alpha/\beta}\right) \quad \text{(total biological effect)} \\[4pt] \text{BED} &= E / \alpha = n \cdot d \cdot \left(1 + \frac{d}{\alpha/\beta}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Where $n$ = number of fractions, $d$ = dose per fraction. The fractionation term $\left(1 + \frac{d}{\alpha/\beta}\right)$ explains why **smaller doses per fraction spare late-responding tissue more than tumors** — the foundation of modern fractionated radiotherapy. ## 3.5 Derivation: Gamma Knife Beam Geometry The Gamma Knife (Elekta) has **201 Co-60 sources** arranged on a hemispherical shell of radius ~ 40 cm. Each source has its own collimator helmet with one of four apertures: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Available beam diameters at isocenter} &= \{4, 8, 14, 18\} \text{ mm} \\[4pt] \text{Total number of beams} &= 201 \\[4pt] \text{Total activity (typical)} &= 6000 \text{ Ci} \ (2.22 \times 10^{14} \text{ Bq}) \end{aligned} $$ Dose rate at isocenter (modern Perfexion/Icon): $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{D}_{iso} &\approx 3 \text{ Gy/min (with 4 mm collimators, full helmet)} \end{aligned} $$ **Key insight:** All 201 beams converge at the isocenter → high dose concentration; off-axis dose falls off by the superposition of 201 inverse-square + attenuation terms, producing a near-spherical dose distribution with sub-millimeter accuracy (0.1–0.3 mm mechanical accuracy). ## 3.6 Numerical Example: Cobalt-60 Decay and Output Correction > **Problem:** A Cobalt-60 source was calibrated to deliver 1.50 Gy/min at $d_{max}$ for a 10×10 cm² field at SSD = 80 cm. What is the dose rate 4 years later? ($T_{1/2} = 5.27$ years) $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{D}(t) &= \dot{D}_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \\[4pt] \lambda &= \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{5.27} = 0.1315 \text{ yr}^{-1} \\[4pt] \dot{D}(4) &= 1.50 \cdot e^{-0.1315 \times 4} = 1.50 \cdot e^{-0.526} \\[4pt] &= 1.50 \times 0.591 = 0.8865 \text{ Gy/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Equivalent calculation using "halving"**: $4/5.27 = 0.759$ half-lives, so remaining fraction $= 2^{-0.759} = 0.591$, giving the same answer. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **Define Percentage Depth Dose (PDD) and mention two factors on which it depends.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **PDD** is the ratio of absorbed dose at a given depth $d$ in tissue to the absorbed dose at the depth of maximum dose $d_{max}$, expressed as a percentage: > $$\text{PDD}(d) = \frac{D(d)}{D(d_{max})} \times 100\%$$ > It depends on: **(1) Depth of measurement $d$** (PDD decreases with depth due to attenuation and inverse square), **(2) Field size $A$** (PDD increases with field area due to increased scatter contribution), and **(3) SSD** (PDD increases with SSD due to reduced divergence effect on the depth dose ratio). **(2 × 1 = 2 marks for two factors, 1 mark for formula).** ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **List the main subsystems of a medical linear accelerator and state the function of the bending magnet.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Main subsystems: (i) Electron gun, (ii) Injector, (iii) RF power source (magnetron/klystron), (iv) Accelerating waveguide, (v) Bending magnet, (vi) Target/scattering foil, (vii) Flattening filter, (viii) Primary and secondary collimators, (ix) MLC, (x) Monitor chambers, (xi) Gantry and treatment couch, (xii) Control console. > **Function of the bending magnet**: To bend the accelerated electron beam by 90° (or 270°) from its horizontal trajectory inside the waveguide into a vertical beam directed downward toward the patient, while also acting as an **energy slit** to filter out electrons of undesired energies (achromatic bend) ensuring a mono-energetic beam at isocenter. **(2 marks for subsystems list, 1 mark for bending magnet function).** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — 7 marks** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Cobalt-60 teletherapy unit. Mention the salient physical properties of the Co-60 source.** > **Model Answer:** > > **Construction (block-wise — refer to Section 4.2 diagram):** > > 1. **Radioactive Source**: A sealed Co-60 pellet (typically cylindrical, ~ 1–2 cm diameter, double-encapsulated in stainless steel) with activity 1000–6000 Ci placed inside a source capsule. > 2. **Source Drawer / Positioner**: Pneumatic or motor-driven mechanism that moves the source between the **ON** (exposed) and **OFF** (shielded) positions within the head. > 3. **Source Housing / Head**: Constructed of high-density shielding material — typically ~ 20 cm of **depleted uranium** or lead — with a primary collimator opening directed toward the patient. > 4. **Primary Collimator**: A fixed lead cylinder defining the maximum circular field (~ 30 cm at 80 cm SSD). > 5. **Secondary Collimators (Jaws)**: Two pairs of adjustable lead blocks that produce rectangular fields. > 6. **Beam-Defining Light and Optical Distance Indicator (ODI)**: Mirrors project a light field simulating the radiation field; a movable scale measures SSD. > 7. **Treatment Couch**: Indexable, lockable couch with controls for translation and rotation. > 8. **Control Console**: Timer-based control with backup mechanical timer; displays cumulative treatment time. > > **Working Principle:** > Co-60 decays to Ni-60 via β⁻ emission, emitting two γ photons (1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV). The mean γ energy of **1.25 MeV** is used for therapy. The collimated beam exits the head, traverses the SSD of typically 80 cm, and delivers dose to the patient. The beam cannot be switched off electronically; it is controlled by retracting the source into the safe. > > **Salient physical properties (1 mark):** > - Half-life: 5.27 years > - Mean γ energy: 1.25 MeV > - Specific activity: ~ 300 Ci/g > - Decay mode: β⁻ + γ > - Daughter product: Ni-60 (stable) > - Exposure rate constant Γ = 0.325 R·m²/(Ci·h) > > **(Distribution: 3 marks diagram, 2 marks construction, 1 mark working, 1 mark properties).** #### **Part (b) — 7 marks** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **A Co-60 source has a half-life of 5.27 years. The output was 1.60 Gy/min at calibration. Calculate the expected output after 3 years. If the same machine is to deliver 2.0 Gy to a depth of 8 cm (PDD₈ for 10×10 cm² field = 60.0%), calculate the treatment time in minutes.** > **Model Answer:** > > **Step 1 — Decay after 3 years** > $$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{5.27} = 0.1315 \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ > $$\dot{D}(3) = 1.60 \times e^{-0.1315 \times 3} = 1.60 \times e^{-0.3945}$$ > $$= 1.60 \times 0.6743 = 1.079 \text{ Gy/min}$$ > > **Step 2 — Dose rate at 8 cm depth** > $$D_{rate,8} = D_{rate,d_{max}} \times \text{PDD} = 1.079 \times 0.600 = 0.6474 \text{ Gy/min}$$ > > **Step 3 — Treatment time for 2.0 Gy** > $$t = \frac{2.0}{0.6474} = 3.09 \text{ minutes} \approx 185 \text{ seconds}$$ > > **(2 marks for decay calculation, 3 marks for PDD application, 2 marks for final time — total 7 marks).** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [ALTERNATIVE] #### **Part (a) — 7 marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the construction and working of a medical linear accelerator with a neat block diagram. Compare it with a Cobalt-60 unit on at least four points.** > **Model Answer:** > > **Construction & Working (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):** > 1. **Electron Gun**: Diode-type with indirectly heated cathode emitting electrons via thermionic emission. > 2. **Injector / Buncher**: Pre-accelerates electrons to ~ 50 keV and bunches them into micro-pulses synchronized with the RF. > 3. **RF Power Source**: A **Magnetron** (for ≤ 6 MV) or **Klystron** (for > 6 MV) generates 3 GHz (S-band) microwaves pulsed at 100–300 Hz, each pulse ~ 1–5 μs long, with peak power 1–5 MW. > 4. **Modulator**: A pulse-forming network (PFN) and thyratron switch produce the high-voltage pulses that power both the gun and the RF source. > 5. **Accelerating Waveguide**: A cylindrical copper structure (traveling-wave or standing-wave) where the electrons ride the crest of the RF wave and gain energy up to 4–25 MeV. > 6. **Bending Magnet**: 90° or 270° achromatic bend redirects the beam vertically; energy slits at the bend filter out off-energy electrons. > 7. **Treatment Head**: Contains the X-ray target (tungsten), flattening filter, primary collimator, secondary jaws, MLC, and transmission ionization chambers (monitor chambers). > 8. **Gantry and Couch**: Isocentric rotation about a single point (isocenter) at SAD = 100 cm. > 9. **Control Console**: Computer-controlled V&V system with interlocks. > > **Comparison with Co-60 (any four, 1 mark each):** > > | Parameter | LINAC | Co-60 | > |---|---|---| > | Beam energy | Variable, multi-energy (4–25 MV) | Fixed 1.25 MeV | > | Beam ON/OFF | Electronic (RF off) | Mechanical source drawer | > | Penumbra | Sharper (focused source) | Wider (large physical source ~ 1.5–2 cm) | > | Dose rate | 2–6 Gy/min | ~ 1.5–2.5 Gy/min | > | Source replacement | None (electrical) | Every 4–5 years | > | Skin sparing | Good (high d_max) | Limited (d_max = 0.5 cm) | > | Cost | High (5–10 × Co-60) | Low | > | Maintenance | Complex (RF, vacuum) | Simple | > > **(3 marks diagram, 2 marks explanation, 2 marks comparison).** #### **Part (b) — 7 marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Derive the Percentage Depth Dose (PDD) Mayneord F-factor used for SSD correction, and apply it to compute the new PDD at 10 cm depth when SSD is changed from 80 cm to 100 cm for a 6 MV photon beam. Given: PDD₁₀(80 cm SSD, 10×10 cm²) = 67.0%, d_max = 1.5 cm. Ignore PSF correction.** > **Model Answer:** > > **Derivation (3 marks):** > The dose at depth $d$ for an SSD = $f$ varies by the inverse square law: > $$D(d, f) \propto \frac{1}{(f + d)^2}$$ > > For PDD, we take the ratio to dose at $d_{max}$: > $$\text{PDD}(d, f) = \frac{D(d, f)}{D(d_{max}, f)} \times 100\%$$ > > For a change of SSD from $f_1$ to $f_2$ (assuming scattered dose is approximately constant for the same field size at the same depth in phantom): > $$\text{PDD}_2(d) = \text{PDD}_1(d) \times \left[\frac{f_1 + d}{f_2 + d}\right]^2 \times \left[\frac{f_2 + d_{max}}{f_1 + d_{max}}\right]^2$$ > $$\text{PDD}_2(d) = \text{PDD}_1(d) \times F$$ > > Where $F = \left(\frac{f_2 + d_{max}}{f_2 + d} \cdot \frac{f_1 + d}{f_1 + d_{max}}\right)^2$ > > **Numerical Application (4 marks):** > Given: $f_1 = 80$ cm, $f_2 = 100$ cm, $d = 10$ cm, $d_{max} = 1.5$ cm, $\text{PDD}_1 = 67.0\%$. > > $$F = \left(\frac{100 + 1.5}{100 + 10} \cdot \frac{80 + 10}{80 + 1.5}\right)^2$$ > $$= \left(\frac{101.5}{110} \cdot \frac{90}{81.5}\right)^2$$ > $$= \left(0.9227 \times 1.1043\right)^2 = (1.0189)^2 = 1.0382$$ > > $$\text{PDD}_2(10) = 67.0\% \times 1.0382 = 69.56\% \approx 69.6\%$$ > > **Physical Interpretation**: Increasing the SSD from 80 cm to 100 cm increases the PDD at depth because the beam divergence has a *relatively* smaller effect at greater depth when normalized to $d_{max}$ — a major advantage of SAD/extended-SSD treatment. > > **(3 marks derivation, 2 marks substitution, 1 mark interpretation, 1 mark final answer — total 7).** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Key):** > 1. **Confusing SSD with SAD**: When asked for MU calculation, students often use the wrong formula. SSD setup → use PDD; SAD setup → use TPR or TAR. > 2. **Forgetting $d_{max}$ normalization**: In PDD problems, students divide by surface dose instead of $D_{d_{max}}$ and lose 1–2 marks. > 3. **Unit errors in MU calculation**: Always express PDD as a decimal (0.585), not a percentage (58.5), in the denominator. > 4. **Inverse square law sign error**: Students sometimes write $(f + d)^2$ instead of $\frac{1}{(f+d)^2}$ — read the question direction carefully. > 5. **Mayneord F-factor squared**: The F-factor itself is the ratio of inverse-square terms **squared**; missing one square is a frequent error. > 6. **Decay constant unit mismatch**: For Co-60, if half-life is in days, $\lambda$ must be in 1/day. Convert consistently. > 7. **Not labeling the block diagram**: In LINAC and Co-60 diagram questions, missing the function of one or two blocks costs 1 mark each. > 8. **α/β ratio confusion**: $\alpha/\beta \approx 10$ for tumors and early-responding tissue; $\alpha/\beta \approx 3$ for late-responding tissue. Wrong assignment → BED calculation error. > 9. **LDR vs. HDR brachytherapy**: Mixing up dose rates (LDR 0.4–2 Gy/h, HDR > 12 Gy/h) and typical isotopes (HDR = Ir-192, LDR seeds = I-125 / Pd-103). > 10. **Build-up region / skin sparing**: Students often forget that Co-60 has $d_{max} = 0.5$ cm (poor skin sparing) while 6 MV has $d_{max} = 1.5$ cm and 18 MV has $d_{max} = 3$ cm. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Radiotherapy Equipment (Module 3)** > > ✅ **Definitions**: PDD, TAR, TPR, PSF, BED, BED, SAD, SSD, GTV, CTV, PTV, OAR, ICRU, ALARA. > > ✅ **Co-60**: $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ yr, $E_\gamma = 1.25$ MeV, $\Gamma = 0.325$ R·m²/(Ci·h), SSD = 80 cm, $d_{max} = 0.5$ cm, replaced every 4–5 yr. > > ✅ **Ir-192**: $T_{1/2} = 73.83$ days, $E_\gamma^{avg} = 0.38$ MeV, used in HDR afterloaders. > > ✅ **LINAC parts**: gun → injector → waveguide (S-band 3 GHz) → bending magnet → target (W) → flattening filter → jaws → MLC → monitor chamber → isocenter (SAD = 100 cm). > > ✅ **RF sources**: Magnetron (≤ 6 MV) vs. Klystron (> 6 MV); peak power 1–5 MW. > > ✅ **d_max values**: 6 MV = 1.5 cm; 10 MV = 2.5 cm; 18 MV = 3.0–3.5 cm; Co-60 = 0.5 cm. > > ✅ **Equivalent square field**: $F = \frac{2ab}{a+b}$. > > ✅ **Mayneord F-factor**: $F = \left[\frac{f_2 + d_{max}}{f_2 + d} \cdot \frac{f_1 + d}{f_1 + d_{max}}\right]^2$. > > ✅ **Inverse square law**: $D \propto 1/d^2$. > > ✅ **LQ model**: $S = e^{-\alpha D - \beta D^2}$, BED $= nd(1 + d/(\alpha/\beta))$. > > ✅ **Gamma Knife**: 201 Co-60 sources, 4–18 mm collimators, sub-mm accuracy. > > ✅ **CyberKnife**: 6 MV LINAC on robotic arm, 6 DoF correction. > > ✅ **Brachytherapy types**: LDR (0.4–2 Gy/h) vs. HDR (> 12 Gy/h); modern afterloaders step an Ir-192 source through dwell positions. > > ✅ **Treatment modalities** (chronological): 2D → 3D-CRT → IMRT → IGRT → VMAT (RapidArc) → SBRT/SRS → Adaptive RT → MR-LINAC. > > ✅ **Dose units**: 1 Gy = 100 rad; 1 Sv = 100 rem; 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq; 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg. > > ✅ **Quality assurance**: Daily (output constancy), weekly (mechanical), monthly (full QA per IAEA TRS-430), annually (full dosimetry audit).

topics.title: Principles of Radiotherapy and Types of Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT502 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Radiotherapy and Types of Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Radiotherapy?

**Radiotherapy (Radiation Therapy)** is a clinical discipline of oncology that uses carefully controlled **ionizing radiation** to destroy or damage malignant (cancerous) cells, while preserving the structural and functional integrity of surrounding healthy tissues. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, radiotherapy sits at the intersection of **radiation physics, radiobiology, and biomedical engineering**, where engineering students focus primarily on the *equipment, dose delivery mechanics, and radiation-matter interaction principles*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (ICRU / ICRP Convention):**
> Radiotherapy is the *therapeutic application of ionizing radiation*, wherein a prescribed **Absorbed Dose (D, measured in Gray, Gy)** is delivered to a defined **Planning Target Volume (PTV)** with the explicit goal of achieving **Tumour Control Probability (TCP)** while keeping the **Normal Tissue Complication Probability (NTCP)** below an acceptable clinical threshold (typically < 5%).

## 1.2 The Central Engineering Problem: The Therapeutic Ratio

The fundamental engineering challenge of radiotherapy is balancing two competing outcomes. We use a metric called the **Therapeutic Ratio (TR)**:

$$TR = \frac{\text{Tumour Control Probability (TCP)}}{\text{Normal Tissue Complication Probability (NTCP)}}$$

A higher TR means a better treatment. The entire history of radiotherapy engineering is essentially the story of *how to push this ratio upward* through better machines, better beam-shaping, and better fractionation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "Precision Sun-Bathing for a Tumour"

Imagine you are a farmer trying to kill a single weed (the tumour) growing among valuable crops (healthy tissue) using a magnifying glass and sunlight. You cannot aim at the weed *only*, so you have two strategies:

1. **Use a low-intensity beam for a long time** — this gives the crops time to "duck" (repair DNA), but the weed is also relatively unharmed. *(This is like poorly fractionated, low-LET radiation.)*
2. **Use a high-intensity, tightly focused beam** that overwhelms even repairing tissue, with sharp edges so neighbouring crops are spared. *(This is like modern IMRT, VMAT, or proton therapy.)*

The "magnifying glass" in modern radiotherapy is the **Linear Accelerator (LINAC)**, the **Gamma Knife**, or the **Cyclotron** — each chosen based on the depth, size, and radio-resistance of the tumour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
> - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - Decay constant of $^{60}\text{Co}$: $\lambda \approx 0.1316$ /year
> - Half-life of $^{60}\text{Co}$: $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ **years**
> - Half-life of $^{137}\text{Cs}$: $T_{1/2} = 30.17$ **years**
> - Standard exposure rate constant of $^{60}\text{Co}$: $\Gamma \approx 0.306$ R·cm²/(mCi·h)

## 1.4 Why Radiation Damages Cells (The 30-Second Version)

When ionizing radiation passes through a cell, it deposits energy along its track. This energy:

1. **Ionizes water molecules** in the cytoplasm, producing **free radicals** (especially the hydroxyl radical $\text{OH}^{\bullet}$).
2. These radicals attack the **DNA**, causing **single-strand breaks (SSB)** and the far more lethal **double-strand breaks (DSB)**.
3. If the cell cannot repair the DSB before attempting division, it undergoes **mitotic catastrophe** → cell death.

The reason tumours are preferentially killed is that *cancer cells are usually defective in DNA repair checkpoints* (a hallmark of cancer), so they cannot recover as well as normal cells — a phenomenon exploited via **fractionation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Radiobiological "4 R's" — Why We Fractionate

Fractionation (delivering the total dose in many small daily doses, e.g., 2 Gy × 30 fractions) is a deliberate engineering decision based on four radiobiological phenomena, all mnemonic-stored as the **"4 R's of Radiobiology"** (originally Hall, later extended by Steel):

### 2.1.1 Repair (of Sublethal Damage)
- **Why it matters:** Healthy cells have a robust DNA repair machinery (homologous recombination, non-homologous end joining). When dose is split, healthy tissue has *time to repair* between fractions.
- **Engineering implication:** Lower dose-per-fraction spares late-responding normal tissue more than tumour (which has low α/β). The fractionation sensitivity is governed by the **linear-quadratic (LQ) model**.

### 2.1.2 Repopulation
- **Why it matters:** Both tumour and normal cells divide during the weeks of treatment. Clonogens (cancer stem cells) that survive an early fraction can *proliferate* before the next dose.
- **Engineering implication:** Treatment must not be unduly prolonged — the "**weekend gap**" is clinically known to reduce local control for fast-growing tumours.

### 2.1.3 Reoxygenation
- **Why it matters:** Hypoxic tumour cells are **2–3 times more radio-resistant** than well-oxygenated ones (the **Oxygen Enhancement Ratio, OER ≈ 2.5–3.0** for low-LET radiation). As outer tumour cells die after a fraction, oxygen diffuses inward, *re-oxygenating* the previously hypoxic core.
- **Engineering implication:** Fractionation *exploits* reoxygenation by giving hypoxic cells time to become oxygenated before the next dose.

### 2.1.4 Redistribution (Cell-Cycle Re-assortment)
- **Why it matters:** Cells are most radiosensitive in **G2/M phase** and most resistant in **S phase**. After a dose, surviving cells are partially synchronized; by the next fraction, they have moved into more sensitive phases.

## 2.2 The Linear-Quadratic (LQ) Model

The LQ model is the *mathematical backbone* of modern fractionation calculations. The **surviving fraction (SF)** of cells after a single dose $D$ is:

$$SF = e^{-\left(\alpha D + \beta D^{2}\right)}$$

- $\alpha$ = linear component (single-hit, irreparable damage)
- $\beta$ = quadratic component (two-hit, repairable damage)
- $\alpha / \beta$ ratio (in **Gy**) determines the tissue's fractionation sensitivity:
  - **Late-responding normal tissue:** $\alpha / \beta \approx 3$ Gy (sensitive to large fractions)
  - **Early-responding tissue & most tumours:** $\alpha / \beta \approx 10$ Gy

## 2.3 Types of Ionizing Radiation Used in Radiotherapy

Radiation is classified by *what it is* and *how densely it deposits energy*.

### 2.3.1 Electromagnetic (Photon) Radiation
| Type | Source | Energy Range | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Superficial X-rays** | X-ray tube (50–150 kVp) | 50–150 keV | Skin cancers (< 5 mm depth) |
| **Orthovoltage X-rays** | X-ray tube (200–500 kVp) | 200–500 keV | Historical, superseded |
| **Megavoltage X-rays** | LINAC | 4–25 MV | Deep seated tumours (most common) |
| **Gamma rays** | $^{60}\text{Co}$, $^{137}\text{Cs}$ | 1.17, 1.33 MeV | Teletherapy, brachytherapy |

### 2.3.2 Particulate Radiation
| Type | Source | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrons** | LINAC (4–25 MeV) | Superficial tumours, skin, chest wall |
| **Protons** | Cyclotron/Synchrotron (70–250 MeV) | Paediatric, base of skull, ocular, prostate |
| **Neutrons** | Cyclotron (d-T, 14 MeV) | Salivary gland, radio-resistant tumours |
| **Heavy ions (Carbon)** | Synchrotron (100–400 MeV/u) | Hypofractionation, radio-resistant |
| **Beta emitters** | $^{90}\text{Sr}$, $^{192}\text{Ir}$ | Brachytherapy, intravascular |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LET — Linear Energy Transfer** (energy deposited per unit track length, in **keV/µm**):
> - **Low-LET** (X-rays, $\gamma$, electrons): LET $\approx 0.2$–$2$ keV/µm → requires $\text{O}_2$ to be effective (OER ≈ 2.5–3)
> - **High-LET** (neutrons, $\alpha$, heavy ions): LET $\approx 50$–$200$ keV/µm → *oxygen-independent*, more lethal per track, less repairable

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inverse Square Law** | $I = \dfrac{I_{0}}{(d/d_{0})^{2}} = \dfrac{\Gamma \cdot A}{d^{2}}$ | X-ray intensity / exposure rate | $d$ = distance from source, $A$ = activity, $\Gamma$ = exposure rate constant |
| 2 | **Activity** | $A = \lambda N$ | Becquerel (Bq) = 1 dps | $N$ = number of atoms, $\lambda$ = decay constant |
| 3 | **Decay Law** | $N(t) = N_{0}\, e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless ratio | Exponential form |
| 4 | **Decay Law (half-life form)** | $A(t) = A_{0}\, (1/2)^{t / T_{1/2}}$ | Bq, Ci | Engineering form |
| 5 | **Decay Constant** | $\lambda = \dfrac{0.693}{T_{1/2}}$ | 1/time | Numerical value |
| 6 | **Specific Gamma Ray Constant** | $\Gamma_{\delta}$ | R·cm²/(mCi·h) or Gy·m²/(GBq·h) | Depends on radionuclide |
| 7 | **Half-Value Layer (HVL)** | $I = I_{0}\, (1/2)^{n}$ | dimensionless | $n$ = number of HVLs |
| 8 | **Mean Life** | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{T_{1/2}}{0.693}$ | time | Average lifetime of a radioactive atom |
| 9 | **Percentage Depth Dose (PDD)** | $PDD(d) = \dfrac{D(d)}{D(d_{max})} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Standard beam data at 10 cm depth for $^{60}\text{Co}$ ≈ 58% |
| 10 | **Tissue-Air Ratio (TAR)** | $TAR(d) = \dfrac{D(d)_{tissue}}{D(d)_{air}}$ | dimensionless | Field-size dependent |
| 11 | **Linear-Quadratic SF** | $SF = e^{-(\alpha D + \beta D^{2})}$ | dimensionless | $\alpha/\beta$ ratio in Gy |
| 12 | **BED (Biologically Effective Dose)** | $BED = nd\left[1 + \dfrac{d}{\alpha/\beta}\right]$ | Gy | $n$ = # fractions, $d$ = dose/fraction |
| 13 | **Equivalent Dose** | $H_{T} = \sum_{R} w_{R} \cdot D_{T,R}$ | Sievert (Sv) | $w_R$ = radiation weighting factor |
| 14 | **Effective Dose** | $E = \sum_{T} w_{T} \cdot H_{T}$ | Sievert (Sv) | $w_T$ = tissue weighting factor |
| 15 | **SSD to SAD conversion** | $PDD_{SAD} = PDD_{SSD} \cdot \left[\dfrac{SSD + d_{max}}{SAD}\right]^{2} \cdot \left[\dfrac{SAD}{SSD + d}\right]^{2}$ | % | Mayers' formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - The **Inverse Square Law** governs every **SSD set-up** in cobalt teletherapy; a 1 cm error at 80 cm SSD = 2.5% dose error — *within ICRU tolerance*.
> - The **LQ model** is used by the **clinic's Treatment Planning System (TPS)** to compute BED when comparing different fractionation schedules (e.g., 60 Gy in 30 fractions vs 70 Gy in 28 fractions).
> - The **PDD curve** is the master beam dataset that every TPS imports for dose calculation.

## 2.5 External Beam vs Brachytherapy — The Two Branches

| Parameter | **External Beam (EBRT)** | **Brachytherapy** |
|---|---|---|
| Source location | Outside the patient | Inside / on the tumour |
| Distance from source to target | 80–100 cm (SSD/SAD) | 0.5–5 cm |
| Dose distribution | Conformal, uniform, megavoltage | Rapid fall-off, very high local dose |
| Dose rate | 1–10 Gy/min | LDR: 0.4–2 Gy/h; HDR: > 12 Gy/h |
| Sources used | LINAC X-rays, electrons, protons; $^{60}\text{Co}$ | $^{192}\text{Ir}$, $^{137}\text{Cs}$, $^{125}\text{I}$, $^{103}\text{Pd}$ |
| Radiobiology | Fractionation possible | Usually single exposure (LDR permanent seeds) |
| Anaesthesia / surgery | None | Often requires applicator placement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3, you must be able to:
> 1. Define radiotherapy and state its goal.
> 2. List and explain the **4 R's of radiobiology**.
> 3. Compare **brachytherapy** vs **teletherapy** with examples.
> 4. State the energy, half-life, and clinical use of **$^{60}\text{Co}$, $^{137}\text{Cs}$, $^{192}\text{Ir}$**.
> 5. Write the **inverse square law** and the **radioactive decay law**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Radioactive Decay Law

### 3.1.1 Starting Postulate
The rate of radioactive decay of a population of $N$ unstable atoms is *directly proportional to the number of atoms present* at that instant. This is a first-order differential equation:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$

The negative sign indicates the population is **decreasing** with time.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Rearrange the equation to separate variables:**

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides from the initial state $(t = 0, N = N_0)$ to a general time $(t, N)$:**

$$\int_{N_{0}}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$\Big[\ln N\Big]_{N_{0}}^{N} = -\lambda \Big[t\Big]_{0}^{t}$$

$$\ln N - \ln N_{0} = -\lambda t$$

**Step 4 — Combine the logarithms and exponentiate:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N}{N_{0}}\right) = -\lambda t$$

$$\frac{N}{N_{0}} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 5 — Final exponential form of the decay law:**

$$N(t) = N_{0} \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 6 — Since Activity $A = \lambda N$ is also proportional to $N$:**

$$A(t) = A_{0} \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to half-life form using $N(T_{1/2}) = N_0/2$:**

$$e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda T_{1/2} = \ln 2 = 0.693$$

Therefore the **decay constant in terms of half-life** is:

$$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute this $\lambda$ back into the exponential:**

$$A(t) = A_{0} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t / T_{1/2}}$$

This form is far more useful in the clinic because we can directly plug in years and half-lives without computing $\lambda$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Use:** A 1000-Ci cobalt source loses 1.1% of its output per month. Every cobalt unit has a "**output correction table**" (timed in months) that the physicist updates quarterly — *this equation is what generates that table*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Inverse Square Law for a Point Source

### 3.2.1 Starting Assumption
Radiation from a *point source* spreads out radially in straight lines. The total *fluence* (number of photons per unit area) is conserved across a sphere of any radius $r$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — The number of photons passing through a sphere of radius $r$ per second is the source's emission rate $S$ (photons/s):**

$$\text{Total photons per second} = S = \text{constant for all } r$$

**Step 2 — The photon fluence rate (fluence per unit area) at radius $r$:**

$$\Phi(r) = \frac{S}{\text{Area of sphere of radius } r} = \frac{S}{4 \pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Since photon energy $E$ is the same at all $r$, the energy fluence rate (and hence the dose rate, in air) scales as:**

$$\dot{D}(r) \;\propto\; \frac{1}{r^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio form used clinically (the *inverse square law*):**

$$\frac{\dot{D}(r_{1})}{\dot{D}(r_{2})} = \left(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Adding the source strength explicitly (with the specific gamma ray constant $\Gamma$):**

$$\dot{X}(r) = \frac{\Gamma \cdot A}{r^{2}}$$

where
- $\dot{X}$ = exposure rate (R/h or Gy/h)
- $\Gamma$ = specific gamma ray constant (R·cm²/mCi·h)
- $A$ = source activity (mCi or GBq)
- $r$ = distance (cm or m)

**Step 6 — Worked example (KTU standard style):**
> A $^{60}\text{Co}$ source has $A = 5000$ Ci. What is the exposure rate at 80 cm SSD?
> - $\Gamma_{^{60}\text{Co}} = 0.306$ R·cm²/(mCi·h)
> - $A$ in mCi $= 5000 \times 10^{3} = 5 \times 10^{6}$ mCi
> - $r = 80$ cm
> 
> $$\dot{X} = \frac{0.306 \times 5 \times 10^{6}}{(80)^{2}} = \frac{1.53 \times 10^{6}}{6400} \approx 239.1 \text{ R/h}$$
> 
> Converting (1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg; for air, W/e ≈ 33.97 J/C):
> $$\dot{D}_{air} \approx 0.876 \times \dot{X}_{\text{R/h}} \text{ cGy/min} \approx 2.10 \text{ Gy/min}$$
> This matches the *measured* output of a clinical cobalt unit (~1.5–2.5 Gy/min at 80 cm SSD).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent and Effective Dose

The *same physical absorbed dose* can produce different *biological effects* depending on the type of radiation. We correct for this using weighting factors.

**Step 1 — Define Equivalent Dose in tissue T:**

$$H_{T} = \sum_{R} w_{R} \cdot D_{T,R}$$

where
- $D_{T,R}$ = average absorbed dose in tissue T from radiation type R
- $w_R$ = radiation weighting factor (ICRP 103 values)

| Radiation type | $w_R$ |
|---|---|
| Photons (X, $\gamma$), electrons | 1 |
| Protons | 2 |
| Alpha particles, fission fragments | 20 |
| Neutrons (energy-dependent) | 5–20 |

**Step 2 — Define Effective Dose (whole body):**

$$E = \sum_{T} w_{T} \cdot H_{T}$$

where $w_T$ is the tissue weighting factor (e.g., 0.12 for breast, 0.08 for gonads).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering relevance:** Effective Dose is what radiation protection regulations quote. KTU students should know that **dose limits for occupational workers = 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years**, and **public = 1 mSv/year** (ICRP / AERB India).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: BED (Biologically Effective Dose) — Proof

**Step 1 — Start from the LQ model for a single dose $d$:**

$$SF = e^{-(\alpha d + \beta d^{2})}$$

**Step 2 — For $n$ fractions with complete repair between fractions, surviving fraction is:**

$$SF_{n} = \left[e^{-(\alpha d + \beta d^{2})}\right]^{n} = e^{-n(\alpha d + \beta d^{2})}$$

**Step 3 — Total biological "effect" is the argument of the exponent, $E = n(\alpha d + \beta d^{2})$. Factor out $\alpha d$:**

$$E = n d \left(\alpha + \beta d\right) = \alpha \cdot n d \left[1 + \frac{d}{\alpha/\beta}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Define BED as $E / \alpha$ (the dose that would produce this effect with infinitely small fractions):**

$$BED = n d \left[1 + \frac{d}{\alpha/\beta}\right]$$

This is the formula used by radiation oncologists to compare prescriptions like 60 Gy/30 fractions vs 50 Gy/20 fractions vs 70 Gy/35 fractions *on a common biological scale*.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Radiation-Tissue Interaction Map

| Property | Photons (X, γ) | Electrons | Protons | Neutrons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nature | Electromagnetic wave | Charged (–) particle | Charged (+) particle | Neutral hadron |
| Penetration | Deep (build-up region) | Limited (~$\,E_{MeV}/3$ cm) | Finite range (Bragg peak) | Very deep |
| LET | 0.2–2 keV/µm | 0.2–2 keV/µm | ~5 keV/µm (entrance) → 100 keV/µm (peak) | 10–100 keV/µm |
| OER | 2.5–3.0 | 2.5–3.0 | 2.0–2.5 | 1.3–1.7 |
| RBE | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.1–1.2 (entrance) → 3–4 (peak) | 3–10 |
| Skin sparing | Yes (high energy) | No (entrance dose high) | Yes (low entrance dose) | Yes |
| Source | LINAC, $^{60}\text{Co}$ | LINAC | Cyclotron | Cyclotron (d–T) |
| Cost | Low | Low | Very high | Very high |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Radiotherapy Treatment Chain

This Mermaid flow diagram represents the entire radiotherapy workflow from patient referral to follow-up, with engineering-relevant equipment stages highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Referral and Diagnosis] --> B[Imaging CT MRI PET]
    B --> C[Target Delineation PTV OAR]
    C --> D[Treatment Planning System TPS]
    D --> E[Plan QA and Dose Verification]
    E --> F[Machine Calibration and Output Check]
    F --> G[Patient Positioning and Immobilization]
    G --> H[Linear Accelerator or Co 60 Unit]
    H --> I[In vivo Dosimetry]
    I --> J[Treatment Delivery Fractionated]
    J --> K[Follow up Imaging and Response Assessment]
    K --> L{Response}
    L --> M[Complete Response]
    L --> N[Partial Response Replan]
    L --> O[Progression Palliative RT]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Stages **D, E, F, H** are the *engineering hardware/software* blocks.
- Stage **J** is where the **fractionation** (4 R's) plays out — typically 5 fractions/week × 6–7 weeks.
- The decision diamond at **L** is the *radiobiological outcome check* (TCP vs NTCP observed).

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Radiation Used in Therapy

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Ionizing Radiation in Radiotherapy] --> B[Electromagnetic Photons]
    A --> C[Particulate Radiation]
    B --> B1[X rays Superficial 50 to 150 kVp]
    B --> B2[X rays Orthovoltage 200 to 500 kVp]
    B --> B3[X rays Megavoltage 4 to 25 MV]
    B --> B4[Gamma rays Co 60 Cs 137]
    C --> C1[Electrons 4 to 25 MeV]
    C --> C2[Protons 70 to 250 MeV]
    C --> C3[Neutrons 14 MeV d T]
    C --> C4[Heavy Ions Carbon 100 to 400 MeV per u]
    C --> C5[Brachytherapy Sources Ir 192 I 125 Pd 103 Sr 90]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: The Radiobiological Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Radiation Photon or Particle] --> P2[Energy Deposition in Tissue]
    P2 --> P3[Water Radiolysis OH dot H dot e aq]
    P3 --> P4[DNA Damage SSB DSB Base Damage]
    P4 --> P5[Cell Cycle Checkpoint Activation]
```
