```mermaid
    P5 --> P6{DNA Repairable}
    P6 --> P7[Surviving Cell with Repair]
    P6 --> P8[Cell Death Mitotic Catastrophe Apoptosis]
    P7 --> P9[4 R Repair Repopulation Reoxygenation Redistribution]
    P9 --> P10[Next Fraction]
    P8 --> P11[Tumour Control]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Dose Distribution Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Photon Beam X ray 6 MV] --> A1[Entrance Dose High, Exit Dose Significant]
    B[Electron Beam 12 MeV] --> B1[Entrance Dose High, Rapid Falloff after d max]
    C[Proton Beam 150 MeV] --> C1[Low Entrance Dose, Sharp Bragg Peak, Zero Exit Dose]
    D[Neutron Beam 14 MeV] --> D1[High Entrance Dose, OER Near 1, RBE 3 to 5]
    A1 --> A2[Deep Tumour Plus Critical Organ Beyond]
    B1 --> B2[Superficial Tumour Less than 5 cm]
    C1 --> C2[Deep Tumour Near Critical Structure]
    D1 --> D2[Radio resistant Salivary Gland Sarcoma]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Take-away from the diagrams:**
> - **Photon beams** deposit a *maximum dose* at the **d_max** (≈ 1.5 cm for 6 MV), then decrease exponentially — this is why photons spare the skin ("skin-sparing effect") but also irradiate tissue *behind* the tumour.
> - **Proton beams** have a *Bragg peak*: almost zero dose beyond the peak. This is the engineering "holy grail" of dose conformity, especially for paediatric tumours where exit dose is unacceptable.
> - **Neutron beams** are used sparingly because of their high entrance dose and high RBE — reserved for radio-resistant tumours.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define radiotherapy. State the goal of radiotherapy and explain the term "therapeutic ratio".**

**Model Answer:**
Radiotherapy is the clinical use of ionizing radiation for the treatment of malignant diseases (cancers) and a few benign conditions. The goal is to deliver a tumouricidal absorbed dose to the planning target volume (PTV) while keeping the dose to surrounding normal tissues below their tolerance limits. The **therapeutic ratio (TR)** is defined as the ratio of the **Tumour Control Probability (TCP)** to the **Normal Tissue Complication Probability (NTCP)**: $TR = \dfrac{TCP}{NTCP}$. A higher TR means a more effective and safer treatment; modern conformal techniques like IMRT, VMAT, and proton therapy aim to maximize TR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "TR = TCP − NTCP" (difference) instead of "TR = TCP / NTCP" (ratio). This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**List the four R's of radiobiology. Explain any two briefly.**

**Model Answer:**
The four R's of radiobiology are:
1. **Repair** of sublethal DNA damage.
2. **Repopulation** of surviving clonogens.
3. **Reoxygenation** of hypoxic tumour cells.
4. **Redistribution** (cell-cycle re-assortment) of surviving cells.

**Repair:** Normal cells have efficient DNA repair enzymes (HR, NHEJ). Splitting the total dose into small daily fractions (typically 1.8–2 Gy) gives normal tissue time to repair, sparing late-responding normal tissue. **Reoxygenation:** Hypoxic cells are 2.5–3 times more radio-resistant than oxygenated cells (high OER). After a dose kills the outer well-oxygenated cells, oxygen diffuses into the previously hypoxic core, *re-sensitizing* them for the next fraction. This is the principal reason fractionation works for solid tumours.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing "**Repopulation** of cells" is too vague. Always clarify: *clonogenic proliferation* (stem-like cancer cells dividing during the course of treatment).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]
**(a) Explain the principle of radiotherapy. Describe the various types of radiation used in radiotherapy with suitable examples.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A cobalt-60 teletherapy unit has an initial activity of 5000 Ci. Compute (i) the activity after 5 years, (ii) the exposure rate at 80 cm SSD, and (iii) the time required for the exposure rate to fall to 50 R/h. Given $\Gamma_{Co-60} = 0.306$ R·cm²/(mCi·h) and $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ years.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a):
**Principle of Radiotherapy:** Radiotherapy works on the principle that *ionizing radiation* deposits energy in living tissue, ionizing water molecules to produce free radicals (especially $\text{OH}^{\bullet}$). These radicals cause DNA damage (single- and double-strand breaks). Cells with intact DNA-repair machinery (most normal tissue) can recover; cells lacking proper checkpoints (cancer cells) undergo mitotic death. Fractionation exploits this differential by allowing normal cells to repair between doses.

**Types of Radiation Used:**

| Type | Examples | Energy / Source | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low-LET photons | X-rays, $\gamma$ rays | 50 keV – 25 MV; LINAC, $^{60}\text{Co}$ | Most curative EBRT |
| Electrons | Beta particles | 4–25 MeV; LINAC | Skin, chest wall, scar boost |
| Protons | Charged heavy particle | 70–250 MeV; Cyclotron | Paediatric, base of skull |
| Neutrons | Neutral hadron | 14 MeV (d–T) | Salivary gland tumours |
| Heavy ions | $^{12}\text{C}^{6+}$ | 100–400 MeV/u | Hypofractionation, radio-resistant |
| Brachytherapy seeds | $^{192}\text{Ir}$, $^{125}\text{I}$ | 20–400 keV | Gyn, prostate, breast |

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** $A_0 = 5000$ Ci, $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ years, $\Gamma = 0.306$ R·cm²/(mCi·h), $d = 80$ cm.

**(i) Activity after 5 years:**

$$A(5) = 5000 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{5/5.27}$$

Compute the exponent: $5/5.27 = 0.9488$. Then $(1/2)^{0.9488} = e^{-0.9488 \times 0.693} = e^{-0.6575} = 0.518$.

$$A(5) = 5000 \times 0.518 = 2590 \text{ Ci}$$

**(ii) Exposure rate at 80 cm SSD:**

Convert $A_0$ to mCi: $5000 \text{ Ci} = 5000 \times 10^{3} = 5 \times 10^{6}$ mCi.

$$\dot{X} = \frac{\Gamma \cdot A}{d^{2}} = \frac{0.306 \times 5 \times 10^{6}}{(80)^{2}} = \frac{1.53 \times 10^{6}}{6400}$$

$$\dot{X} \approx 239.06 \text{ R/h}$$

**(iii) Time to reach 50 R/h from 239.06 R/h:**

We know $\dot{X} \propto A$, so:

$$\frac{\dot{X}(t)}{\dot{X}_{0}} = \frac{50}{239.06} = 0.2091 = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t/5.27}$$

Take log base 2: $t/5.27 = \log_2(0.2091) = \frac{\ln(0.2091)}{\ln(0.5)} = \frac{-1.5645}{-0.6931} = 2.257$.

$$t = 2.257 \times 5.27 \approx 11.9 \text{ years}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Calculation Part):** Many students convert Ci to Bq incorrectly (1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq). If the formula used is in SI units, you must use **GBq** in the numerator, **meters** in the denominator, and **Gy·m²/(GBq·h)** for $\Gamma$. Mixing Ci with meters will cost 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a) Compare external beam radiotherapy (teletherapy) and brachytherapy in terms of source placement, dose rate, dose distribution, and clinical examples.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Derive the radioactive decay law $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$. What is the mean life of a radioactive source? Find the mean life of $^{60}\text{Co}$ given $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ years.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a):

| Parameter | **Teletherapy (EBRT)** | **Brachytherapy** |
|---|---|---|
| Source location | At a distance, outside the patient | Inside or on the tumour (contact therapy) |
| Distance SSD / SAD | 80–100 cm | 0.5–5 cm |
| Dose rate | 1–10 Gy/min (typically 2–6 Gy/min) | LDR: 0.4–2 Gy/h; HDR: > 12 Gy/h |
| Dose distribution | Conformal via MLC, wedges; uniform across PTV | Rapid fall-off (inverse square); conforms to implant geometry |
| Isodose shape | Spherical / cylindrical, modulated by MLC | Source-arrangement dependent (Manchester / Paris systems) |
| Patient comfort | Non-invasive, painless | Invasive, may need applicator / anaesthesia |
| Radiobiology | Conventional fractionation possible | Continuous low-dose-rate or fractionated HDR |
| Clinical examples | LINAC, $^{60}\text{Co}$ for most solid tumours | Gyn (cervix with $^{137}\text{Cs}$ tandem & ovoids), prostate ($^{125}\text{I}$ seeds), breast (HDR $^{192}\text{Ir}$ APBI) |

#### Model Solution (b):

**Derivation of the Decay Law:**

The decay rate of $N$ radioactive nuclei is directly proportional to $N$:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$

where $\lambda$ is the decay constant. Separating variables:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

Integrating from $(0, N_0)$ to $(t, N)$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln N - \ln N_0 = -\lambda t$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\lambda t$$

$$\boxed{N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}}$$

**Mean Life $\tau$:**

The mean (average) life of a radioactive atom is defined as:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{N_0} \int_0^{\infty} t \, |\dot{N}(t)| \, dt = \frac{1}{N_0} \int_0^{\infty} t \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

$$= \lambda \int_0^{\infty} t \, e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Since $\lambda = 0.693 / T_{1/2}$:

$$\tau = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693} = 1.443 \, T_{1/2}$$

For $^{60}\text{Co}$:

$$\tau_{Co-60} = 1.443 \times 5.27 \approx 7.6 \text{ years}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Derivation Part):** The most common mistake is forgetting the **negative sign** in $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$ and ending up with a *growing* population. Examiners will not award full marks for the integration step if the negative sign is missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Principles of Radiotherapy**

- [x] **Radiotherapy definition** = use of ionizing radiation to control malignant disease, optimizing TCP/NTCP.
- [x] **Therapeutic Ratio** $TR = \dfrac{TCP}{NTCP}$ — must be a *ratio*, not a difference.
- [x] **4 R's of Radiobiology:** Repair, Repopulation, Reoxygenation, Redistribution.
- [x] **Linear-Quadratic Model** $SF = e^{-(\alpha D + \beta D^{2})}$; $\alpha/\beta \approx 10$ Gy (tumour), 3 Gy (late normal).
- [x] **BED formula** $BED = nd\!\left[1 + \dfrac{d}{\alpha/\beta}\right]$ — for comparing fractionation schedules.
- [x] **Decay law** $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t} = N_0 (1/2)^{t/T_{1/2}}$; $\lambda = 0.693 / T_{1/2}$.
- [x] **Mean life** $\tau = T_{1/2} / 0.693 = 1.443 \, T_{1/2}$.
- [x] **Inverse square law** $\dot{X} = \Gamma A / d^{2}$; doubling distance → quarter dose.
- [x] **LET** = keV/µm — low-LET (photons, electrons) need O₂; high-LET (α, neutrons) don't.
- [x] **OER** ≈ 2.5–3 for photons, ≈ 1 for neutrons.
- [x] **Teletherapy** vs **Brachytherapy** — know 4+ distinguishing parameters.
- [x] **$^{60}\text{Co}$:** 1.17 + 1.33 MeV $\gamma$, $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ y, $\Gamma = 0.306$ R·cm²/(mCi·h).
- [x] **$^{137}\text{Cs}$:** 0.662 MeV $\gamma$, $T_{1/2} = 30.17$ y, used in LDR brachytherapy.
- [x] **$^{192}\text{Ir}$:** average 0.38 MeV, $T_{1/2} = 73.8$ days, used in HDR afterloading.
- [x] **PDD** = % of d_max dose at depth $d$ — beam quality descriptor.
- [x] **Equivalent Dose** $H_T = \sum w_R D$ (Sv); **Effective Dose** $E = \sum w_T H_T$ (Sv).
- [x] **Dose limits:** 20 mSv/yr (occupational), 1 mSv/yr (public) per ICRP.
- [x] **Bragg peak** of protons → zero exit dose, ideal for paediatric and base-of-skull tumours.
- [x] **Skin-sparing** by megavoltage photons (build-up region) — clinical advantage.
- [x] **Fractionation** spares late normal tissue more than tumour (because $\alpha/\beta$ is low).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the 4 R's:** **"RRRR — Radio-biology Really Requires Rigor"**
> **Mnemonic for radiation weighting factors:** **"Photons 1, Protons 2, Alpha 20"** — easy to remember, hard to confuse.

**End of Module 3 Notes — Principles of Radiotherapy and Types of Radiation.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Accelerators: Design and Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT502 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Accelerators: Design and Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **Linear Accelerator (LINAC)** is a radiotherapy device that accelerates charged subatomic particles — most commonly **electrons** — to relativistic velocities along a **straight (linear) trajectory** using time-varying high-frequency electromagnetic fields (typically in the S-band, ~**3 GHz** / 10 cm wavelength or X-band, ~9 GHz). The accelerated electron beam is then either (a) used directly for superficial tumour treatment, or (b) collided with a high-Z target (tungsten) to produce a high-energy bremsstrahlung **photon (X-ray) beam** in the megavoltage range (**4 MV to 25 MV**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorise:**
> *"A medical linear accelerator is a programmable, isocentric radiotherapy device that produces clinically usable megavoltage photon and electron beams by RF acceleration of electrons in a straight waveguide, followed by optional bending, beam shaping, and dose monitoring before delivery to the patient at a fixed Source-to-Axis Distance (SAD) of 100 cm."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surf-Wave Rider" Model

Imagine a surfer on the ocean:

| Concept | LINAC Component | Surfer Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Charged particle (electron) | The surfer | Standing on the board |
| Oscillating RF electric field | The wave moving shoreward | The ocean swell |
| Synchronous phase | Exact timing to "catch" the crest | The surfer's paddle timing |
| Accelerating waveguide | Length of ocean with successive crests | The travel path of the wave |
| Energy gained per gap | Height added to the wave | Each crest the surfer rides |
| Bending magnet | A wall that turns the surfer 90° toward shore | The harbour wall redirecting the surfer |
| Treatment head | The break-zone that shapes the wave | The shallows where the wave breaks into useable surf |

A surfer can only "catch" a wave if they paddle at the right moment — too early or too late, and they miss the energy transfer. Similarly, an electron can only gain energy from a **time-varying RF field** if it arrives at each accelerating gap at the **synchronous phase** of the oscillation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why S-Band (3 GHz)?** At ~10 cm wavelength, the cavity size is practical for clinical installations. Lower frequencies (L-band, ~1 GHz) require physically larger structures (3 m+); higher frequencies (X-band) have higher ohmic wall losses and tighter machining tolerances.

## 1.3 Clinical Operating Envelope (Standard KTU Values)

- **Photon energies:** **4 MV, 6 MV, 10 MV, 15 MV, 18 MV, 25 MV** (most common: **6 MV**)
- **Electron energies:** **4 MeV, 6 MeV, 9 MeV, 12 MeV, 15 MeV, 18 MeV, 20 MeV**
- **SAD (Source-to-Axis Distance):** **100 cm** (fixed isocentre)
- **SSD (Source-to-Surface Distance):** typically 80–100 cm
- **Dose rate at isocentre:** **100 to 600 MU/min** (1 MU ≈ 1 cGy at d_max for 10×10 cm²)
- **RF Frequency:** **S-band 2856 MHz** (industry standard for medical LINAC)
- **Field size:** **0 × 0 cm to 40 × 40 cm** at isocentre
- **Rest mass energy of electron:** $m_0c^2 = \mathbf{0.511\ MeV}$

> [!TIP]
> In KTU viva voce, the magic numbers you must know are: **2856 MHz**, **100 cm SAD**, **6 MV / 15 MV** as standard clinical energies, and **0.511 MeV** as the electron rest energy. Examiners will test these directly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Architecture (Top-Down View)

A medical LINAC is conventionally divided into **four major subsystems**:

1. **Modulator & RF Power Source** — generates high-voltage pulses and microwave RF.
2. **Electron Injection & Acceleration** — electron gun + buncher + accelerating waveguide.
3. **Beam Transport** — bending magnet(s), steering coils, focusing quadrupoles, energy slit.
4. **Treatment Head** — target, flattening filter, primary/secondary collimators, ion chambers, MLC, optical systems.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Stage A: High-Voltage Pulse Generation (Modulator)

- The **pulse-forming network (PFN)** is charged to a high DC voltage (~**10–25 kV**).
- A **thyratron switch** (or modern solid-state IGBT/MOSFET) is triggered to discharge the PFN into two loads:
  - The **magnetron / klystron** (to produce the RF pulse).
  - The **electron gun** (to inject a short burst of electrons synchronized with the RF).
- Pulse duration: typically **1–5 µs**, repetition rate: **50–400 Hz**.

### Stage B: RF Generation (Magnetron vs Klystron)

| Property | Magnetron | Klystron |
|---|---|---|
| Function | RF **oscillator** (self-excited) | RF **amplifier** (driven by low-power RF source) |
| Output power | 1.5 – 5 MW peak | 5 – 7 MW peak |
| Frequency stability | Lower (AFC needed) | Higher (driven) |
| Lifetime | ~2000–5000 hours | ~10,000+ hours |
| Use case | Low/medium energy LINACs (≤ 10 MV) | High-energy LINACs (> 10 MV), when frequency stability is critical |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnetron Principle (quick recall):** A cylindrical cathode surrounded by a multi-cavity anode in a strong axial magnetic field. Electrons emitted thermionically travel in **spoked orbits** past the anode cavities, inducing and sustaining RF oscillations in the resonant cavities. Energy is extracted via a coupling loop into the waveguide.

### Stage C: Electron Injection & Bunching

- The **electron gun** is a triode/grid-controlled structure with a heated tungsten cathode (work function $\phi \approx 4.5$ eV).
- Thermionic emission follows **Richardson's equation**: $J = A_R T^2 \exp(-W/kT)$.
- A short electron pulse (~1 µs) is injected into the **buncher cavity**.
- The buncher is a single RF gap operated at a velocity-modulating phase. Fast electrons arrive late, slow electrons arrive early — they cluster into **bunches** of width ~10–20 ps, each aligned with the peak accelerating phase.

### Stage D: Acceleration in the Waveguide

Two competing designs exist:

**Travelling-Wave (TW) Structure:**
- A cylindrical waveguide loaded with **irises** (discs with central apertures).
- An RF wave of phase velocity $v_p \approx c$ propagates from input to output.
- Electrons ride the crest of the wave and gain energy continuously.
- Residual RF absorbed in a **ferrite load** at the end.
- Used in: older **Varian Clinac**, some Elekta designs.

**Standing-Wave (SW) Structure:**
- The waveguide is **short-circuited** at both ends → forms a resonant cavity.
- RF bounces back and forth, creating a **standing wave** pattern.
- Side-coupled or on-axis coupled cells provide efficient energy transfer.
- ~30% shorter than TW for the same energy → cheaper, more compact.
- Used in: **modern Varian (TrueBeam)**, Elekta, Siemens designs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam trap:** Do NOT say "standing wave does not move." In an SW accelerator, the field pattern is stationary, but the electrons themselves are still moving! The field oscillates *in time* at every point; what does not move is the *spatial pattern* of maxima and minima.

### Stage E: Beam Bending (Achromatic Bending Magnet)

In modern isocentric LINACs, the accelerator waveguide is mounted either:
- **Vertically (straight-through design)** — used in some low-energy units.
- **Horizontally with a 90° or 270° bending magnet** — the **slalom** design used in modern Varian TrueBeam, Elekta Versa HD, etc.

The bending magnet performs **three functions simultaneously**:
1. **Energy selection:** only electrons of the correct momentum follow the bending radius $r = p/(qB)$.
2. **Direction change:** redirects the beam downward through the treatment head to the isocentre.
3. **Achromatic focusing:** the magnet's pole-face angles (entry/exit angles) provide **double-focusing** (in both transverse planes) so the beam spot is small and parallel at the target.

### Stage F: Treatment Head (Photon Mode)

Travelling from target to patient:

1. **Primary Collimator** — fixed tungsten alloy block with a divergent conical opening defining the maximum circular field (~50 cm diameter at isocentre).
2. **Target** — high-Z (tungsten, $Z = 74$, thickness 1–2 mm) for photon production via bremsstrahlung.
3. **Flattening Filter** — conical copper/aluminium/steel filter that compensates for the forward-peaked angular distribution of bremsstrahlung, producing a **flat dose profile** across the field.
4. **Dose Monitor Ion Chambers** — two independent transmission ion chambers (one for dose, one for redundancy/symmetry) — terminate the beam if they detect dose-rate or symmetry faults.
5. **Secondary Colimators (Jaws)** — two pairs of tungsten blocks (X-jaws and Y-jaws), each providing a rectangular field up to 40 × 40 cm at isocentre.
6. **Multileaf Collimator (MLC)** — 80–160 pairs of narrow tungsten leaves (5 mm or 10 mm width) for **conformal shaping** of irregular fields (IMRT, VMAT).
7. **Light Field & Optical Distance Indicator (ODI)** — mirror projects the field outline onto the patient; a scale provides SSD readout.
8. **Wedge Filters / Compensators** (optional) — modify dose distribution.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Force on electron in RF gap | $F = qE_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | N | $q = -e$ for electron |
| 2 | Energy gain in single gap | $W = qV_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | eV | $V_0$ = peak gap voltage |
| 3 | Energy gain in N-cell structure | $W_T = N \cdot qV_0 \cos\phi_s$ | eV | $\phi_s$ = synchronous phase |
| 4 | Rest mass energy of electron | $E_0 = m_0 c^2 = 0.511$ | MeV | **Always quote this exactly** |
| 5 | Total relativistic energy | $E = \gamma m_0 c^2$ | MeV | $\gamma = 1/\sqrt{1-\beta^2}$ |
| 6 | Kinetic energy | $T = (\gamma - 1) m_0 c^2$ | MeV | **This is the beam energy** |
| 7 | Lorentz factor | $\gamma = 1 + T/(m_0 c^2)$ | — | For 6 MeV electron: $\gamma \approx 12.7$ |
| 8 | Relativistic momentum | $p = \gamma m_0 v = \beta \gamma m_0 c$ | MeV/c | Used in bending magnet design |
| 9 | Bending magnet radius | $r = p/(qB) = \beta\gamma m_0 c/(qB)$ | m | Defines energy selection |
| 10 | Phase velocity in TW | $v_p = \omega/k$ | m/s | Made $\approx c$ by irises |
| 11 | Wavelength (S-band) | $\lambda = c/f = 3\times 10^{8} / 2.856\times 10^{9}$ | m | $\approx 0.105$ m |
| 12 | Electron range in water | $R_e \approx 0.412 \cdot E^{1.265 - 0.0954 \ln E}$ | g/cm² | Empirical, valid 1–20 MeV |
| 13 | Photon mean free path | $\lambda_{mfp} = 1/(\mu)$ | cm | Energy-dependent, lead: ~1 cm at 6 MV |
| 14 | %DD at d_max (6 MV) | $\approx 100\%$ | — | Reference depth ≈ 1.5 cm |
| 15 | MU-to-dose conversion | 1 MU = 1 cGy at d_max, 10×10 cm², SAD 100 | cGy | Calibration standard |
| 16 | Synchronous phase | $\phi_s = \arccos(W_T/(N qV_0))$ | rad | Determines accelerator efficiency |

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU exam, **Equation 3** and **Equations 4–7** are the most-tested. Memorise the values of $\gamma$ at 6 MeV ($\approx 12.7$) and at 1 MeV ($\approx 2.96$). Examiners love the question: *"What is the velocity of a 6 MeV electron as a fraction of c?"* — answer: $\beta = \sqrt{1 - 1/\gamma^2} = \sqrt{1 - 1/12.7^2} \approx 0.9969c$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear accelerators are the **workhorse of modern radiotherapy**, treating > **50% of all cancer patients** receiving radiation. They enable:
- **3D-CRT (3D Conformal Radiotherapy):** simple rectangular/irregular fields.
- **IMRT (Intensity-Modulated Radiotherapy):** non-uniform fluence maps from multiple beam angles.
- **VMAT (Volumetric Modulated Arc Therapy):** gantry rotation + dynamic MLC + variable dose rate.
- **SRS / SBRT (Stereotactic):** sub-mm precision for brain and body lesions.
- **Electron beam therapy:** for superficial tumours (< 5 cm depth).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Gain of a Charged Particle in a Single RF Gap

**Starting point:** The Lorentz force on a charge $q$ moving through an electric field $\vec{E}$:

$$
\vec{F} = q\vec{E} + q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}
$$

**Step 1:** Inside the accelerating gap, the magnetic component is negligible (TE mode in a cylindrical cavity has $E_z$ on axis and $B_\theta$ which gives no axial force). So the axial force is:

$$
F_z = qE_z(t, z)
$$

**Step 2:** For a uniform field across a gap of length $L$, the energy gained by a particle traversing the gap from $z = 0$ to $z = L$ is:

$$
W = \int_0^L qE_z \, dz
$$

**Step 3:** Since the field is uniform in space but sinusoidal in time, with $E_z(t) = E_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, this becomes:

$$
W = \int_0^L q E_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi) \, dz
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $dz = v\, dt$ (the particle moves at velocity $v$ through the gap):

$$
W = q E_0 \int_{t_1}^{t_2} v \sin(\omega t + \phi) \, dt
$$

**Step 5:** If the transit time $T_{tr} = L/v$ is small compared to one RF period $T_{RF} = 2\pi/\omega$, the velocity and phase can be approximated as constant across the gap. Then:

$$
W \approx q E_0 L \sin(\omega t_s + \phi)
$$

**Step 6:** Defining the **peak gap voltage** as $V_0 = E_0 L$, we obtain the canonical form:

$$
\boxed{\,W = qV_0 \sin\phi_s\,}
$$

where $\phi_s$ is the **synchronous phase** at which the particle crosses the gap.

**Step 7:** For a multi-cell structure with $N$ identical accelerating gaps, the total energy gained is:

$$
\boxed{\,W_T = N \cdot qV_0 \cos\phi_s\,}
$$

(The cosine appears because we choose the synchronous phase such that the particle is on the rising crest; the convention in medical LINAC textbooks is $\cos\phi_s$ for the synchronous particle's peak energy gain.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relativistic Velocity of a 6 MeV Electron

**Given:** Electron accelerated to kinetic energy $T = 6$ MeV. Find its velocity as a fraction of $c$.

**Step 1:** Total relativistic energy:

$$
E = T + m_0 c^2 = 6 + 0.511 = 6.511\ \text{MeV}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Lorentz factor $\gamma$:

$$
\gamma = \frac{E}{m_0 c^2} = \frac{6.511}{0.511} = 12.7427
$$

**Step 3:** Solve $\gamma = 1/\sqrt{1-\beta^2}$ for $\beta$:

$$
\sqrt{1-\beta^2} = \frac{1}{\gamma} = \frac{1}{12.7427} = 0.07848
$$

$$
1 - \beta^2 = (0.07848)^2 = 0.006159
$$

$$
\beta^2 = 1 - 0.006159 = 0.993841
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\beta = \sqrt{0.993841} = 0.99692\,}
$$

**Step 4:** Velocity of the electron:

$$
v = \beta c = 0.99692 \times 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s} \approx 2.991 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 (Interpretation):** The electron is travelling at **99.7% of the speed of light**. At this velocity, its mass has increased by a factor of **12.7×** compared to its rest mass. This is why classical (Newtonian) mechanics **cannot** be used in LINAC design — relativistic corrections are essential.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bending Magnet Radius for Energy Selection

**Given:** A 6 MeV electron beam is bent through 90° using a sector magnet. The flux density is $B = 1.2$ T. Calculate the bending radius $r$.

**Step 1:** Relativistic momentum:

$$
p = \beta \gamma m_0 c
$$

With $\beta \approx 0.9969$ and $\gamma \approx 12.74$:

$$
\beta\gamma = 0.9969 \times 12.74 = 12.70
$$

$$
p = 12.70 \times 0.511\ \text{MeV}/c = 6.490\ \text{MeV}/c
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to SI units. Recall $1\ \text{MeV}/c = 5.344 \times 10^{-22}\ \text{kg·m/s}$:

$$
p = 6.490 \times 5.344 \times 10^{-22} = 3.468 \times 10^{-21}\ \text{kg·m/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate centripetal force to magnetic Lorentz force:

$$
\frac{\gamma m_0 v^2}{r} = qvB
$$

Solving for $r$:

$$
r = \frac{\gamma m_0 v}{qB} = \frac{p}{qB}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values ($q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C):

$$
r = \frac{3.468 \times 10^{-21}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.2}
$$

$$
r = \frac{3.468 \times 10^{-21}}{1.922 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.804 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,r \approx 1.80\ \text{cm} \approx 18\ \text{mm}\,}
$$

**Step 5:** A bending radius of 18 mm is very small — this is why LINAC bending magnets use **strong permanent or electromagnetic fields** and **carefully shaped pole faces** to focus the beam in both transverse planes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Forgetting the relativistic mass factor $\gamma$. With classical mechanics, you would get $r \approx 1.4$ mm — an answer that is **off by a factor of 12.7**. This is a guaranteed zero in KTU valuation if the answer is wrong by an order of magnitude.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Electron Range in Water (Empirical Power Law)

For therapeutic electron beams, the practical range $R_p$ in water is given by the **Markovsky empirical formula**:

$$
R_p = 0.412 \cdot E^{1.265 - 0.0954 \ln E}\ \ \text{g/cm}^2
$$

where $E$ is in MeV and $R_p$ is in g/cm² (multiply by water density 1 g/cm³ to get cm).

**Worked example:** For $E = 12$ MeV electrons:

$$
\ln 12 = 2.4849
$$

$$
1.265 - 0.0954 \times 2.4849 = 1.265 - 0.2371 = 1.0279
$$

$$
E^{1.0279} = 12^{1.0279} = e^{1.0279 \times \ln 12} = e^{1.0279 \times 2.4849} = e^{2.5542} = 12.864
$$

$$
R_p = 0.412 \times 12.864 = 5.30\ \text{g/cm}^2 \approx 5.3\ \text{cm in water}
$$

**Clinical significance:** 12 MeV electrons will treat tumours up to about **5 cm deep**, beyond which the dose falls off rapidly. This is the basis for electron beam therapy of superficial lesions.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Useful Numerical Benchmarks (for KTU Numerical Problems)

| Beam | Energy | $\gamma$ | $\beta$ | $v/c$ | $p$ (MeV/c) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electron | 1 MeV | 2.96 | 0.941 | 94.1% | 2.78 |
| Electron | 6 MeV | 12.74 | 0.9969 | 99.69% | 6.49 |
| Electron | 15 MeV | 30.36 | 0.9995 | 99.95% | 15.51 |
| Electron | 25 MeV | 49.92 | 0.9998 | 99.98% | 25.51 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Full LINAC System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete signal and beam flow from power-on to patient delivery. Each block represents a physical subsystem, with the *beam path* shown as solid arrows and the *RF / control signal path* shown as dashed arrows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[HV Power Supply and PFN] --> MOD[Modulator and Thyratron Switch]
    MOD -- HV Pulse --> EGUN[Electron Gun Grid and Cathode]
    MOD -- HV Pulse --> MAG[Magnetron or Klystron RF Source]
    MAG -- 2856 MHz RF --> WG[Accelerating Waveguide SW or TW]
    EGUN -- Electron Pulse --> BUN[Buncher Cavity]
    BUN --> WG
    WG -- Accelerated Electron Beam --> BMAG[270 Degree Achromatic Bending Magnet]
    BMAG --> ENGS[Energy Slit and Steering Coils]
    ENGS --> TGT[Tungsten Target X Ray Production]
    MAG -- RF Reference --> AFC[Automatic Frequency Control Loop]
    AFC --> MAG
    ENGS -- Scattered Beam --> FF[Flattening Filter]
    TGT -- Bremsstrahlung Photons --> FF
    FF --> ICH[Dual Transmission Ion Chambers Dose and Symmetry]
    ICH --> JAWS[Secondary Colimator Jaws X and Y]
    JAWS --> MLC[Multileaf Collimator 80 to 160 pairs]
    MLC --> MIR[Mirror and Light Field Projection]
    MIR --> PAT[Patient at Isocentre SAD 100 cm]
    ICH -- Dose Signal --> CTRL[Console Computer and Interlock System]
    CTRL -- Trigger Enable --> MOD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left half** (above the bending magnet) is the *acceleration chain* — produces a high-energy electron beam.
- The **right half** is the *treatment head* — shapes, monitors, and delivers the beam to the patient.
- The **dashed RF return path** (Magnetron → AFC → Magnetron) is the **automatic frequency control** that locks the magnetron to the resonant frequency of the waveguide (essential because magnetrons drift in frequency with temperature and anode current).
- The **interlock feedback** (ICH → CTRL → MOD) is the safety chain that aborts the beam within microseconds if a dose-rate or symmetry fault is detected.

## 4.2 Treatment Head — Photon Mode Internal Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Electron Beam or Photons from Bending Magnet] --> PC[Primary Collimator Divergent Cone]
    PC --> TGT2[Target Tungsten for Photon Mode]
    TGT2 --> FF2[Flattening Filter Copper or Steel]
    FF2 --> IC1[Ion Chamber 1 Dose Integration]
    IC1 --> IC2[Ion Chamber 2 Symmetry Backup]
    IC2 --> YJ[Y Jaws Upper and Lower]
    YJ --> XJ[X Jaws Left and Right]
    XJ --> MLC2[Multileaf Collimator MLC]
    MLC2 --> M2[Field Defining Mirror 45 Degree]
    M2 --> ODI[Optical Distance Indicator and Light Field]
    ODI --> OUT[Beam to Patient SAD 100 cm]
    WEDGE[Optional Wedge Filter or Compensator] -. Insertable .-> FF2
```

## 4.3 Standing-Wave vs Travelling-Wave Accelerator — Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SW[Standing Wave Structure]
        SW1[Coupler Iris] --> SW2[Accelerating Cell 1]
        SW2 --> SW3[Coupling Cell]
        SW3 --> SW4[Accelerating Cell 2]
        SW4 --> SW5[End Short]
    end
    subgraph TW[Travelling Wave Structure]
        TW1[Input Coupler] --> TW2[Iris Loaded Cell 1]
        TW2 --> TW3[Iris Loaded Cell 2]
        TW3 --> TW4[Iris Loaded Cell N]
        TW4 --> TW5[Ferrite Absorber Load]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid rendering tip:** If the Mermaid block fails to render in your viewer (e.g., GitHub dark mode), the SW structure has *alternating accelerating and coupling cells*, while the TW structure has a *uniform chain of iris-loaded cells terminated by an absorber*. The structural difference is the **end termination**: SW uses a short circuit, TW uses a matched load.

## 4.4 Energy Selection in the Bending Magnet (Process Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BEAM[Polydisperse Electron Beam from Waveguide] --> B[270 Degree Sector Magnet B Field]
    B -- Correct Momentum --> FOC[Focused Spot at Target]
    B -- Too Low Momentum --> LOW[Deflected Outside Aperture Absorbed]
    B -- Too High Momentum --> HIGH[Deflected Other Side Absorbed]
    FOC --> TARGET[Tungsten Target]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The bending magnet acts as a **momentum filter** — only electrons with $p = qBr$ reach the target. This is why a LINAC's **energy spread** at the target is much smaller (typically ±2–3%) than the energy spread leaving the waveguide (~±5–10%).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define **isocentre** in a linear accelerator. Mention its standard distance from the target. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer:**
The **isocentre** is the point in space about which all mechanical rotations of the gantry, collimator, and treatment couch are performed, and at which the central axis of the radiation beam intersects for all field sizes and gantry angles. In a clinical LINAC, the isocentre is located at a **Source-to-Axis Distance (SAD) of 100 cm** from the X-ray target. The isocentre is a virtual point inside the patient (typically marked by the room lasers) where the tumour volume is positioned for treatment, allowing multi-field coplanar and non-coplanar beam arrangements without moving the patient. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2. Differentiate between **standing-wave** and **travelling-wave** linear accelerators. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Standing Wave (SW) | Travelling Wave (TW) |
|---|---|---|
| RF field pattern | Stationary in space; oscillates in time | Propagates from input to output |
| Termination | Short-circuited at both ends | Matched ferrite absorber at far end |
| Length for 6 MV | ~30 cm (compact) | ~1.5 m (longer) |
| Power efficiency | High (no absorbed residual RF) | Lower (residual RF dumped) |
| Modern usage | Preferred in current Varian / Elekta LINACs | Older designs (Varian Clinac 600C) |

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for table structure, key physical difference, and engineering implication.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (internal choice).

---

### **Question A. [14 Marks]** *(KTU University Exam — July 2024)*

**(a) Explain the working principle of a magnetron used in a medical linear accelerator, with a neat sketch. List its advantages and limitations compared to a klystron.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A **magnetron** is a high-power microwave oscillator that generates the 2856 MHz RF pulse required for electron acceleration.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- A **cylindrical copper anode block** with an even number (typically 8 or 10) of resonant cavities machined into its inner face, surrounding a central **oxide-coated tungsten cathode**.
- A strong **axial magnetic field** $B$ is provided by permanent magnets external to the anode.
- A **coupling loop** (or slot) in one cavity extracts RF energy into the waveguide.

**Working principle (3 marks):**
1. The cathode is heated to ~1100 K, producing thermionic electrons.
2. A pulsed DC voltage (~25 kV) is applied between cathode and anode. Without a magnetic field, electrons would travel radially outward.
3. The axial $B$ field forces electrons into **curved (cycloidal) paths** under combined $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$ drift.
4. As electrons sweep past the cavity openings, they induce oscillating RF currents in the cavity walls. The cavities are tuned to resonate at 2856 MHz.
5. The RF field in turn **bunches** the electrons into **spoke-like patterns** rotating around the cathode. Each spoke transfers kinetic energy to the RF field (rather than absorbing it), sustaining and amplifying oscillation.
6. RF is extracted via the coupling loop and delivered to the accelerating waveguide.

**Advantages over Klystron (1 mark):**
- Self-oscillating — does not require a low-power RF driver.
- Compact and cheaper.
- Sufficient for low-/medium-energy LINACs (≤ 10 MV).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Frequency drifts with temperature and anode current → needs **AFC (Automatic Frequency Control)** to lock to waveguide resonance.
- Shorter lifetime (~3000 h vs 10,000+ h for klystron).
- Lower peak power than klystron → unsuitable for very high-energy (> 15 MV) units.

---

**(b) Describe the components of a LINAC treatment head for **photon mode** operation, with the function of each component. Draw a labelled diagram.** [7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

From the target (upstream) towards the patient (downstream):

1. **Primary Collimator (1 mark):** A fixed tungsten-alloy block with a divergent conical opening. Defines the maximum circular field (~ Ø 50 cm at isocentre). Stops photons scattered outside the useful beam.

2. **Target (1 mark):** A thin (1–2 mm) high-Z tungsten disc ($Z = 74$) positioned in the electron beam. The electron beam strikes the target and produces a **bremsstrahlung (X-ray) beam** in the forward direction.

3. **Flattening Filter (1 mark):** A conical copper or steel filter placed in the beam. It preferentially attenuates the central axis (where bremsstrahlung intensity is highest) to produce a **flat dose profile** (uniformity ±3%) across the field at 10 cm depth.

4. **Dose Monitor Ion Chambers (1 mark):** Two independent thin transmission ion chambers monitor dose (integrated) and symmetry/dose-rate. Provide **interlock signals** that terminate the beam within microseconds of any fault.

5. **Secondary Colimators / Jaws (1 mark):** Two orthogonal pairs of tungsten blocks (X-jaws, Y-jaws) providing variable rectangular fields from 0 × 0 cm up to **40 × 40 cm** at isocentre.

6. **Multileaf Collimator (MLC) (1 mark):** 80–160 pairs of independently driven tungsten leaves (width 5 or 10 mm at isocentre) for **conformal shaping** of irregular tumour outlines. Essential for IMRT and VMAT.

7. **Light Field and Optical Distance Indicator (1 mark):** A mirror and projection lamp project the field outline onto the patient skin. A scale provides SSD readout. The **x-ray field and light field must coincide within 2 mm** at the isocentre (IEC requirement).

---

### **Question B. [14 Marks]** *(Internal Alternative — KTU Model Paper 2024)*

**(a) Explain the construction and working of the **electron gun and bunching system** in a medical LINAC.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Electron Gun (4 marks):**
- A **triode-type electron gun** with three electrodes: cathode, grid (Wehnelt), and anode.
- **Cathode:** A flat or concave tungsten disc coated with barium-strontium oxide, heated indirectly to ~1100–1300 K by a filament behind it. Thermionic emission per **Richardson's law**: $J = A_R T^2 \exp(-W/kT)$, where $W$ is the work function.
- **Grid:** A control electrode (held at a small negative bias, typically −100 to −300 V) that gates the emission — opening only during the ~1–5 µs RF pulse to produce a synchronised electron burst.
- **Anode:** A grounded aperture plate that accelerates the emitted electrons (to ~10–50 keV) into the buncher.
- A small **focusing electrode** shapes the beam into a converging pencil of ~2–3 mm diameter at the buncher entrance.

**Bunching System (3 marks):**
- The buncher is a **single RF gap** resonant at 2856 MHz, fed with a small amount of RF power (~10% of main power).
- The buncher is operated at the **velocity-modulation phase** (≈ 0° crossing on the rising slope of the sine wave).
- **Velocity modulation:** Electrons that arrive early (at negative phase) are decelerated; electrons that arrive late (at positive phase) are accelerated. The fast ones catch up with the slow ones, and the bunches coalesce.
- The output of the buncher is a train of **short electron bunches**, each ~10–20 ps long, separated by one RF period (~350 ps).
- These bunches then enter the main accelerating waveguide at the correct synchronous phase and ride successive wave crests to high energy.

---

**(b) Describe the **beam transport system** in a modern isocentric LINAC. Explain the role of the **270° bending magnet** in energy selection and focusing.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Beam Transport Subsystems (3 marks):**
- After exiting the accelerating waveguide, the electron beam has an energy spread of ~±5–10% and a diameter of ~3–5 mm.
- **Steering coils** (magnetic dipoles) correct the beam's trajectory so it enters the bending magnet on-axis.
- **Quadrupole focusing magnets** (electromagnetic doublets or triplets) keep the beam narrow during its transit through the bending magnet and into the treatment head.
- **Energy slit:** A variable-aperture slit at the exit of the bending magnet can be closed to reduce the energy spread at the cost of dose rate. In clinical operation, it is usually fully open.

**Role of the 270° Bending Magnet (4 marks):**
1. **Direction change:** The horizontal beam from the waveguide is bent 270° (or 90° in older designs) so that it travels **vertically downward** through the treatment head and exits at the isocentre 100 cm from the target.
2. **Energy selection:** A charged particle in a uniform magnetic field follows a circular path of radius $r = p/(qB) = \beta\gamma m_0 c/(qB)$. Only electrons with momentum $p$ such that $r$ matches the magnet's physical radius (typically ~3–5 cm) reach the exit aperture. Electrons with higher or lower momentum are deflected into the magnet's walls and absorbed.
3. **Achromatic double focusing:** The **entrance and exit pole faces** of the magnet are tilted at angles (typically 26.6° each for 270° bending). This shape provides:
   - **Horizontal focusing** (in the bending plane) — the entrance/exit angles converge the rays radially.
   - **Vertical focusing** (in the perpendicular plane) — the fringe field gradients act like a weak quadrupole.
4. **Mounting geometry:** The 270° design allows the heavy gantry components (waveguide, modulator) to be mounted in a "saddle" behind the bending magnet, distributing mass symmetrically and making the gantry mechanically stable during rotation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings / Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **1. Energy units:** Examiners *will* deduct marks if you mix up **MV (megavoltage for photons)** with **MeV (mega-electron-volt for electrons)**. Photon energies are quoted in MV (the maximum electron energy striking the target); electron energies are quoted in MeV. They are numerically similar but conceptually different.

> [!WARNING]
> **2. Relativistic mass vs relativistic mass increase:** Do **not** say "the mass of the electron is 12.7 times bigger." Modern convention: the rest mass $m_0$ is invariant, and the relativistic *energy* is $\gamma m_0 c^2$. Say: "the relativistic energy is 12.7 times the rest energy" or "the inertia is 12.7 times the rest inertia."

> [!WARNING]
> **3. Magnetron vs Klystron confusion:** A magnetron is an **oscillator** (self-excited); a klystron is an **amplifier** (requires a low-power RF driver). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

> [!WARNING]
> **4. SAD vs SSD:** SAD = Source to **Axis** (isocentre) Distance = **100 cm**. SSD = Source to **Surface** Distance = typically 80–100 cm for SSD setups. They are **not** the same.

> [!WARNING]
> **5. Dose rate units:** Always quote MU/min for LINAC output, not cGy/min directly. 1 MU is **defined** as 1 cGy at d_max for a 10×10 cm² field at SAD 100 cm. Examiners expect this definition.

> [!WARNING]
> **6. Common calculation error:** In the bending radius derivation, students forget the factor of $\gamma$ and obtain answers that are off by **exactly** a factor equal to the Lorentz factor. Always show the $\gamma$ explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **LINAC = Linear particle Accelerator** — accelerates electrons in a straight line using RF (2856 MHz, S-band).
- ✅ **Four major subsystems:** Modulator + RF source | Electron gun + buncher + waveguide | Bending magnet + transport | Treatment head.
- ✅ **Magnetron** for low/medium energy (≤10 MV); **Klystron** for high energy (>10 MV).
- ✅ **Travelling-wave (TW):** iris-loaded waveguide, RF propagates, ferrite termination.
- ✅ **Standing-wave (SW):** resonant cavity chain, side-coupled, ~30% shorter, preferred modern design.
- ✅ **Synchronous phase** $\phi_s$ ensures electrons arrive at each gap at the right time.
- ✅ **Energy per gap:** $W = qV_0 \sin\phi_s$ — total: $W_T = N qV_0 \cos\phi_s$.
- ✅ **Electron rest energy:** $m_0 c^2 = 0.511$ **MeV** (MUST memorise).
- ✅ **Relativistic velocity:** $\beta = \sqrt{1 - 1/\gamma^2}$, where $\gamma = 1 + T/(0.511)$.
- ✅ **6 MeV electron:** $\gamma \approx 12.7$, $\beta \approx 0.997$, $v \approx 2.99 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **Bending magnet:** $r = p/(qB) = \beta\gamma m_0 c/(qB)$; acts as momentum filter + focuser + direction changer.
- ✅ **Treatment head (photon mode):** Primary collimator → Target → Flattening filter → Ion chambers → Jaws → MLC → Light field → Patient.
- ✅ **Standard SAD = 100 cm**; field size 0–40 × 40 cm; photon energies 6/15 MV typical; electron energies 6/9/12/15 MeV typical.
- ✅ **1 MU = 1 cGy at d_max**, 10×10 cm², SAD 100 cm (calibration standard).
- ✅ **Electron range in water:** $R_p \approx 0.412 \cdot E^{1.265 - 0.0954 \ln E}$ g/cm².
- ✅ **Safety chain:** Dose-rate interlock, symmetry interlock, wedge interlock, MLC interlock — all feedback to the modulator and abort the beam in microseconds.
- ✅ **Isocentre =** the fixed point of gantry/collimator/couch rotation; SAD = 100 cm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brachytherapy: Techniques and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT502 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Brachytherapy: Techniques and Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brachytherapy** (from the Greek word *brachys* meaning "short distance") is a form of radiotherapy in which a sealed radioactive source is placed **inside, on the surface of, or very close to** the tumour or treatment volume, typically at a distance of **0.5 cm to 5 cm** from the target. The procedure is also known as **Endocurietherapy** or **Curietherapy**, after Marie Curie.

The essential distinction between brachytherapy and external beam radiotherapy (teletherapy) lies in the **dose gradient fall-off**: brachytherapy follows the **inverse square law (1/d²)** which delivers an extremely high dose to the tumour and a rapidly decreasing dose to the surrounding healthy tissue, making it an ideal conformal therapy modality.

> [!NOTE]
> **ICRU Report 38 (1984)** and **ICRU Report 89 (2013)** define the dosimetric and clinical terminology for brachytherapy. Total Reference Air Kerma (TRAK) and reference isodose concepts are standardized by these protocols.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are trying to read a book at night. You could:
- **External Beam (Teletherapy)** – Place a ceiling spotlight above you. The light is spread across the room and the book is illuminated, but so is everything else. Energy is wasted on healthy areas.
- **Brachytherapy** – Hold a small reading lamp directly above the book, touching it. The book gets bright light, but the rest of the room is dark. Energy is concentrated exactly where it is needed.

This "short-distance" principle is what gives brachytherapy its superior **conformality** and makes it the gold standard for several gynaecological, prostate, breast, and head-and-neck cancers.

## 1.3 Classification by Dose Rate

| Dose Rate Category | Abbreviation | Dose Rate Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low Dose Rate | **LDR** | **0.4 – 2 Gy/hr** | Gynaecological, head & neck |
| Medium Dose Rate | **MDR** | **2 – 12 Gy/hr** | Historical, transitional |
| High Dose Rate | **HDR** | **> 12 Gy/hr** | Gyn, prostate, breast, lung |
| Pulsed Dose Rate | **PDR** | **Pulses of 0.5 – 1 Gy/hr** mimicking LDR | Gynaecological |

> [!NOTE]
> The conversion **1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq** and **1 Gy = 100 rad** are mandatory fundamentals for KTU numerical problems.

## 1.4 Classification by Source Position

| Technique | Source Location | Clinical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface / Contact** | Plaques, moulds on skin | Skin, eye (ophthalmic) |
| **Intracavitary** | Inside body cavities | Cervix, endometrium, vagina, bronchus, oesophagus |
| **Interstitial** | Within the tissue/tumour | Breast, prostate, brain, sarcoma, tongue |
| **Intraluminal** | Inside a lumen | Bronchus, oesophagus, bile duct |
| **Intravascular** | Inside a blood vessel | Coronary artery (brachy-radiotherapy) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Physics of Brachytherapy

Brachytherapy is governed by three core physical principles:

### 2.1.1 Inverse Square Law
The intensity (and therefore the dose rate) of radiation emanating from a point source falls off with the **square of the distance** from the source. Unlike teletherapy, where the inverse square law is a "drawback" producing penumbra, in brachytherapy it is a **therapeutic advantage**, sparing surrounding healthy tissue.

### 2.1.2 Source Strength Quantities
Multiple historical and modern units are used. A KTU student must be conversant with all of them:

- **Curie (Ci) / Becquerel (Bq):** Activity (decays per second). 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq.
- **Milligram-Radium Equivalent (mgRaeq):** The mass of radium-226 that produces the same exposure rate.
- **Air Kerma Strength (S_K), Unit: U (capital U):** The dose-equivalent quantity recommended by the AAPM (TG-32, TG-43). **1 U = 1 μGy·m²·h⁻¹ = 1 cGy·cm²·h⁻¹**.
- **Reference Air Kerma Rate (RAKR):** The kerma rate to air in free space at 1 m from the source.

### 2.1.3 Dose Fall-off and Anisotropy
- **Isotropic sources** (e.g., early radium tubes) emit uniformly in all directions.
- **Anisotropic sources** (modern microSelectron, Flexisource, GammaMed) have an angular variation in dose rate described by the **anisotropy function F(r,θ)**.

## 2.2 Brachytherapy Techniques in Detail

### 2.2.1 Surface / Contact Therapy
A radioactive source is moulded into a **plaque** or **applicator** placed on the skin or eye surface. The most common system historically used the **Manchester mould system** with radium sources, replaced today by low-energy radionuclide applicators.

### 2.2.2 Intracavitary Therapy
- The applicator is inserted into a body cavity near the tumour.
- The **Fletcher-Suit** and **Henschke** applicators are the classical intracavitary applicators for gynaecological cancers.
- The **tandem** (placed in the uterine canal) and **ovoids / colpostats** (placed in the vaginal fornices) are the geometric standard.
- The Manchester system defines a **Point A** (defined below) and **Point B** as prescription and reference points.

### 2.2.3 Interstitial Therapy
- Radioactive needles, seeds, or wires are placed directly into the tumour volume.
- The **Manchester system** and the **Paris system** are the two foundational dosimetric systems.
- Modern systems use **permanently implanted seeds** (I-125, Pd-103) for prostate cancer.

### 2.2.4 Intravascular Brachytherapy
Used to prevent **restenosis** after coronary angioplasty. Sources used are **Sr-90/Y-90**, **P-32**, or **Ir-192** delivered by a catheter-based afterloader.

## 2.3 The Afterloading Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Afterloading** is the cardinal safety principle of modern brachytherapy. The empty applicator is first placed in the patient, and the radioactive source is loaded into the applicator only after correct positioning is verified.

- **Hot loading (manual, historical):** Active sources handled directly — high staff exposure.
- **Manual afterloading:** Empty applicator first, then source loaded manually.
- **Remote afterloading:** Source moved by a motor-driven mechanism; the operator stays outside the shielded room.

## 2.4 Classical Dosimetric Systems

### 2.4.1 Manchester System (Paterson & Parker, 1934)
Developed for radium needle and tube implants. Defines:
- **Point A**: Located 2 cm superior to the lateral vaginal fornix, and 2 cm lateral to the central canal of the uterus (along the tandem). This is the prescription point for cervical cancer.
- **Point B**: 3 cm lateral to Point A. Represents the dose to the normal pelvic tissue and the obturator lymph nodes.
- **mg-hr rule**: A specified total radium-equivalent activity × time produces a defined dose.
- **Three rules of source distribution** for volume implants (1 mm lead walls, 0.5 mm platinum filter, 1.5 cm active length).

### 2.4.2 Paris System
Developed by **Pierquin, Chassagne, and Dutreix (1960s)** for Ir-192 wire implants.
- **Linear activity** is uniform along each source.
- Sources are parallel, equidistant, and centred in the same plane.
- The **prescription isodose** is the **85% of the basal dose** (the average of the minimum dose in the central plane between sources).
- **Crossing points** of perpendicular bisectors of the source axes define the prescription point.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Inverse Square Law | $D(d) \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}}$ | d = distance from source |
| Dose rate from source | $\dot{D}(r) = \dfrac{S_{K} \cdot \Lambda}{r^{2}} \cdot g(r) \cdot F(r,\theta)$ | r in cm, $S_K$ in U |
| Source decay | $S(t) = S_{0} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}$ |
| Decay constant | $\lambda = \dfrac{0.693}{T_{1/2}}$ | per hour or per day |
| Total dose (no decay) | $D_{total} = \dot{D}_{0} \times T$ | Gy |
| Total dose (with decay) | $D_{total} = \dfrac{\dot{D}_{0} \cdot T_{1/2}}{0.693} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda T} \right)$ | Gy |
| Treatment time | $T = \dfrac{D \cdot r^{2}}{S_{0} \cdot \Lambda}$ | hours |
| Air Kerma Strength | $S_{K} = \dot{K}_{air} \cdot d^{2}$ | U (μGy·m²/h) |
| Activity to Air Kerma | $S_{K}\ (\text{Ir-192}) \approx 4.08 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{U/mCi}$ | conversion |
| Manchester mg-hr | $D\ (\text{cGy}) \approx \dfrac{8.4 \times m}{V_{eff}}$ | m = mg-hr, V in cm³ |
| Exposure rate const. | $\Gamma_{\delta}$ (R·cm²/mCi·h at 1 cm) | 4.04 for Ir-192 |
| Curie to Bq | $1\ \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Bq}$ | exact |
| R to Gy in air | 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg → 0.876 rad → 8.76 mGy | exposure to dose |
| Sievert integral (Ir-192) | $\Lambda = 1.108\ \text{cGy·h}^{-1}\text{·U}^{-1}$ | dose rate constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Pitfall:** $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value should always be written as $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in equations. The decay factor is $\lvert 1 - e^{-\lambda T} \rvert$ which approaches 1 for long time horizons.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Treatment Planning Systems (TPS)** such as Oncentra Brachy, BrachyVision, Eclipse Brachy, and Pinnacle use these exact formulas.
- **TG-43 formalism** is the global standard for source calibration and dose computation.
- The **AAPM TG-229** protocol covers brachytherapy dose calculation beyond TG-43, using model-based algorithms.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Inverse Square Law for a Point Source

**Statement:** The dose rate at a distance *d* from an isotropic point source is inversely proportional to *d²*.

$$
\dot{D}(d) = \dfrac{S_{K} \cdot \Lambda}{d^{2}}
$$

**Derivation steps:**

Let a radioactive source emit $N$ photons per second isotropically (in all directions). Consider a sphere of radius *d* centred on the source. The surface area of the sphere is:

$$
A = 4\pi d^{2}
$$

The number of photons crossing unit area per second (photon fluence rate) is:

$$
\dot{\Phi} = \dfrac{N}{4\pi d^{2}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dot{\Phi} \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}}
$$

Since the dose rate is proportional to fluence rate (with the mass energy absorption coefficient as proportionality):

$$
\dot{D}(d) \propto \dot{\Phi} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dot{D}(d) \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}}
$$

Q.E.D. The dose rate falls to 1/4 at 2d, 1/9 at 3d, 1/100 at 10d.

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Dose Delivered with Source Decay

**Statement:** A brachytherapy source decays during the treatment time. The integrated dose over a treatment time *T* is:

$$
D_{total} = \dfrac{\dot{D}_{0}\cdot T_{1/2}}{\ln 2}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda T}\right)
$$

**Derivation:**

The instantaneous dose rate at time *t* is:

$$
\dot{D}(t) = \dot{D}_{0}\,e^{-\lambda t}
$$

The total dose is the time integral of the instantaneous dose rate:

$$
D_{total} = \int_{0}^{T} \dot{D}_{0}\,e^{-\lambda t}\,dt
$$

$$
D_{total} = \dot{D}_{0} \int_{0}^{T} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt
$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$
D_{total} = \dot{D}_{0}\left[\dfrac{-1}{\lambda}e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{T}
$$

$$
D_{total} = \dot{D}_{0}\left(\dfrac{1 - e^{-\lambda T}}{\lambda}\right)
$$

Substituting $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$:

$$
D_{total} = \dot{D}_{0} \cdot \dfrac{T_{1/2}}{\ln 2} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda T}\right)
$$

**Limiting cases:**
- If $T \ll T_{1/2}$ (e.g., 6 min HDR treatment vs 73.8 day Ir-192 half-life), then $e^{-\lambda T} \approx 1 - \lambda T$ and $D_{total} \approx \dot{D}_{0} \cdot T$. (No decay correction needed.)
- If $T \approx T_{1/2}$, the correction factor becomes significant.

## 3.3 Derivation: Treatment Time Calculation

**Statement:** Given a prescribed dose *D* and a measured initial dose rate $\dot{D}_{0}$, find the irradiation time *T*.

Starting from the basic dose equation (ignoring decay for HDR short treatments):

$$
D = \dot{D}_{0} \cdot T
$$

Solve for *T*:

$$
T = \dfrac{D}{\dot{D}_{0}}
$$

With decay included:

$$
D = \dfrac{\dot{D}_{0}}{\lambda}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda T}\right)
$$

Solve for *T*:

$$
e^{-\lambda T} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda D}{\dot{D}_{0}}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{-1}{\lambda}\ln\left(1 - \dfrac{\lambda D}{\dot{D}_{0}}\right)
$$

This is the exact expression for the treatment time of an LDR implant with significant decay during treatment.

## 3.4 Derivation: Manchester mg-hr Formula

**Statement:** The dose delivered by a radium implant (in cGy) is approximately:

$$
D\ (\text{cGy}) = \dfrac{8.4 \times m}{V_{eff}}
$$

where *m* is the total mg-hr (sum of milligram-radium-equivalents × hours) and $V_{eff}$ is the effective treated volume in cm³.

**Derivation logic:**

- The exposure rate at distance *r* from a point radium source of activity *m* mg is:

$$
\dot{X} = \dfrac{m \cdot \Gamma_{Ra}}{r^{2}}
$$

where $\Gamma_{Ra} = 8.4\ \text{R·cm}^2/(\text{mg·h})$ at 1 cm, including the 0.5 mm Pt filtration.
- For a small uniform distribution of sources in a sphere of effective volume $V_{eff}$, the average distance from a point inside the volume to a source is approximately proportional to $V_{eff}^{1/3}$.
- The energy deposited per unit mass (dose) is proportional to exposure rate × W/e (33.97 J/C for air, but for tissue ≈ 0.876 rad/R).
- Combining: $D \approx \dfrac{m \times 8.4 \times 0.876 \times 100}{V_{eff}^{2/3}} \approx \dfrac{8.4 \times m}{V_{eff}}$ (with appropriate normalization).
- This is the famous **mg-hr** rule, used clinically in the 1930s–1970s.

## 3.5 Derivation: Activity – Air Kerma Strength Conversion (Ir-192)

**Statement:** For an Ir-192 source, $S_{K}\ (\text{U}) = 4.08 \times 10^{-2} \times A\ (\text{mCi})$ or equivalently $S_{K}\ (\text{U}) = 1.108 \times 10^{-1} \times A\ (\text{Ci})$ per the AAPM TG-43 protocol.

**Derivation steps:**

- Air kerma rate at distance *d* from a source of activity *A* (in Ci) and exposure rate constant $\Gamma_{\delta}$ is:

$$
\dot{K}_{air}(d) = A \cdot \Gamma_{\delta} \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{e}\right)
$$

For Ir-192, $\Gamma_{\delta} = 4.04\ \text{R·cm}^2/(\text{mCi·h})$.
- The W/e for air is 33.97 J/C; converting 1 R to kerma in air: 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg = 8.76 mGy.
- Thus: $\dot{K}_{air}(1\ \text{cm}) = A\ (\text{mCi}) \times 4.04 \times 8.76 = 35.39\ A\ \text{mGy·cm}^2/\text{h}$ = $35.39 \times 10^{-2}\ A\ \text{cGy·cm}^2/\text{h}$ = $0.3539\ A\ \text{cGy·cm}^2/\text{h}$.
- Recalibrated with modern Sievert integral: $S_{K}/A = 4.08 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{U/mCi}$.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Brachytherapy Techniques

| Parameter | Intracavitary | Interstitial | Surface / Mould | Intravascular |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Source-to-tumour distance | 1–5 cm | 0.5–2 cm | 0–1 cm | 2–5 mm |
| Typical dose rate | LDR / HDR | LDR / HDR / permanent | LDR | HDR |
| Common sources | Cs-137, Ir-192, Co-60 | Ir-192, I-125, Pd-103 | Sr-90, Ra-226 | Sr-90, P-32, Ir-192 |
| Common system | Manchester (Point A) | Paris / Manchester | Manchester mould | IVBT |
| Common sites | Cervix, endometrium | Breast, prostate, tongue | Skin, eye | Coronary arteries |
| Applicator | Tandem & ovoid | Needles, plastic tubes | Moulded plaques | Catheter |
| Total prescription | 80–85 Gy LDR / 6–9 Gy × 5 HDR | 60–70 Gy (LDR) | 40–60 Gy | 15–25 Gy |
| Anaesthesia required | Yes (HDR only) | Yes / sedation | No | Yes |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Differentiate between LDR and HDR brachytherapy. Mention the typical clinical applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | LDR Brachytherapy | HDR Brachytherapy |
|---|---|---|
| Dose rate | 0.4 – 2 Gy/hr | > 12 Gy/hr |
| Treatment time | 24 – 144 hours (days) | A few minutes |
| Radiation protection | Manual afterloading / shielded room; patient is radioactive for days | Fully remote afterloading; no staff exposure |
| Source movement | Static | Stepping source |
| Radiobiology | Continuous, low dose → more repair of sublethal damage | Fractionated short pulses |
| Typical applications | Gynaecological, head & neck, tongue | Cervical, prostate, breast, lung, oesophagus |
| Anaesthesia | Often required for the entire duration | Required only during applicator insertion |

**(3 marks complete)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Define the term "Air Kerma Strength" (S_K). State its unit and the conversion relation to Ci for Ir-192.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Definition:** The Air Kerma Strength $S_K$ of a brachytherapy source is defined as the product of the air kerma rate $\dot{K}_{air}(d)$ measured in free space at a distance *d* along the transverse axis of the source, multiplied by the square of that distance *d²*.
- **Formula:** $S_K = \dot{K}_{air}(d) \cdot d^{2}$
- **Unit:** U, where **1 U = 1 μGy·m²·h⁻¹ = 1 cGy·cm²·h⁻¹**
- **Conversion for Ir-192:** $1\ \text{Ci}\ (\text{Ir-192}) \approx 4.08 \times 10^{4}\ \text{U}$ (using modern microSelectron-Flexisource calibration, equivalent to $S_K/A \approx 4.08 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{U/mCi}$)

**(3 marks complete)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model]

**A) (a)** Explain the different brachytherapy techniques with suitable clinical examples. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the construction, working, and safety features of a modern HDR remote afterloading system with a block diagram. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Brachytherapy Techniques** are classified by **source position** as follows:

1. **Surface / Contact Therapy**
   - Sources in the form of plaques or moulds are placed on the skin or eye surface.
   - Low-energy emitters are used (Sr-90, Ru-106 for eye plaques; Ra-226 for skin moulds).
   - Example: Ophthalmic brachytherapy for choroidal melanoma using Ru-106 plaques.

2. **Intracavitary Therapy**
   - Applicator placed inside a body cavity close to the tumour.
   - Example: **Tandem and ovoid** applicator for cervical cancer; vaginal cylinder for vaginal cancer; oesophageal catheter for oesophageal tumours.
   - Most commonly uses Cs-137 (LDR) or Ir-192 (HDR).

3. **Interstitial Therapy**
   - Radioactive needles, seeds, or wires placed directly within the tumour tissue.
   - Examples: **Permanent seed implantation** of I-125 or Pd-103 for prostate cancer; **Ir-192 wire implants** for breast, head and neck; **temporary HDR interstitial** using plastic catheters.
   - The Paris system is the standard dosimetric system.

4. **Intraluminal Therapy**
   - A special case of intracavitary where a source is placed inside a tubular lumen.
   - Example: Endobronchial Ir-192 HDR for lung cancer palliation.

5. **Intravascular Brachytherapy (IVBT)**
   - Catheter-based source delivery inside a coronary artery after angioplasty.
   - Sr-90/Y-90, P-32, or Ir-192 sources used.
   - Prevents **intimal hyperplasia** and restenosis.

6. **Comparison with Teletherapy**

| Parameter | Brachytherapy | Teletherapy |
|---|---|---|
| Source distance | Short (0.5–5 cm) | Long (80–100 cm) |
| Dose conformity | Excellent (1/d² fall-off) | Moderate |
| Radiation protection | Need shielding; remote afterloading | Standard bunker |
| Staff exposure | Higher if manual | Lower |

**(7 marks complete)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**HDR Remote Afterloading System – Construction, Working, Safety:**

**Major Components** (block diagram in §4.2):
1. **Operator Console** – User interface outside the treatment room. Inputs treatment parameters, dwell positions, dwell times, channel selection.
2. **Treatment Planning Computer** – Generates the dwell-time pattern from the prescribed dose.
3. **Control Unit** – Main microcontroller; executes the plan, monitors interlocks.
4. **Stepper Motor Drive** – Moves the source wire with sub-mm accuracy.
5. **Source Safe** – A lead/tungsten shielded container that holds the source when not in use. Default position.
6. **Indexing Mechanism** – Distributes the source to the correct channel (1 of N).
7. **Channels** – Tubes through which the source travels to the applicator.
8. **Patient Applicators** – Pre-placed catheters or intracavitary devices.
9. **Backup Battery** – Maintains retraction in case of power failure.
10. **Emergency Retraction System** – Source returns to safe within <1 second on interlock failure.
11. **Source Position Encoders** – Verify source location at all times.

**Working Principle:**
- Empty applicators are placed in the patient first (afterloading principle).
- The patient is imaged (CT/MRI), the target is delineated, and a plan is generated.
- Dwell positions and times are calculated: typically 5–10 mm spacing, individual dwell times 5–60 seconds.
- On treatment initiation, the source is driven from the safe through the selected channel to each dwell position, with each dwell executed in sequence.

**Safety Features:**
- Multiple **interlocks** on door, applicator connection, source position, timer.
- **Backup battery** for emergency retraction.
- **Continuous radiation monitoring** in the room.
- **Independent verification** of dwell positions via dual encoders.
- **Handheld emergency stop** within and outside the room.
- **Decay correction** automatically applied at each fraction.
- **Source tracking** and **logging** for regulatory compliance.

**(7 marks complete)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model, Alternative Choice]

**B) (a)** Compare the **Manchester system** and the **Paris system** of brachytherapy dosimetry in terms of their source distribution, prescription, and dosimetric calculations. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the expression for total dose delivered by a decaying brachytherapy source, and hence determine the treatment time for an LDR implant delivering 60 Gy at 0.5 cm from a 4 mgRaeq source. Given: $\Gamma = 8.4\ \text{R·cm}^2/(\text{mg·h})$ and 1 R = 0.876 rad. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Comparison: Manchester vs Paris System**

| Parameter | Manchester System | Paris System |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Paterson & Parker, 1934 (UK) | Pierquin, Chassagne & Dutreix, 1960s (France) |
| **Source** | Radium needles and tubes | Ir-192 wires / plastic tube Ir-192 |
| **Source distribution** | Differential loading rules (1 mg / 1 cm of wire) | Uniform linear activity |
| **Geometry** | Surface moulds, volume implants, linear sources | Parallel, equidistant sources in a plane |
| **Active length** | Variable; ends are reinforced (×1.5 to ×2) | Equal; ends are NOT reinforced |
| **Source spacing** | Non-uniform (peripheral vs core) | Uniform (typically 1–1.5 cm) |
| **Prescription point** | 0.5 cm from implant surface (for moulds) | 85% of basal dose (central plane) |
| **Dose specification** | mg-hr rule; Point A & B for gynaec | Minimum target dose in central plane |
| **Volume calculation** | Sum of cylinders of each source | $V = L \times S \times W$ (length × spacing × width) |
| **Modern usage** | Gyn (Fletcher applicator), some interstitial | Interstitial implants (HDR/LDR) |

**Key features of Manchester rules for volume implants:**
- Volume < 30 cc: 2/3 activity on periphery, 1/3 in core.
- Volume 30–75 cc: 1/2 on periphery, 1/2 crossing in core.
- Volume > 75 cc: 2/3 on periphery, 1/3 in core, with double-strength outer ring.

**Key features of Paris system:**
- All sources have the same linear activity (kerma rate per unit length).
- Sources are equidistant, parallel, and centred in a plane.
- Reference isodose = 85% of **basal dose** (average of minimum doses in central plane).
- Active length = treatment length; no end reinforcement.

**(7 marks complete)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 – Derivation of the Total Dose Expression:**

The instantaneous dose rate at the prescription point decreases exponentially with time due to radioactive decay:

$$
\dot{D}(t) = \dot{D}_{0} \, e^{-\lambda t}
$$

where $\dot{D}_{0}$ is the initial dose rate and $\lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}$ is the decay constant.

The total dose delivered over a treatment time *T* is:

$$
D = \int_{0}^{T} \dot{D}_{0}\,e^{-\lambda t}\,dt
$$

Integrating:

$$
D = \dot{D}_{0}\left[\dfrac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda}\right]_{0}^{T}
$$

$$
D = \dfrac{\dot{D}_{0}}{\lambda}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda T}\right)
$$

Substituting $\lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}$:

$$
D = \dfrac{\dot{D}_{0} \cdot T_{1/2}}{\ln 2}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda T}\right)
$$

This is the **master equation** for an LDR implant with significant decay.

**Step 2 – Application: Numerical Treatment Time**

Given:
- Prescribed dose, $D = 60\ \text{Gy} = 6000\ \text{rad}$
- Distance from source to point, $r = 0.5\ \text{cm}$
- Source strength, $m = 4\ \text{mgRaeq}$
- Exposure rate constant, $\Gamma = 8.4\ \text{R·cm}^2/(\text{mg·h})$
- W/e for tissue: 1 R = 0.876 rad

**Step 3 – Calculate Initial Dose Rate $\dot{D}_{0}$:**

Exposure rate at the prescription point:

$$
\dot{X} = \dfrac{m \cdot \Gamma}{r^{2}} = \dfrac{4 \times 8.4}{(0.5)^{2}} = \dfrac{33.6}{0.25} = 134.4\ \text{R/h}
$$

Convert to dose rate in tissue:

$$
\dot{D}_{0} = 134.4 \times 0.876 = 117.7\ \text{rad/h} = 1.177\ \text{Gy/h}
$$

**Step 4 – Estimate the Treatment Time (Ignoring Decay Initially):**

$$
T \approx \dfrac{D}{\dot{D}_{0}} = \dfrac{60}{1.177} \approx 51\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 5 – Apply Decay Correction (Iterative):**

For a 4 mg Ra-226 source, $T_{1/2} = 1600\ \text{years} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{7}\ \text{h}$. Thus $\lambda = 4.95 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{h}^{-1}$, which is negligible during 51 hours.

Therefore, **decay correction is insignificant**, and:

$$
\boxed{T \approx 51\ \text{hours}\ (\text{about 2.1 days})}
$$

**(7 marks complete)**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the distance** in the inverse square law. At 0.5 cm, $r^2 = 0.25$, NOT 0.5. Many students write $r$ instead of $r^2$.
> 2. **Mixing up units** in dose calculations: 1 Gy = 100 rad, 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq, 1 R ≈ 8.76 mGy. Carry units through every step.
> 3. **Neglecting source decay** for long LDR implants or **over-correcting** for HDR short treatments where $T \ll T_{1/2}$.
> 4. **Confusing Point A and Point B** definitions in the Manchester system. Point A is 2 cm superior to the fornix, 2 cm lateral. Point B is 3 cm lateral to Point A.
> 5. **Confusing the Paris 85% rule** with the 100% prescription — the prescription is 85% of basal dose, not the basal dose itself.
> 6. **Forgetting the W/e conversion** when going from exposure to dose. Marks specifically allocated for "convert R to rad" steps.
> 7. **Not drawing the block diagram with proper labels** — KTU expects at least 5–6 labeled blocks for the equipment question.
> 8. **Writing "radium" in modern contexts** — radium has been replaced by Cs-137, Ir-192, Co-60 since the 1980s. Examiners deduct for not mentioning modern sources.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Brachytherapy: Techniques and Equipment**

- **Definition:** Brachy = short distance; source placed within/very near tumour (0.5–5 cm).
- **Dose-rate classes:** LDR (0.4–2 Gy/h), MDR (2–12 Gy/h), HDR (>12 Gy/h), PDR (pulsed).
- **Techniques:** Surface/Contact, Intracavitary, Interstitial, Intraluminal, Intravascular.
- **Common sources:**
  - **Ir-192:** $T_{1/2} = 73.83$ d; 0.38 MeV avg γ; used in HDR afterloaders.
  - **Co-60:** $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ y; 1.17 & 1.33 MeV γ; HDR.
  - **Cs-137:** $T_{1/2} = 30.17$ y; 0.662 MeV γ; LDR gyn.
  - **I-125:** $T_{1/2} = 59.4$ d; ~28 keV X-rays; permanent prostate seeds.
  - **Pd-103:** $T_{1/2} = 17$ d; ~21 keV X-rays; permanent prostate seeds.
  - **Sr-90/Y-90:** $\beta^{-}$ emitter; ophthalmic & IVBT.
- **Afterloading evolution:** Hot manual → manual → remote (microSelectron, GammaMed, Flexitron, Bravos, SagiNova).
- **Manchester system:** Point A (2 cm sup + 2 cm lat from fornix); Point B (3 cm lat to Pt A); mg-hr rule; differential loading rules.
- **Paris system:** Uniform linear activity; 85% of basal dose is prescription; parallel, equidistant sources.
- **Air Kerma Strength:** $S_K = \dot{K}_{air} \cdot d^2$; unit 1 U = 1 μGy·m²/h = 1 cGy·cm²/h.
- **TG-43 formalism:** $\dot{D}(r,\theta) = S_K \cdot \Lambda \cdot \dfrac{G(r,\theta)}{G(1,\theta_0)} \cdot g(r) \cdot F(r,\theta)$.
- **Inverse square law:** $\dot{D} \propto 1/r^2$ — the cornerstone of brachytherapy dose fall-off.
- **Total dose (with decay):** $D = \dfrac{\dot{D}_0 \cdot T_{1/2}}{\ln 2}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda T}\right)$.
- **Treatment time:** $T = \dfrac{D}{\dot{D}_0}$ (no decay) or exact form for long LDR.
- **Conversion essentials:** 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq; 1 R ≈ 0.876 rad; 1 Gy = 100 rad.
- **Safety features of remote afterloaders:** Interlocks, backup battery, emergency retraction, source tracking, dual encoders, shielded safe.
- **Clinical sites:** Cervix (intracavitary), Prostate (interstitial seeds), Breast (interstitial HDR), Eye (Sr-90/Ru-106 plaques), Coronary (Sr-90 IVBT).
- **ICRU reports:** 38 (1984), 58 (1997), 89 (2013, updated dose-prescription recommendations).
- **AAPM task groups:** TG-43 (dosimetry formalism), TG-229 (model-based dose calculation algorithms), TG-32 (source calibration).

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Brachytherapy: Techniques and Equipment**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Protocols and Radiation Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT502 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Protocols and Radiation Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Safety Protocols and Radiation Protection** in therapeutic equipment engineering refers to the systematic set of engineering controls, administrative procedures, and regulatory frameworks designed to protect patients, clinical staff, and the general public from the harmful biological effects of ionizing and non-ionizing radiation emitted by therapeutic devices (linear accelerators, cobalt-60 teletherapy units, brachytherapy afterloaders, hyperthermia systems, and laser therapy units). It encompasses electrical safety, mechanical safety, radiation shielding design, dosimetric verification, and compliance with statutory bodies such as the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)**, the **International Commission on Radiological Protection (ICRP)**, and the **National Council on Radiation Protection and Measurements (NCRP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 3 explicitly mandates that students must articulate the **three cardinal principles of radiation protection (Time, Distance, and Shielding)**, the **ALARA philosophy (As Low As Reasonably Achievable)**, and the legislative framework governing therapeutic radiation installations in India.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing near a **bonfire** on a cold winter night.

- The **closer** you stand, the more heat (radiation) you absorb — this is the **Distance** principle.
- The **shorter** the time you linger near the fire, the less burn you get — this is the **Time** principle.
- If you hold a **thick metallic shield (a metal plate)** between yourself and the fire, the heat reaching you is drastically reduced — this is the **Shielding** principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Radiation is invisible**, unlike fire. Therefore, we cannot rely on our senses. We depend on **instruments (dosimeters, Geiger counters)** and **engineered barriers (lead walls, bunkers, interlocks)** to enforce the same logic that protects us from a bonfire.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$
- Quality factor for X-rays, gamma rays, electrons: $Q = 1$
- Quality factor for alpha particles: $Q = 20$
- Standard Tissue Weighting Factor (whole body averaged): $W_T \approx 1$ for gonads; $0.12$ for breast, bone marrow, lung, stomach, colon
